topics.title: Overview, Definition, and Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview, Definition, and Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyber Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system that integrates sensing, computation, control, and networking into physical processes via a tightly coupled feedback loop between the *cyber* (computation, communication, control software) and *physical* (natural, engineered, human) worlds.

In strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a CPS is defined as a system where **computation, communication, and control capabilities are deeply embedded in and tightly integrated with the physical components and processes being controlled**. The acronym CPS is sometimes expanded as:

$$
\text{CPS} \;\equiv\; \text{Cyber}\;\;(\text{Computation} + \text{Communication})\;\;+\;\;\text{Physical}\;\;(\text{Sensors},\;\text{Actuators},\;\text{Process})
$$

The "cyber" component refers to algorithms, microprocessors, software, and communication networks, while the "physical" component refers to the real-world processes, mechanical systems, and biological entities. The defining characteristic is **bidirectional, real-time interaction** — computation monitors physical phenomena and physical actuators are driven by computational decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A CPS is NOT merely an embedded system. The distinction lies in the **closed-loop, real-time, networked interaction** between the cyber and physical domains. Embedded systems can be standalone; CPS by design communicates, coordinates, and collaborates with other cyber and physical entities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **smart air-conditioner in your home that talks to your electricity meter, weather forecast service, and your phone**, all at the same time.

- The **physical part** is the compressor, fan, and the room air (you can touch these).
- The **cyber part** is the small computer chip running a program that decides how cool the room should be.
- The **magic of CPS** is that this tiny computer is *constantly talking* to the cloud weather service (to anticipate a heatwave), to the electricity meter (to run when power is cheap), and to your phone (to let you override the temperature).

A simple traditional AC would *not* be a CPS because it operates in isolation. The moment it begins **sensing → computing → communicating → actuating** in a tight feedback loop with the outside world, it becomes a Cyber Physical System.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Think of CPS as a system that has a *body* (sensors, motors, physical process) and a *brain* (microcontroller, software, network) working together continuously, with the brain receiving signals from the body and the body being guided by the brain's decisions.

### 1.3 Position in the Engineering Hierarchy

CPS sits at the convergence of three traditional domains:

| Traditional Domain | Contribution to CPS |
|---|---|
| **Control Engineering** | Feedback loops, stability analysis, actuators |
| **Computer Science** | Algorithms, real-time scheduling, networking |
| **Mechanical / Electrical Engineering** | Physical processes, sensors, hardware |

> [!TIP]
> For a KTU exam, a strong one-line answer to "What is CPS?" should mention all three keywords: *integration*, *real-time feedback*, and *cyber-physical interaction*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Pillars of CPS

A CPS, regardless of application, rests on **three inseparable pillars**:

1. **Sensing (Physical → Cyber):** Sensors convert physical quantities (temperature, pressure, velocity, light) into digital signals the computer can read.
2. **Computation & Communication (Cyber ↔ Cyber):** The embedded processor runs algorithms, makes decisions, and exchanges data over a network (Wi-Fi, CAN, 5G, Ethernet).
3. **Actuation (Cyber → Physical):** Based on the computed decision, actuators (motors, valves, heaters) physically alter the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> The cycle **Sense → Compute → Actuate → Sense → ...** is the heartbeat of every CPS. Without all three, you do not have a true CPS.

### 2.2 Logical Operation of a CPS — Step by Step

The operational flow of a CPS can be decomposed into the following structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Physical Event Detection:** A physical phenomenon (e.g., a vehicle approaching an intersection) occurs.
- **Step 2 — Sensor Sampling:** A sensor (camera, LiDAR, radar) transduces the physical event into an electrical signal.
- **Step 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** The signal is sampled at frequency $f_s$ and quantized into bits.
- **Step 4 — Pre-processing:** Noise is filtered, data is normalized, and features are extracted.
- **Step 5 — Cyber Decision:** An algorithm (control law, neural network, rule engine) computes the required response.
- **Step 6 — Network Communication:** The decision (or raw data) is shared with other CPS nodes or a cloud server.
- **Step 7 — Control Signal Generation:** A command value is computed and sent to the actuator.
- **Step 8 — Actuation:** The actuator physically changes the environment (e.g., applies brakes).
- **Step 9 — Feedback:** The new physical state is sensed again, closing the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each step must complete within a **bounded time interval (deadline)**. Failing a deadline in a safety-critical CPS (e.g., airbag deployment, autonomous braking) can be catastrophic. This is the foundation of *real-time systems* theory within CPS.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency |
| **ADC Quantization** | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | Volts | $n$ = ADC bit-width |
| **Feedback Loop Latency** | $T_{loop} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{act}$ | seconds | Must be $<$ system deadline |
| **Closed-Loop Transfer Function** | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | dimensionless | Classical control formulation |
| **State-Space Update** | $x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k]$ | — | Discrete-time CPS dynamics |
| **Actuator Output** | $y[k] = C x[k] + D u[k]$ | — | $C, D$ are output matrices |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Higher SNR $\Rightarrow$ better sensing |
| **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$ | bits | Critical for networked CPS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** In KTU answer scripts, students frequently write the *sampling theorem* as $f_s = 2f_{max}$. The correct inequality is $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$. The equality holds only in the *ideal* non-realizable case. Examiners award the mark for the **inequality** form.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CPS engineering principles are now applied in nearly every modern industry:

- **Automotive:** Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), autonomous vehicles, electronic stability control.
- **Healthcare:** Robotic surgery (Da Vinci), insulin pumps, pacemakers, ICU ventilators.
- **Energy:** Smart grids, wind turbine pitch control, microgrid energy management.
- **Manufacturing:** Industry 4.0, robotic assembly lines, digital twins.
- **Aerospace:** Fly-by-wire systems, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), space exploration rovers.
- **Smart Cities:** Adaptive traffic lights, structural health monitoring, water distribution networks.

> [!TIP]
> For exam answers, choose **one example** and describe it end-to-end (sensors, computation, actuators, feedback loop). Examiners reward *depth over breadth*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the Minimum Sampling Frequency Requirement

The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** is foundational to every CPS that observes a continuous physical process through a digital sensor. We will derive the minimum sampling rate $f_s$ needed to reconstruct a band-limited signal $x(t)$ from its samples $x[n] = x(nT)$.

**Statement:** A band-limited signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its discrete samples *if and only if* the sampling frequency $f_s$ is at least twice the highest frequency.

**Derivation — Step 1: Define the sampled signal.**

Let $x(t)$ be a continuous-time signal with Fourier transform $X(f)$ that is non-zero only for $\vert f \vert \leq f_{max}$. The impulse-train sampling produces:

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

where $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period.

**Derivation — Step 2: Take the Fourier Transform.**

Using the modulation property of the impulse train, the spectrum of the sampled signal is a periodic replication of the original spectrum:

$$
X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(f - k f_s\right)
$$

**Derivation — Step 3: Check for spectral aliasing.**

For perfect reconstruction using a low-pass filter, the shifted copies of $X(f)$ must not overlap. This requires:

$$
f_s - f_{max} > f_{max}
$$

**Derivation — Step 4: Solve the inequality.**

$$
f_s - f_{max} > f_{max}
$$
$$
f_s > 2 f_{max}
$$
$$
\boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_{max}}
$$

This is the **Nyquist criterion**, and $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** If you sample a 1 kHz audio signal, you must take at least 2,000 samples per second. Audio CDs use 44.1 kHz to comfortably exceed the ~20 kHz human hearing range.

### 3.2 Deriving the Quantization Step Size

In any CPS, the analog sensor voltage is converted to a digital number by an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. For an $n$-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{ref}$, the size of one quantization level is derived as follows.

**Derivation — Step 1: Total number of representable levels.**

An $n$-bit ADC produces:

$$
N = 2^{n} \text{ discrete levels}
$$

**Derivation — Step 2: Divide the voltage range by the number of levels.**

$$
\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}
$$

For example, a **12-bit** ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ has:

$$
\Delta = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096} \approx 0.806\,\text{mV per level}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In exam problems, always show units. A 12-bit ADC with 3.3 V reference → 0.806 mV resolution. Examiners love to see units clearly written.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CPS vs. Embedded System vs. IoT

This comparative matrix is a **high-value KTU exam asset** because comparison questions (4–5 marks) appear very frequently.

| Attribute | Embedded System | IoT (Internet of Things) | Cyber Physical System (CPS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Dedicated computation within a device | Connectivity & data exchange | Closed-loop control of physical processes |
| **Network Dependency** | Usually optional | Mandatory (Internet) | Often mandatory (may use local networks) |
| **Real-Time Feedback** | Often soft real-time | Not guaranteed | Hard real-time required |
| **Core Loop** | Sense → Compute → Output | Sense → Send → Receive | Sense → Compute → Communicate → Actuate → Sense |
| **Safety Criticality** | Variable | Low to medium | Often high (medical, automotive, aerospace) |
| **Interaction with Physical World** | Limited / one-way | Minimal (mostly sensing) | Bidirectional, continuous, deep |
| **Example** | Washing machine controller | Smart bulb, fitness band | Autonomous car, robotic surgery |
| **Data Use** | Local decisions | Cloud analytics | Local + networked control decisions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 3-mark question asks *“Distinguish between CPS and IoT.”* Use the table above as the basis. The single most important distinction is that **CPS is closed-loop and safety-critical**, while **IoT is mostly open-loop and data-oriented**.

### 3.4 End-to-End CPS Modeling Equation

A complete CPS can be expressed using the **discrete-time state-space model** (which examiners often ask students to write for 3–5 marks):

$$
x[k+1] = A \, x[k] + B \, u[k]
$$
$$
y[k] = C \, x[k] + D \, u[k]
$$

Where:
- $x[k] \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **state vector** (e.g., position, velocity, temperature).
- $u[k] \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **control input** (e.g., voltage to a motor).
- $y[k] \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the **measured output** (e.g., sensor reading).
- $A, B, C, D$ are matrices of appropriate dimensions describing the system dynamics.

The feedback control law is typically:

$$
u[k] = -K \, x[k] + r[k]
$$

where $K$ is the controller gain matrix and $r[k]$ is the reference setpoint. Substituting into the state equation:

$$
x[k+1] = (A - B K) x[k] + B \, r[k]
$$

The closed-loop dynamics are governed by the **matrix $A - BK$**, and stability is ensured when **all eigenvalues of $A - BK$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** of the complex plane.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the same mathematical model used in control theory; what makes it *Cyber Physical* is that $A, B, C, D$ are *implemented on a digital processor with bounded timing guarantees* and *communicated over a network with potential delays and packet loss*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level CPS Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the canonical feedback architecture of any Cyber Physical System. Every real-world CPS — from a pacemaker to a Mars rover — fits this template.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Physical Process] -->|Physical Quantity| S[Sensor Array]
    S -->|Analog Signal| ADC[ADC and Pre-processing]
    ADC -->|Digital Data| CU[Cyber Computation Unit]
    CU -->|Control Command| NET[Communication Network]
    NET -->|Shared Data| CS[Cloud Server and Other CPS Nodes]
    CS -->|Coordinated Commands| NET
    NET -->|Actuation Signal| DAC[DAC and Driver]
    DAC -->|Power Signal| A[Actuator]
    A -->|Physical Action| P
    CU -->|Decision Logic| CU
    style P fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style S fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style ADC fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1
    style CU fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d
    style NET fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6
    style CS fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style DAC fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1
    style A fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows clockwise starting from the **Physical Process**. The Physical Process is sensed → digitized → computed → communicated → actuated → which then changes the Physical Process, and the loop restarts. This is the defining closed loop of a CPS.

### 4.2 The 5C Architecture for CPS (Industry Standard)

The **5C (Connection, Conversion, Cyber, Cognition, Configuration) Architecture** is a widely taught CPS design framework in KTU-aligned textbooks. Each "C" represents a layer of abstraction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1 - Connection] --> L2[Level 2 - Conversion]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - Cyber]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 - Cognition]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 - Configuration]
    L1:::lv1
    L2:::lv2
    L3:::lv3
    L4:::lv4
    L5:::lv5
    classDef lv1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    classDef lv2 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    classDef lv3 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    classDef lv4 fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d
    classDef lv5 fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6
```

| Level | Function | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Connection** | Acquire raw data from sensors | Hardware, sensors, data acquisition |
| **2. Conversion** | Convert raw signals to meaningful data | Signal processing, feature extraction |
| **3. Cyber** | Network the data, store in cloud | Database, web services, security |
| **4. Cognition** | Analyze, learn, predict | Machine learning, analytics, anomaly detection |
| **5. Configuration** | Feed insights back to physical layer | Control logic, optimization, actuation |

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Smart Grid as a CPS

A **Smart Grid** is a flagship CPS example. Because the physical infrastructure of a power grid is too complex to draw, we use a *block-level functional architecture* that shows how cyber and physical layers interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PhysicalLayer["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        GEN[Power Generation Plants]
        TRANS[Transmission Lines]
        DIST[Distribution Network]
        LOAD[Consumer Loads and Smart Meters]
    end
    subgraph CyberLayer["CYBER LAYER"]
        PMU[Phasor Measurement Units and Sensors]
        SCADA[SCADA Control Center]
        EMS[Energy Management System]
        CLOUD[Cloud Analytics Platform]
        OPT[Optimization Engine]
    end
    GEN --> TRANS --> DIST --> LOAD
    LOAD -->|Real Time Demand Data| PMU
    PMU --> SCADA
    SCADA --> EMS
    EMS --> CLOUD
    CLOUD --> OPT
    OPT -->|Generation Schedule and Pricing Signal| GEN
    OPT -->|Load Balancing Command| DIST
    style GEN fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style LOAD fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style SCADA fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style CLOUD fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style OPT fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Use:** When asked "Explain a Smart Grid as a CPS," present the diagram above and explain the bidirectional arrows. The two-way flow between physical generation/demand and cyber optimization is what makes it a *true* CPS, not merely a monitored grid.

### 4.4 Examples Mapping Diagram

The following Mermaid mind map shows how CPS principles map to common real-world applications.

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Cyber Physical Systems Examples))
    Transportation
      Autonomous Vehicles
      Adaptive Cruise Control
      Railway Signaling
      UAV Drones
    Healthcare
      Pacemaker
      Robotic Surgery
      Smart Insulin Pump
      Patient Monitoring
    Energy
      Smart Grid
      Microgrid Controller
      Wind Turbine Pitch
    Industry
      Robotic Assembly
      Digital Twin
      Predictive Maintenance
    Smart Cities
      Adaptive Traffic Lights
      Water Network Monitoring
      Structural Health Sensors
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
> **Define Cyber Physical System. List any four distinguishing features of a CPS.**

**Model Answer:**

A Cyber Physical System (CPS) is an engineered system in which computation, communication, and control are tightly integrated with physical processes via a real-time feedback loop.

**Distinguishing features (any four):**

1. **Tight cyber-physical coupling:** Computation directly controls physical processes in real time.
2. **Networked operation:** Multiple CPS nodes coordinate via communication networks (CAN, Wi-Fi, 5G).
3. **Real-time guarantees:** Tasks must complete within strict deadlines (hard real-time).
4. **Safety-criticality:** Failures can lead to loss of life or property (e.g., medical devices, autonomous cars).
5. **Heterogeneity:** Combines hardware, software, sensors, actuators, and physical processes in a single system.
6. **Feedback-driven:** Operates as a closed loop — sense, compute, actuate, repeat.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`
> **Differentiate between a traditional embedded system and a Cyber Physical System with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form for Full Marks):**

| Parameter | Embedded System | Cyber Physical System |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single device, standalone | Network of cooperating nodes |
| **Connectivity** | Optional, often offline | Mandatory, networked |
| **Real-time** | Soft real-time | Hard real-time |
| **Feedback** | Open loop or simple | Continuous closed loop |
| **Example** | Microwave oven controller | Autonomous vehicle platoon |
| **Safety criticality** | Generally low | Often high |

**Example explanation:** A microwave oven runs a pre-programmed timer (open loop) — it is an embedded system. An autonomous vehicle continuously senses the road, computes steering, and adjusts actuators, while communicating with other vehicles and the cloud — it is a CPS.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. (A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain the fundamental architecture of a Cyber Physical System with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block.
> **(b)** Illustrate any **two** real-world applications of CPS in detail, clearly identifying the sensors, computation, communication, and actuators involved.

## Model Solution for Q3 (A)

### Part (a) — CPS Architecture (7 Marks)

A CPS is organized as a **closed-loop architecture** with three core functional blocks: *Sensing*, *Cyber*, and *Actuation*, bridged by communication and feedback.

**Block-by-block description (write in exam):**

- **Physical Process:** The real-world entity being controlled — e.g., a motor, a vehicle, a chemical reactor, the human heart.
- **Sensors:** Transduce physical quantities (temperature, pressure, position) into analog electrical signals. Example: thermocouple, gyroscope, LiDAR.
- **ADC and Pre-processing:** Converts analog signals to digital form and removes noise using filters.
- **Cyber Computation Unit (CCU):** A microcontroller or processor running real-time algorithms (control law, decision logic, AI). Executes the state-space update $x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k]$.
- **Communication Network:** Shares data with other CPS nodes and cloud servers. Can be wired (CAN, Ethernet) or wireless (Wi-Fi, 5G, ZigBee).
- **DAC and Actuator Driver:** Converts digital decisions back into analog signals strong enough to drive motors/valves.
- **Actuators:** Physically alter the environment — motors, heaters, pumps, brakes.

**(Award 7 marks for: definition 1m + diagram 2m + description of each block 1m each, well-labeled.)**

### Part (b) — Two Applications (7 Marks)

**Application 1: Autonomous Vehicle (3.5 marks)**

| CPS Element | Implementation in Autonomous Car |
|---|---|
| **Sensors** | LiDAR, radar, cameras, GPS, IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) |
| **Computation** | On-board GPU + CPU running deep-learning perception and path planning |
| **Communication** | V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle), V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure), 5G |
| **Actuators** | Steering motor, throttle controller, brake-by-wire |
| **Feedback** | Continuously updated map of surroundings, latency < 100 ms for safety |

**Application 2: Smart Insulin Pump (3.5 marks)**

| CPS Element | Implementation in Smart Insulin Pump |
|---|---|
| **Sensors** | Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) under the skin |
| **Computation** | Embedded microcontroller computing insulin dose = f(glucose, trend, meal) |
| **Communication** | Bluetooth to patient's smartphone, cloud upload to doctor |
| **Actuators** | Micro-pump delivering insulin subcutaneously |
| **Feedback** | Glucose level re-sensed every 5 minutes; loop is closed continuously |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — How Students Lose Marks Here:**
> 1. Writing the *definition* but forgetting to **draw the block diagram** (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. Listing application examples without identifying **all four CPS elements** (sensors, computation, communication, actuators). Examiners specifically check for this — partial credit only.
> 3. Using IoT examples (like a smart bulb) when the question asks for a **CPS** — smart bulb is open-loop, not a true CPS.

---

> **Q3. (B) — Internal Choice Alternative** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **5C Architecture** of a Cyber Physical System. Explain the role of each "C" layer.
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **CPS, IoT, and Embedded Systems** in a tabular form. Explain why CPS is considered the next evolution beyond IoT.

## Model Solution for Q3 (B)

### Part (a) — 5C Architecture (7 Marks)

The 5C architecture is a five-layer reference model used to build industrial CPS:

1. **Connection (1m):** First level — acquires raw, unprocessed data directly from physical assets and sensors. Establishes the *bridge* between physical and cyber worlds using hardware-level protocols.
2. **Conversion (1m):** Second level — converts raw signals into meaningful, context-aware data. Signal processing, calibration, feature extraction happen here.
3. **Cyber (1.5m):** Third level — aggregates data from multiple sources, stores it in databases, and exposes it through networked services (cloud, web APIs). Cybersecurity measures are implemented at this layer.
4. **Cognition (1.5m):** Fourth level — applies machine learning, statistics, and analytics to generate insights, predictions, and anomaly detection.
5. **Configuration (2m):** Fifth and final level — uses the insights from the cognition layer to *feed back* control decisions to the physical system, closing the loop. This is what makes the architecture a *Cyber Physical* one — the "C" in "5C" CPS.

### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)

Present the comparison table from **Section 3.3** of these notes, with these additional points:

- **Why CPS is beyond IoT (3 marks to write):**
  - IoT primarily connects *things to the internet* to gather and analyze data — the focus is on *data collection*.
  - CPS uses that data to *actively control physical processes* in real time, with safety guarantees.
  - IoT can be open-loop (e.g., a smart bulb you turn off via app); CPS is fundamentally closed-loop (e.g., a car brake that must respond in milliseconds).
  - In short: **IoT is "sense-and-share," while CPS is "sense, decide, act, and verify."**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — How Students Lose Marks Here:**
> 1. Confusing **5C with 3C** (Connection, Computation, Control) — both exist; mention which one you are using.
> 2. Writing the table without giving a *substantive paragraph* on "Why CPS is beyond IoT" — examiners expect 2–3 lines of analytical reasoning, not just the table.
> 3. Skipping the diagram for the 5C architecture — a 1.5 to 2 mark penalty is typical.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** CPS = tight integration of computation, communication, and control with physical processes, via a real-time feedback loop.
- **Three Pillars:** Sensing → Computing → Actuating (each mandatory).
- **Not the same as Embedded System:** CPS is *networked* and *closed-loop*; embedded systems can be standalone.
- **Not the same as IoT:** IoT is *data-oriented*; CPS is *control-oriented* and *safety-critical*.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — equality is ideal, inequality is the real rule.
- **ADC Quantization Step:** $\Delta = V_{ref} / 2^{n}$.
- **State-Space Model:** $x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k]$; control law $u = -Kx + r$.
- **5C Architecture:** Connection → Conversion → Cyber → Cognition → Configuration.
- **Loop Latency:** $T_{loop} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{comm} + T_{act}$ — must be less than the system deadline.
- **Real-Time:** CPS are typically *hard real-time*; missing a deadline can be catastrophic.
- **Famous Examples:** Autonomous vehicles, smart grid, robotic surgery, smart insulin pumps, adaptive traffic control, UAV drones, Industry 4.0 robotics, structural health monitoring.
- **Comparison Strategy:** For "distinguish" questions, use a **tabular format** with at least 5 attributes for full marks.
- **Diagram Strategy:** Always include a *neatly labeled block diagram* for any 7+ mark question; examiners often award 1.5–2 marks purely for the diagram.
- **Definition Strategy:** Use these three keywords together: *integration*, *real-time*, *closed-loop* — they are the examiner's favorite trinity.
- **Application Strategy:** Pick one example, list all four CPS elements (sensor, computation, communication, actuator), explain the feedback loop in 2–3 sentences.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** If a question is worth 7 marks and asks for an application, allocate marks as: *Application name + context* (1m) + *Sensor identification* (1.5m) + *Computation/Algorithm* (1.5m) + *Communication* (1m) + *Actuator + Feedback* (2m). This 1-1.5-1.5-1-2 split is exactly how KTU examiners typically break the marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interdisciplinary Nature and Key Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interdisciplinary Nature and Key Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered, integrated computational and physical system whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled, and integrated by a computation and communication core. The system tightly couples physical processes (the *physical layer*) with cyber entities such as embedded processors, software algorithms, sensor networks, and communication protocols (the *cyber layer*) through continuous feedback loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The course begins by establishing that CPS is **not** the same as a traditional embedded system or a stand-alone IoT device. It is a *system of systems* in which computation, communication, and control are inseparably fused with the physical dynamics of the surrounding world.

The **interdisciplinary nature** of CPS arises because its design, analysis, and deployment demand the simultaneous application of knowledge from multiple engineering and science domains:

| Knowledge Domain | Contribution to CPS |
|---|---|
| Computer Science | Algorithms, operating systems, real-time scheduling, AI/ML |
| Electrical/Electronics | Circuit design, embedded processors, signal processing |
| Mechanical Engineering | Physical dynamics, structural analysis, thermal systems |
| Control Engineering | Feedback control, stability analysis, state-space modeling |
| Communication Engineering | Wireless protocols, network architecture, data integrity |
| Mathematics | Differential equations, probability, linear algebra, optimization |
| Civil/Industrial Engineering | Infrastructure, manufacturing systems, process automation |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy — "The Human Body as a CPS"**

Imagine a human body:
- The **brain and nervous system** = the *cyber layer* (computes, decides, communicates).
- The **muscles, bones, organs, and skin** = the *physical layer* (act and react to the real world).
- The **sensory nerves (eyes, ears, skin)** = *sensors* feeding data back to the brain.
- The **motor neurons** = *actuators* executing the brain's decisions.

Just as your body cannot function if the brain is disconnected from the limbs, a CPS fails if the cyber and physical layers lose their tight, real-time coupling. A modern **smart car** is a perfect engineering example: cameras and LiDAR (sensors) feed the onboard computer (cyber), which then commands the brakes and steering (physical actuators) — all within milliseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Memorize (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - **Sensing** — acquiring physical data via sensors.
> - **Computation** — processing sensor data to make decisions.
> - **Actuation** — translating decisions into physical actions.
> - **Feedback Loop** — the closed cycle connecting sensing → computation → actuation → physical process → sensing.
> - **Real-Time Constraint** — the requirement that computations complete within bounded time limits.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in CPS Engineering

- **Latency:** end-to-end delay, typically in **milliseconds (ms)** for safety-critical CPS.
- **Jitter:** variability in latency, measured in **μs** to **ms**.
- **Sampling Frequency (fₛ):** sensor data acquisition rate in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Reliability (R):** probability of correct operation, often targeted at **≥ 0.99999** ("five nines") for industrial CPS.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** typically **10,000 to 100,000 hours** for industrial-grade hardware.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of CPS Architecture

Every Cyber-Physical System, regardless of its application, is built upon three interacting pillars:

1. **The Physical Layer (Plant / Environment)**
   - Represents the real-world process being controlled.
   - Governed by natural laws expressed as **differential equations**.
   - Examples: a motor's rotor, a chemical reactor's temperature, a vehicle's position.

2. **The Cyber Layer (Computation + Communication)**
   - Contains microcontrollers, processors, operating systems (often Real-Time OS), software models, and data buses.
   - Executes **control algorithms** (PID, MPC, fuzzy logic, neural networks).
   - Communicates via **wired** (CAN, Ethernet, RS-485) or **wireless** (Wi-Fi, BLE, 5G, LoRa) protocols.

3. **The Interaction Layer (Sensors + Actuators + Feedback)**
   - **Sensors** transduce physical quantities → electrical signals: temperature (thermistor), pressure (piezoelectric), position (encoder), light (photodiode).
   - **Actuators** transduce electrical signals → physical action: motors, solenoids, heaters, hydraulic pumps.
   - The **feedback loop** closes the cycle, enabling self-correction and stability.

### 2.2 Interdisciplinary Convergence Map

The "interdisciplinary" nature is best understood by examining how disciplines converge at each stage of the CPS lifecycle:

| Lifecycle Stage | Disciplines Involved | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|
| Modeling & Simulation | Mathematics, Physics, Control Theory | Differential equation models, digital twins |
| Hardware Design | Electronics, Electrical, VLSI | Sensor/actuator interfaces, embedded boards |
| Software Development | Computer Science, Software Engineering | RTOS kernels, control code, communication stacks |
| Networking | Communication Engineering, Cybersecurity | Wireless protocols, secure data exchange |
| Verification & Validation | Formal Methods, Statistics | Test suites, safety proofs, reliability metrics |
| Deployment & Operation | Industrial Engg., Domain-Specific (Automotive/Aerospace/Medical) | Functional CPS in the field |

### 2.3 Why CPS Demands an Interdisciplinary Approach — The "Why" Behind the "How"

- **Dynamic coupling:** The physical process evolves continuously, so the cyber layer must respond at a rate fast enough to maintain stability. This demands **control theory** + **real-time computing**.
- **Heterogeneity:** Sensors, processors, and actuators use different signal types, voltages, and protocols — requiring **electronics** + **communication** expertise.
- **Safety & Reliability:** Failures can cause physical harm (a car crash, a factory explosion, a pacemaker failure). This requires **formal methods** + **safety engineering**.
- **Scalability & Intelligence:** Modern CPS use AI/ML for perception and decision-making, demanding **computer science** + **statistics** + **domain knowledge**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** *"Why is CPS considered interdisciplinary and not merely an embedded system?"*
> **Model Answer:** An embedded system is a *component* that performs dedicated computation within a larger device. A CPS, however, integrates computation, communication, sensing, and actuation *with* the physical world through tight feedback — requiring expertise from control, mechanical, communication, and software engineering simultaneously. An embedded system is a *part* of a CPS; a CPS is a *system of systems*.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $y(t) = f(u(t), t)$ | Physical process input-output relation (general) | $u$: control input, $y$: measured output |
| $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ | Continuous-time state equation | $x$: state vector, $A, B$: system matrices |
| $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$ | Output equation | $C, D$: output matrices |
| $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ | Transfer function of the physical plant | $s$: Laplace variable |
| $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID control law | $K_p, K_i, K_d$: tunable gains |
| $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Tracking error | $r$: reference/setpoint |
| $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem | $f_s$: sampling rate, $f_{max}$: signal bandwidth |
| $T_{deadline} = T_{computation} + T_{comm} + T_{actuation}$ | End-to-end deadline budget | All times in seconds (s) |
| $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential distribution) | $\lambda$: failure rate (1/h) |
| $S = \dfrac{\partial y / \partial x}{\partial u / \partial x}$ | Sensitivity of output to parameter variation | Dimensionless ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU exam answers, **always state the units** of any numerical quantity and **clearly define every variable** before using it in an equation. This earns the "clarity of expression" marks that examiners explicitly reward.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of CPS Concepts

CPS principles are foundational to **autonomous vehicles** (Tesla Autopilot, Waymo), **smart grids** (real-time load balancing), **medical devices** (insulin pumps, robotic surgery), **industrial robotics** (Industry 4.0), **aerospace** (fly-by-wire aircraft), and **smart cities** (traffic control, water distribution). Every modern safety-critical engineered system is now a CPS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closing the Loop — From Open-Loop to CPS Feedback

**Problem Setup:** Consider a simple physical plant — a DC motor whose angular position $\theta(t)$ we wish to control. The motor's dynamics are governed by:

$$J \ddot{\theta}(t) + b \dot{\theta}(t) = \tau(t)$$

where:
- $J$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $b$ = viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $\tau(t)$ = applied torque (N·m)
- $\theta(t)$ = angular position (rad)

**Step 1 — Identify the state variables.** Let $x_1(t) = \theta(t)$ and $x_2(t) = \dot{\theta}(t)$. Then the state-space form becomes:

$$\dot{x}_1(t) = x_2(t)$$

$$\dot{x}_2(t) = -\dfrac{b}{J} x_2(t) + \dfrac{1}{J} \tau(t)$$

**Step 2 — Express in matrix form** (this is the standard form examiners expect):

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1(t) \\ \dot{x}_2(t) \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -\frac{b}{J} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{J} \end{bmatrix} \tau(t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the open-loop transfer function.** Taking the Laplace transform of the original equation with zero initial conditions:

$$J s^2 \Theta(s) + b s \Theta(s) = T(s)$$

$$G(s) = \dfrac{\Theta(s)}{T(s)} = \dfrac{1}{J s^2 + b s} = \dfrac{1}{s(J s + b)}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce a proportional controller (the cyber layer's intervention).** Let the controller be $\tau(t) = K_p (\theta_{ref} - \theta(t))$. In the Laplace domain:

$$T(s) = K_p \left( \Theta_{ref}(s) - \Theta(s) \right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the closed-loop transfer function (the CPS feedback loop):**

$$\Theta(s) = G(s) \cdot K_p \left( \Theta_{ref}(s) - \Theta(s) \right)$$

$$\Theta(s) \left( 1 + K_p G(s) \right) = K_p G(s) \, \Theta_{ref}(s)$$

$$\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{\Theta_{ref}(s)} = \dfrac{K_p G(s)}{1 + K_p G(s)}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $G(s)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{\Theta_{ref}(s)} &= \dfrac{K_p \cdot \dfrac{1}{s(J s + b)}}{1 + K_p \cdot \dfrac{1}{s(J s + b)}} \\
&= \dfrac{K_p}{s(J s + b) + K_p} \\
&= \dfrac{K_p}{J s^2 + b s + K_p}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The denominator is now the **characteristic equation** of the closed-loop CPS. Its roots (eigenvalues) determine stability, response speed, and oscillation — and they are influenced by the *cyber* parameter $K_p$. This single derivation shows how a design choice made entirely in the cyber domain (changing $K_p$) directly reshapes the *physical* behavior of the system.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Interdisciplinary Contributions

| CPS Design Question | Discipline that Answers It | Example Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| How fast must the sensor sample? | Communication + Signal Processing | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) |
| How do we represent physical dynamics? | Mathematics + Control Theory | State-space / differential equations |
| How is control logic implemented? | Computer Science + Software Engg. | RTOS tasks, PID code, schedulers |
| How is data transmitted reliably? | Communication Engineering | CAN, MQTT, 5G, error correction |
| How is the system powered? | Electrical Engineering | Battery, PoE, energy harvesting |
| How do we guarantee safety? | Formal Methods + Safety Engg. | Model checking, fail-safe design |
| How does it interact with humans? | HCI + Cognitive Science | Interface design, trust calibration |
| How is it mass-produced? | Industrial + Manufacturing Engg. | Assembly lines, quality control |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Sampling Rate Justification

**Problem:** A vibration sensor in a CPS monitors a motor with rotational frequency **1500 RPM**. What minimum sampling rate is required to avoid aliasing?

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to Hz:**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1500 \text{ rev/min}}{60 \text{ s/min}} = 25 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist criterion** (with a 20% safety margin as is standard in CPS design):

$$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{s,\text{recommended}} = 1.2 \times 50 = 60 \text{ Hz}$$

**Conclusion:** The sensor must sample at least at **60 Hz**; the chosen design uses 100 Hz to provide an additional margin for filter roll-off and jitter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always show the unit conversion step (RPM → Hz) explicitly. Examiners allocate 1 mark for the conversion and 1 mark for the formula application. Skipping the unit step is a common reason for losing marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level CPS Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Physical Process / Plant"] --> node2["Sensors"]
    node2 --> node3["Signal Conditioning and ADC"]
    node3 --> node4["Communication Network"]
    node4 --> node5["Computation Unit / Controller"]
    node5 --> node6["Decision and Control Algorithm"]
    node6 --> node7["Communication Network Downlink"]
    node7 --> node8["Actuator Drivers"]
    node8 --> node9["Actuators"]
    node9 --> node10["Physical Process / Plant"]
    node5 --> node11["Human Machine Interface HMI"]
    node5 --> node12["Cloud Storage and Analytics"]
    node5 --> node13["Security and Authentication Module"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (node1 → node2 → node3 → node4 → node5 → node6 → node7 → node8 → node9 → node10) is the canonical CPS feedback loop.
- **node11 (HMI)** allows human operators to monitor and override.
- **node12 (Cloud)** provides historical data, machine-learning-based analytics, and remote updates.
- **node13 (Security)** ensures authentication, encryption, and intrusion detection — a cross-cutting concern in modern CPS.

### 4.2 Interdisciplinary Convergence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Cyber Physical System CPS"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["Cyber Layer"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Physical Layer"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Interaction Layer"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Computer Science"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Communication Engineering"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Control Theory"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Mechanical Engineering"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Electrical Engineering"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Chemical and Process Engineering"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Sensor and Instrumentation"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Actuator Design"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Embedded Electronics"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Cross Cutting Concerns"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Safety Engineering"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Cybersecurity"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["Formal Verification"]
    nodeE --> nodeE4["Mathematics and Statistics"]
```

### 4.3 Feedback Loop Schematic (Sequence View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Physical World State X"] -->|Measured by| nodeS["Sensors"]
    nodeS -->|Signal y t| nodeA["ADC and Pre processing"]
    nodeA -->|Digital data| nodeC["Controller Cyber Layer"]
    nodeC -->|Control law u t| nodeD["DAC and Driver"]
    nodeD -->|Power signal| nodeAC["Actuator"]
    nodeAC -->|Physical action| nodeP
    nodeC -.->|Telemetry| nodeH["HMI and Cloud"]
```

This sequence diagram is the **single most important picture** for KTU Module 1 questions. Memorize the chain: **Physical → Sensor → ADC → Controller → DAC → Actuator → Physical**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Cyber-Physical System. List any two distinguishing features that differentiate a CPS from a traditional embedded system.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Cyber-Physical System (CPS) is an engineered system in which physical processes are tightly integrated with computation, communication, and control through continuous feedback loops, enabling the cyber elements to influence and be influenced by the physical world in real time. *(1 mark)*

Distinguishing features:
1. **Tight bidirectional coupling with the physical world** — the cyber layer not only monitors but *actively modifies* the physical process through actuators, whereas a traditional embedded system often operates in isolation. *(1 mark)*
2. **Closed-loop real-time feedback** — CPS relies on continuous feedback from sensors with strict timing deadlines; many embedded systems execute open-loop or best-effort tasks. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the role of sensors and actuators in a CPS with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Sensors are the **input transducers** of a CPS — they convert physical quantities (temperature, pressure, position, light) into electrical signals that the cyber layer can process. *(1 mark)*

Actuators are the **output transducers** — they convert electrical control signals from the cyber layer into physical actions (motion, heat, force, flow). *(1 mark)*

**Example:** In an **anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** of a car, wheel-speed sensors detect rotation, the ECU (cyber layer) computes the optimal brake pressure, and hydraulic actuators modulate the brake force to prevent wheel lock-up — a complete CPS feedback cycle. *(1 mark)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** from a choice of two. The question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each, often (a) theory and (b) application/derivation.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Choose either A or B*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **interdisciplinary nature** of Cyber-Physical Systems. Discuss how control engineering, computer science, and communication engineering each contribute to a CPS.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A temperature control CPS maintains a chemical reactor at 90 °C. The temperature sensor produces 10 mV/°C and the ADC has a 12-bit resolution over a 0–5 V range. Calculate (i) the digital output for the setpoint, and (ii) the minimum detectable temperature change.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram of a CPS:** *(2 marks)*

```
[Physical Process] → [Sensors] → [ADC] → [Controller] → [DAC] → [Actuators] → [Physical Process]
                            ↑                                              ↓
                            └────────────── Feedback Loop ─────────────────┘
```

**Interdisciplinary Contributions:** *(5 marks)*

- **Control Engineering:** Provides the mathematical framework (state-space, transfer functions, stability analysis) to design the controller that ensures the physical process tracks the desired setpoint. For the reactor example, a PID controller $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ would regulate heater power.
- **Computer Science:** Provides the software stack — embedded firmware, Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS), task schedulers, and increasingly AI/ML models — that implements the control algorithm and handles data logging, fault detection, and HMI interaction.
- **Communication Engineering:** Provides the protocols (CAN, Modbus, MQTT, 5G, Wi-Fi) for inter-node data exchange, ensuring deterministic latency, error correction, and security between distributed sensors, controllers, and actuators.
- *Additional credit (any 1):* Electrical engineering for power and signal conditioning; mechanical engineering for plant dynamics; mathematics for modeling and optimization.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Setpoint temperature: $T_{set} = 90 \,^\circ\text{C}$
- Sensor sensitivity: $S = 10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}$
- ADC: 12-bit, range $0$–$5$ V

**Step 1 — Sensor output voltage at setpoint:** *(1 mark)*

$$V_{set} = S \times T_{set} = 10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C} \times 90 \,^\circ\text{C} = 900 \text{ mV} = 0.9 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage resolution of the ADC (size of 1 LSB):** *(1 mark)*

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{5}{4096} \approx 1.221 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Digital output for the setpoint:** *(2 marks)*

$$D_{set} = \dfrac{V_{set}}{\Delta V} = \dfrac{900 \text{ mV}}{1.221 \text{ mV}} \approx 737.1 \approx 737$$

(In binary: $D_{set} = 737_{10} = 001011100001_2$.)

**Step 4 — Minimum detectable temperature change (temperature per LSB):** *(2 marks)*

$$\Delta T_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{S} = \dfrac{1.221 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}} \approx 0.1221 \,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Conclusion:** *(1 mark)* The controller receives a digital count of **737** for the 90 °C setpoint, and the system's smallest measurable temperature change is approximately **0.12 °C**, which is acceptable for chemical-reactor control where ±0.5 °C tolerance is typical.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Describe the **key components of a Cyber-Physical System** in detail. Explain how feedback is established between the cyber and physical layers.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A CPS samples a pressure sensor signal of bandwidth 200 Hz. (i) Determine the minimum sampling rate as per the Nyquist criterion. (ii) If each sample is 16 bits and 100 samples are transmitted per second, calculate the data rate in **kbps**.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Key Components of a CPS:** *(4 marks)*

1. **Sensors:** Convert physical variables to electrical signals (e.g., thermistor → voltage).
2. **Actuators:** Convert electrical control signals to physical actions (e.g., motor, valve).
3. **Computation/Controller Unit:** Microcontroller, microprocessor, or SoC executing the control algorithm; may run an RTOS.
4. **Communication Network:** Wired (CAN, Ethernet) or wireless (Wi-Fi, BLE, 5G) channels connecting distributed CPS nodes.
5. **Physical Plant:** The real-world process (motor, reactor, vehicle, robot arm) governed by physical laws.
6. **Software Stack:** Firmware, control algorithms, communication protocols, security modules.
7. **Power Supply & Management:** Batteries, energy harvesters, regulators.
8. **HMI / Cloud Interface:** Dashboards, displays, remote monitoring.

**Feedback Establishment:** *(3 marks)*
The cyber layer continuously receives sensor data, compares it with a setpoint $r(t)$ to compute error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, processes the error through a control algorithm, and sends commands to the actuators. The actuators modify the physical plant, whose new state is sensed again — closing the loop. This closed-loop feedback is what distinguishes a CPS from a passive monitoring system and enables **autonomy, stability, and self-correction**.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $f_{max} = 200$ Hz, sample size = 16 bits, samples per second = 100.

**(i) Minimum sampling rate:** *(3 marks)*

$$f_{s,\min} = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
- Per Nyquist, the sampling rate must be at least twice the highest frequency in the signal to avoid aliasing.
- $f_{max} = 200$ Hz is the signal bandwidth.
- Therefore $f_{s,\min} = 400$ Hz.

**(ii) Data rate calculation:** *(4 marks)*

$$\text{Data Rate} = \text{samples/sec} \times \text{bits/sample}$$

$$= 100 \times 16 = 1600 \text{ bits/sec} = 1.6 \text{ kbps}$$

**Conclusion:** The CPS requires a minimum sampling rate of **400 Hz** and produces a data stream of **1.6 kbps** — well within the capacity of even low-bandwidth wireless protocols like LoRa, making remote pressure monitoring feasible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (RPM to Hz, mV to V). Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing CPS with IoT.** IoT is primarily about *connectivity and data*; CPS is about *control and feedback*. Saying "a CPS is just an IoT device" will cost full marks.
> 3. **Omitting the feedback loop from diagrams.** KTU diagrams must explicitly show the loop closing from actuators back to the physical process.
> 4. **Skipping variable definitions.** Every variable used in an equation must be defined in the previous line.
> 5. **Writing generalities without examples.** A 7-mark question demands a real-world example (ABS, drone, insulin pump, smart grid) — abstract answers lose at least 2 marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CPS = Cyber + Physical + Interaction**, with a **closed feedback loop** as its defining feature.
- **CPS ≠ Embedded System ≠ IoT** — CPS is a *system of systems*; embedded systems are *components*; IoT emphasizes *data over the internet*.
- The three **key components** are: **(1) Sensors, (2) Computation/Controller, (3) Actuators**, supported by **communication networks**, **software stack**, and **power systems**.
- The **interdisciplinary nature** spans: Computer Science, Electrical/Electronics, Mechanical, Control, Communication, Mathematics, and domain-specific engineering (Automotive, Aerospace, Biomedical, Industrial).
- A CPS is described mathematically by **state-space equations** $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$ and by **transfer functions** $G(s) = Y(s)/U(s)$.
- The **Nyquist criterion** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ governs sensor sampling.
- The **PID control law** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ is the most common control algorithm in CPS.
- **Real-time constraints** require bounded computation, communication, and actuation times.
- **Cross-cutting concerns** in CPS include safety, security, reliability, and formal verification.
- For KTU exam answers: **always draw the feedback block diagram**, **state all units**, **define all variables**, and **give at least one real-world example**.
- Real-world CPS examples to memorize: **autonomous vehicles, ABS, insulin pump, smart grid, fly-by-wire aircraft, industrial robotic arms, smart thermostats**.
- The **deadline budget** of a CPS is $T_{deadline} = T_{computation} + T_{comm} + T_{actuation}$ — violating it means system failure.

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Interdisciplinary Nature and Key Components**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Embedded Systems and Real-Time Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Embedded Systems and Real-Time Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Embedded System — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *purpose-built, resource-constrained computing subsystem* that is an integral part of a larger electromechanical or physical device, dedicated to executing a *specific set of functions* under strict *real-time, power, memory, and cost* constraints.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, the embedded system acts as the *cyber half* — sampling the physical world through sensors, computing decisions, and driving the physical world through actuators. It is **not** a general-purpose computer; its hardware, software, and I/O are co-designed for one product family.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of an Embedded System:**
> 1. **Sensing** (transducers / ADCs) — Observe the physical plant.
> 2. **Computation** (MCU / SoC + Firmware / RTOS) — Make decisions.
> 3. **Actuation** (PWM / DAC / Drivers) — Modify the physical plant.

---

## 1.2 Real-Time System — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time System:** A system whose *logical correctness* depends not only on the **value** of the output, but also on the **time instant** at which the output is produced.

A late answer is a *wrong* answer. The system is judged by its *temporal fidelity* to the physical world it is observing or controlling.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reflexes of a Machine"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Human Reflex vs. Thought:**
> - *General-Purpose Computer* = a philosopher sitting in a chair. If he answers a question in 5 seconds or 5 minutes, the answer is still correct.
> - *Embedded System* = a cricket batsman facing a 140 km/h fast bowler. If his "computation" (the decision to swing the bat) finishes in **500 ms** instead of **50 ms**, the ball has already passed — and the answer is now *factually wrong* (he is bowled out).
> - *Hard Real-Time System* = a batsman who **must** hit the ball (missing = catastrophic, no second chance).
> - *Soft Real-Time System* = a YouTube video buffering; a 200 ms delay is *annoying* but not *catastrophic*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** CPS is the engineering of "machine reflexes." The embedded controller inside a CPS is essentially a *digital nervous system* — its deadlines, jitter, and response times dictate the **stability, safety, and quality** of the entire physical system.

---

## 1.4 Quantitative Distinctions (Bold Highlights)

| Property | General-Purpose Computer | Embedded System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Clock Speed** | 2–5 **GHz** | 16–200 **MHz** |
| **Power Budget** | 65–250 **W** | 0.01–5 **W** |
| **Deadline Hardness** | None (best-effort) | **Hard / Firm / Soft** |
| **RTOS Required** | No | **Often Yes** |
| **User Reprogrammability** | High | **Low to None** (firmware is fixed) |
| **Operating Environment** | Climate-controlled | **Harsh** (vibration, -40°C to +125°C) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Embedded System

A CPS-grade embedded platform is composed of three tightly coupled layers. The interaction between these layers is what creates the "cyber-physical" loop.

### 2.1.1 Hardware Layer
- **Processor Core:** Microcontroller (MCU), Microprocessor (MPU), System-on-Chip (SoC), or Digital Signal Processor (DSP).
- **Memory Hierarchy:** ROM/Flash (program), SRAM (data), EEPROM (parameters), external SDRAM (for advanced SoCs like Raspberry Pi class).
- **Clock & Timers:** Hardware timers, Watchdog Timers (WDT), Real-Time Clocks (RTC).
- **Peripherals:** GPIO, ADC, DAC, PWM, UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, Ethernet.

### 2.1.2 Software Layer
- **Firmware / Application Code:** The control algorithm (e.g., PID, state machine, Kalman filter).
- **RTOS Kernel:** Scheduler, inter-task communication (queues, semaphores), memory management.
- **Device Drivers:** HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) for peripherals.
- **Bootloader:** Initializes hardware and loads firmware.

### 2.1.3 I/O / Interface Layer
- **Sensors:** Temperature (LM35), IMU (MPU6050), Pressure, Vision (Camera), LIDAR.
- **Actuators:** DC motor, Stepper, Servo, Solenoid, Relay, Heater.
- **Communication Bus:** CAN (automotive), LIN, FlexRay, EtherCAT, TSN.

---

## 2.2 Processor Architectures (KTU Board Favorites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Von Neumann vs. Harvard Architecture — *Why it matters for real-time:***
> - **Von Neumann:** Single bus for instructions + data. Simpler, but creates a *bottleneck* (the "Von Neumann bottleneck"). Used in x86 PCs.
> - **Harvard:** Separate instruction and data buses (and often separate memories). Allows **simultaneous fetch + read**, reducing *instruction-cycle jitter* — *critical* for hard real-time DSP. Used in **ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC, DSPs**.

---

## 2.3 Real-Time System Classification

A real-time task τᵢ is characterized by a *triple*:

$$ \tau_i = (C_i, \, T_i, \, D_i) $$

where:
- $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) — **bounded maximum** execution time.
- $T_i$ = Period (for periodic tasks) or Minimum Inter-Arrival Time.
- $D_i$ = Relative Deadline — *time budget* from release to completion.

### 2.3.1 Hard vs. Firm vs. Soft Real-Time

| Class | Deadline Miss Consequence | Example | Tolerance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hard** | Catastrophic system failure | ABS brake, Airbag, Pacemaker | **Zero** tolerance |
| **Firm** | Result is useless, but no chain-reaction | Video frame drop, Radar tracking | **Zero** useful; restartable |
| **Soft** | Quality of Service degrades gracefully | Web page load, Audio playback | **Tolerable** statistical misses |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "real-time = fast." This is **wrong**. Real-time means *predictable*. A 1 GHz system with unbounded jitter is *not* real-time; a 16 MHz deterministic MCU *is*.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for the KTU End Semester Examination. Memorize units and conditions.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le U \le 1$ |
| 2 | **Liu–Layland RMS Bound** | $U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | dimensionless | Sufficient condition |
| 3 | **Limit as $n \to \infty$** | $U_{bound} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | dimensionless | 69.3% ceiling |
| 4 | **Response Time** | $R_i = C_i + I_i$ | time units | Under fixed priority |
| 5 | **Interference from higher-priority tasks** | $I_i = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | time units | $hp(i)$ = higher-priority set |
| 6 | **Response Time Iteration** | $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | time units | Converges when $R^{(k+1)} = R^{(k)}$ |
| 7 | **Schedulability (RTA)** | $R_i \le D_i$ | — | Must hold for all $i$ |
| 8 | **Jitter** | $J_i = R_{i,\max} - R_{i,\min}$ | time units | Lower is better |
| 9 | **Hyperperiod (LCM)** | $H = \mathrm{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ | time units | Repeats every $H$ |
| 10 | **Deadline Monotonic Priority** | Shorter $D_i$ → Higher priority | — | Used when $D_i < T_i$ |

---

## 2.5 Scheduling Algorithms — The Big Two

### 2.5.1 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)
- **Static-priority**, *fixed-priority* algorithm.
- **Rule:** Shorter period $T_i$ → higher priority.
- **Optimality:** Among all *static-priority* algorithms, RMS minimizes the worst-case response time.
- **Test:** Liu–Layland utilization bound is *sufficient* (not necessary).

### 2.5.2 Earliest Deadline First (EDF)
- **Dynamic-priority** algorithm.
- **Rule:** At every scheduling instant, run the task with the *earliest absolute deadline*.
- **Optimality:** EDF can achieve full utilization $U \le 1$ (a task set is schedulable under EDF **iff** $U \le 1$).
- **Drawback:** Higher runtime overhead; harder to implement in low-end MCUs.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where this theory is used in industry:**
> - **AUTOSAR** (Automotive): Uses RMS-style fixed priorities for chassis and powertrain ECUs.
> - **ARINC 653** (Avionics): Hard real-time, partitioned scheduling for flight control.
> - **VxWorks / FreeRTOS / RTEMS:** Commercial and open RTOS kernels implementing these algorithms.
> - **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Hard real-time guarantees required for FDA certification.
> - **Industrial IoT (IEC 61131-3):** PLC scan cycles rely on bounded WCET.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides a fully worked-out mathematical treatment of the two most-tested topics in KTU Module 1: (a) the Liu–Layland utilization bound, and (b) the Response Time Analysis (RTA) iteration. Every algebraic step is shown; *no logical jumps are permitted*.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Liu–Layland Bound for $n=2$ Tasks

The classical proof uses the "critical instant" — the worst-case response time of a task occurs when it is released *simultaneously* with all higher-priority tasks.

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider two periodic tasks under RMS:
- Task 1: $(C_1, T_1)$ — higher priority (shorter period).
- Task 2: $(C_2, T_2)$ — lower priority, with $T_1 < T_2$.

We need to find the *maximum* $C_2$ that still keeps Task 2 schedulable, i.e., $R_2 \le T_2$.

### 3.1.2 Response Time of Task 2
At the critical instant, Task 1 preempts Task 2 *every* $T_1$ units. The number of Task 1 instances during $[0, T_2]$ is:

$$ k_1 = \left\lceil \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right\rceil $$

Therefore, the total CPU demand in the interval $[0, T_2]$ is:

$$ W = C_2 + k_1 C_1 $$

### 3.1.3 Schedulability Condition
For Task 2 to complete by its deadline $D_2 = T_2$:

$$ C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 \le T_2 $$

Divide throughout by $T_2$:

$$ \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \left\lceil \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right\rceil \frac{C_1}{T_2} \le 1 $$

Since $\left\lceil \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right\rceil \le \frac{T_2}{T_1} + 1$, the *worst case* (i.e., the tightest constraint on the ratios) occurs when the ceiling value is as large as possible. Taking the bound:

$$ \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} + 1 \right) \frac{C_1}{T_2} \le 1 $$

Rearranging:

$$ \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} \le \frac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \frac{C_1}{T_2} + \frac{C_1}{T_2} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} \le 1 $$

Let $u_1 = C_1 / T_1$ and $u_2 = C_2 / T_2$. The critical ratio $r = T_1 / T_2$ ranges in $(0, 1)$. Substituting and using calculus to find the minimum of the bound over $r$:

$$ u_1 + u_2 \le r \cdot u_1 + (1 - r) + (1 - r) \cdot \frac{u_1}{r \cdot u_1 / (1-r)} $$

Carrying out the standard calculus minimization (Liu & Layland, 1973) for $n=2$ yields the closed-form bound:

$$ U_{bound}(n=2) = 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.8284 $$

Hence, for $n=2$:

$$ \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} \le 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.828 $$

**Generalization for $n$ tasks (Liu–Layland, 1973):**

$$ U_{bound}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even with an *infinite* number of RMS tasks, the worst-case sustainable utilization is only **69.3%**. The remaining 30.7% is "wasted" by preemption overhead and critical-instant arrivals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Response Time Analysis (RTA) Iteration

Unlike the utilization bound, RTA gives an **exact** test — it does not over-approximate. It is therefore a *necessary and sufficient* condition for fixed-priority preemptive scheduling.

### 3.2.1 Setup
For task $\tau_i$ with priority $i$, define:
- $hp(i) = \{ j : \text{priority}(j) > \text{priority}(i) \}$.
- The *worst-case response time* $R_i$ is the longest time from release to completion.

At the critical instant, $\tau_i$ is released *simultaneously* with all tasks in $hp(i)$. The number of jobs of $\tau_j \in hp(i)$ that arrive in a window of length $R_i$ is:

$$ N_j(R_i) = \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil $$

Each such job consumes $C_j$. The total interference on $\tau_i$ is:

$$ I_i = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} N_j(R_i) \cdot C_j = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$

The response time must satisfy the *fixed-point* equation:

$$ R_i = C_i + I_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$

### 3.2.2 Iterative Solution
Since $R_i$ appears on both sides, we use the iterative scheme:

$$ R_i^{(0)} = C_i $$

$$ R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$

The iteration has **three possible outcomes**:
1. **Convergence:** $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$. Task is schedulable iff $R_i \le D_i$.
2. **Deadline Miss:** $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$. Task is **not schedulable**.
3. **Divergence / No Convergence in reasonable time:** Bound the search at $R_i \le \sum_{j \in hp(i) \cup \{i\}} T_j$ (the *busy period* bound).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — 3-Task RTA

> [!TIP]
> **Exam-Style Problem (for practice):** Three real-time tasks are scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Determine if the system is schedulable using (a) the Liu–Layland bound, and (b) exact Response Time Analysis.

| Task $\tau_i$ | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\tau_1$ | 2 | 10 | 10 |
| $\tau_2$ | 3 | 15 | 15 |
| $\tau_3$ | 4 | 20 | 20 |

### Step 1 — Total CPU Utilization

$$ U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} $$

$$ U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{15} + \frac{4}{20} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.60 $$

### Step 2 — Liu–Layland Bound for $n=3$

$$ U_{bound}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) $$

We know $2^{1/3} \approx 1.2599$:

$$ U_{bound}(3) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798 $$

### Step 3 — Sufficiency Check

$$ U = 0.60 \le U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark $$

The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under RMS by the sufficient condition.

### Step 4 — Exact Response Time for $\tau_1$
No higher-priority tasks exist, so:

$$ R_1 = C_1 = 2 \, \text{ms} \le D_1 = 10 \, \text{ms} \quad \checkmark $$

### Step 5 — Exact Response Time for $\tau_2$

$$ R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 3 $$

$$ R_2^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 0 \cdot 2 = 3 $$

Convergence at $R_2 = 3 \, \text{ms} \le D_2 = 15 \, \text{ms} \quad \checkmark$

### Step 6 — Exact Response Time for $\tau_3$

$$ R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 4 $$

$$ R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4 $$

Convergence at $R_3 = 4 \, \text{ms} \le D_3 = 20 \, \text{ms} \quad \checkmark$

### Step 7 — Final Verdict
All three tasks satisfy their deadlines. The system is **schedulable** with a *headroom* of $0.7798 - 0.60 = 0.1798$ (≈18%) of CPU time still available for aperiodic or interrupt-driven work.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Analysis Method | Type | Complexity | Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Utilization Bound** (Liu–Layland) | Sufficient | $O(n)$ | Trivial computation | Pessimistic; rejects some schedulable sets |
| **Hyperplane Test** (Hyperbolic Bound) | Sufficient | $O(n \log n)$ | Tighter than Liu–Layland | Still only sufficient |
| **Exact RTA** (Joseph–Pandya) | Necessary & Sufficient | Pseudo-polynomial | Exact answer | Slow for large $n$ |
| **Time Demand Analysis** (Lehoczky) | Necessary & Sufficient | $O(n \cdot H)$ | Visualizable on $[0, T_i]$ | Requires graphical inspection |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams render the two most important architectural views in CPS Module 1: (a) the closed-loop CPS architecture showing real-time feedback, and (b) the internal hierarchy of an embedded controller.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Closed-Loop CPS Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    phy["PHYSICAL PLANT"]
    sens["SENSOR SUBSYSTEM"]
    cond["SIGNAL CONDITIONING AND ADC"]
    proc["EMBEDDED PROCESSOR MCU OR SoC"]
    ctrl["CONTROL ALGORITHM FIRMWARE RTOS"]
    dac["DAC OR PWM DRIVER"]
    act["ACTUATOR SUBSYSTEM"]
    bus["COMMUNICATION BUS CAN OR ETHERNET"]
    user["HUMAN OPERATOR OR REMOTE CLOUD"]

    phy -->|MEASURED VARIABLE| sens
    sens -->|ANALOG SIGNAL| cond
    cond -->|DIGITAL SAMPLE| proc
    proc --> ctrl
    ctrl -->|CONTROL OUTPUT| proc
    proc -->|ACTUATION COMMAND| dac
    dac -->|POWER SIGNAL| act
    act -->|PHYSICAL ACTION| phy
    proc <-->|TELEMETRY COMMANDS| bus
    bus <-->|HMI OR CLOUD LINK| user
    ctrl -.->|DEADLINE WCET JITTER| proc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The physical plant's state is sensed, digitized, and fed to the embedded processor. The control algorithm computes a corrective action, which is converted back into a physical signal by the actuator. The dashed loop (CTRL → PROC) represents the *temporal constraint* layer — every arrow carries an implicit **deadline, WCET, and jitter budget**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Internal Hierarchy of an Embedded System

```mermaid
graph TB
    sys["EMBEDDED SYSTEM PLATFORM"]
    hw["HARDWARE LAYER"]
    sw["SOFTWARE LAYER"]
    io["IO INTERFACE LAYER"]

    sys --> hw
    sys --> sw
    sys --> io

    hw --> cpu["CPU CORE ARM CORTEX M OR R"]
    hw --> mem["MEMORY FLASH ROM SRAM"]
    hw --> clk["CLOCK SOURCE TIMERS WDT"]
    hw --> pwr["POWER MANAGEMENT UNIT"]

    sw --> boot["BOOTLOADER"]
    sw --> rtos["RTOS KERNEL SCHEDULER"]
    sw --> app["APPLICATION FIRMWARE"]
    sw --> drv["DEVICE DRIVERS HAL"]
    sw --> mid["MIDDLEWARE COMMUNICATION STACK"]

    io --> inp["INPUT SENSORS GPIO ADC I2C SPI"]
    io --> outp["OUTPUT ACTUATORS PWM DAC GPIO"]
    io --> com["NETWORK UART CAN ETHERNET WIRELESS"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The hardware layer provides deterministic resources; the software layer adds *time-aware* orchestration (RTOS scheduler); the I/O layer translates between *cyber bits* and *physical signals*. In a CPS, all three layers must be **co-designed** for real-time guarantees.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Real-Time Scheduling Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    trig["PERIODIC OR APERIODIC EVENT TRIGGER"]
    rel["TASK RELEASED INTO READY QUEUE"]
    sch["SCHEDULER DISPATCHER"]
    exec["PROCESSOR EXECUTES TASK"]
    comp["TASK COMPLETES WITHIN DEADLINE"]
    miss["DEADLINE MISSED HARD FAILURE"]

    trig --> rel
    rel --> sch
    sch --> exec
    exec -->|SUCCESS| comp
    exec -->|TIMEOUT| miss
    miss -.->|SYSTEM HALT OR SAFE STATE| trig
```

This section contains exam-grade questions modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Each question is tagged with a simulated KTU Past Year Question identifier and mapped to Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (per NEP 2020).
