---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an Embedded System. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a general-purpose computer. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Cognitive Level:** Remember / Understand
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Embedded System** is a computing system that is an integral part of a larger device, dedicated to performing specific functions, with constraints on power, cost, size, and real-time response.
Four distinguishing characteristics:
1. **Single-purpose function** — designed for one specific task (e.g., only washing-machine control).
2. **Tight resource constraints** — limited CPU speed (kHz–MHz), RAM (KB), and power (mW).
3. **Real-time behaviour** — must meet deadlines; *timing* is part of correctness.
4. **Minimal or no user interface** — often headless; communicates via LEDs, buzzers, or a few buttons.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between Hard Real-Time and Soft Real-Time systems. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Cognitive Level:** Understand
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Basis | **Hard Real-Time** | **Soft Real-Time** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Deadline miss | **Catastrophic** — system fails, may endanger life | **Tolerable** — degrades quality |
| Guarantee | Deterministic, provable | Best-effort, statistical |
| Example | Airbag deployment controller, Cardiac pacemaker | Online video streaming, Email delivery |
| Penalty | Engine damage, Loss of human life | User annoyance, Buffering |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU ESE, every 14-mark question offers **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B). Attempt *one* of the two. Both are provided below for revision.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) — Explain the major hardware and software components of an embedded system used in a Cyber-Physical System. Compare Von Neumann and Harvard architectures with respect to real-time performance. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Cognitive Level:** Understand / Analyze

**Model Solution:**

**1. Hardware Components (4 marks):**
- **Processor:** Microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4) or SoC, with integrated CPU, memory, and peripherals.
- **Memory:** Flash/ROM (program storage), SRAM (runtime data), EEPROM (persistent configuration).
- **I/O Peripherals:** GPIO, ADC (analog input), DAC/PWM (analog output), timers, communication modules (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN).
- **Sensors and Actuators:** Sensors (LM35, MPU6050) convert physical quantities to electrical signals; actuators (motors, solenoids) convert electrical signals back to physical action.
- **Clock & Power Management:** Crystal oscillator, PLL, voltage regulators, watchdog timer.

**2. Software Components (3 marks):**
- **Bootloader:** Initializes hardware and loads the firmware.
- **RTOS Kernel:** Provides scheduler, semaphores, queues, and interrupt management. Common RTOS: FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS.
- **Firmware / Application Code:** Implements the control algorithm (PID, state machine, FSM).
- **Device Drivers / HAL:** Hardware Abstraction Layer that hides register-level details from the application.
- **Middleware (optional):** TCP/IP stack, CANopen, OPC-UA for industrial CPS.

**3. Von Neumann vs Harvard (2 marks):**
- **Von Neumann:** Single shared bus for instructions and data. Simpler, cheaper, but causes the *Von Neumann bottleneck* — instruction fetch and data read cannot occur simultaneously, increasing *execution-time jitter*.
- **Harvard:** Separate buses and memories for instructions and data. Enables *simultaneous* fetch + memory access, producing **deterministic, low-jitter** timing — *ideal for hard real-time* DSP and control loops.
- *Conclusion:* Harvard is preferred in real-time CPS controllers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M uses a modified Harvard architecture; DSPs use pure Harvard).

---

#### Q.A(b) — A CPS controller runs three real-time tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. The task parameters are: $\tau_1 = (1, 4), \, \tau_2 = (2, 6), \, \tau_3 = (3, 10)$. Check schedulability using (i) the Liu–Layland utilization bound, and (ii) exact Response Time Analysis. Assume $D_i = T_i$. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyze

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $C_1=1, T_1=4; C_2=2, T_2=6; C_3=3, T_3=10$; all $D_i = T_i$.

**Step 1 — RMS Priority Assignment (1 mark):**
Priority order follows increasing period: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$.

**Step 2 — Total CPU Utilization (1 mark):**

$$ U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883 $$

**Step 3 — Liu–Layland Bound for $n=3$ (1 mark):**

$$ U_{bound}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798 $$

**Comparison:** $U = 0.883 > U_{bound} = 0.7798$. The Liu–Layland test **fails** — *but the task set may still be schedulable*. (Sufficient, not necessary.)

**Step 4 — Exact Response Time Analysis (4 marks):**

For $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no interference):

$$ R_1 = C_1 = 1 \le D_1 = 4 \quad \checkmark $$

For $\tau_2$ (interference from $\tau_1$ only):

$$ R_2^{(0)} = 2 $$

$$ R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 $$

$$ R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \text{(converged)} $$

$R_2 = 3 \le D_2 = 6 \quad \checkmark$

For $\tau_3$ (interference from $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$):

$$ R_3^{(0)} = 3 $$

$$ R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 $$

$$ R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 $$

$$ R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9 $$

$$ R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 $$

$$ R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \quad \text{(converged)} $$

$R_3 = 10 \le D_3 = 10 \quad \checkmark$ *(just meets the deadline)*

**Step 5 — Final Verdict (1 mark):**
The task set **is schedulable** under RMS, even though the Liu–Layland sufficient condition failed. This demonstrates the *pessimism* of the utilization bound and the *exactness* of RTA.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### Q.B(a) — Define a Real-Time System. With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the RTOS scheduler in a CPS. Compare Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) on at least four parameters. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Cognitive Level:** Understand / Analyze

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A **Real-Time System** is a system whose correctness depends not only on the *logical* result of computation, but also on the *time instant* at which that result is produced. A late result may be a *wrong* result.

**2. RTOS Scheduler Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Event Source] --> [Interrupt] --> [RTOS Tick]
                                         |
                                         v
                                   [Ready Queue] <----+
                                         |             |
                                         v             |
                                    [Scheduler] -------+
                                     (RMS or EDF)
                                         |
                                         v
                                    [CPU Execution]
                                         |
                                         v
                                   [Task Completion]
                                    (before deadline)
```

The scheduler is invoked on every RTOS tick or interrupt. It selects the *next-to-run* task from the ready queue using either **RMS** (static priority by period) or **EDF** (dynamic priority by earliest deadline). The dispatcher then performs a context switch onto the CPU.

**3. RMS vs EDF Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | **RMS** | **EDF** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Priority type | Static (fixed) | Dynamic (changes at run-time) |
| Optimality | Optimal among *static-priority* schemes | Optimal among *all* schemes (achieves $U \le 1$) |
| Runtime overhead | Low (priority lookup is $O(1)$) | Higher (must search deadlines) |
| Schedulability bound | $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1) \le 0.693$ | $U \le 1$ exactly |
| Implementation on MCU | Easy (used in FreeRTOS, VxWorks) | Complex (rare in low-end MCUs) |
| Use case | Automotive ECUs, Avionics (AUTOSAR) | Multimedia, Industrial soft-RT |

**Conclusion:** RMS is preferred for **hard real-time, low-overhead** MCUs; EDF is preferred when **maximum CPU utilization** is needed and the platform can afford higher scheduling overhead.

---

#### Q.B(b) — A Cyber-Physical drone controller runs four periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. The task set is:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\tau_1$ (IMU read) | 1 | 5 | 5 |
| $\tau_2$ (PID control) | 2 | 10 | 10 |
| $\tau_3$ (Telemetry) | 3 | 25 | 25 |
| $\tau_4$ (Camera) | 4 | 50 | 50 |

Determine:
(i) the total CPU utilization,
(ii) the Liu–Layland bound for $n=4$,
(iii) the exact worst-case response time of $\tau_4$ using RTA iteration. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyze

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — RMS Priority Order (0.5 mark):**
By increasing period: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3 > \tau_4$ (highest to lowest priority).

**Step 2 — Total CPU Utilization (1.5 marks):**

$$ U = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{25} + \frac{4}{50} $$

$$ U = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.120 + 0.080 = 0.600 $$

**Step 3 — Liu–Layland Bound for $n=4$ (1.5 marks):**

$$ U_{bound}(4) = 4 \left( 2^{1/4} - 1 \right) $$

We know $2^{1/4} = \sqrt{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{1.4142} \approx 1.1892$:

$$ U_{bound}(4) = 4 \times (1.1892 - 1) = 4 \times 0.1892 = 0.7569 $$

**Comparison:** $U = 0.600 \le 0.7569$ ✓. The system **passes the sufficient test**.

**Step 4 — Exact RTA for $\tau_4$ (3.5 marks):**
Higher-priority tasks: $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$. Initialize:

$$ R_4^{(0)} = C_4 = 4 $$

**Iteration 1:**

$$ R_4^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{25} \right\rceil \cdot 3 $$

$$ R_4^{(1)} = 4 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) + (1)(3) = 4 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 10 $$

**Iteration 2:**

$$ R_4^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{25} \right\rceil \cdot 3 $$

$$ R_4^{(2)} = 4 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) + (1)(3) = 4 + 2 + 2 + 3 = 11 $$

**Iteration 3:**

$$ R_4^{(3)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{25} \right\rceil \cdot 3 $$

$$ R_4^{(3)} = 4 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) + (1)(3) = 4 + 3 + 4 + 3 = 14 $$

**Iteration 4:**

$$ R_4^{(4)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{25} \right\rceil \cdot 3 $$

$$ R_4^{(4)} = 4 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) + (1)(3) = 4 + 3 + 4 + 3 = 14 \quad \text{(converged)} $$

**Step 5 — Schedulability Verdict (0.5 mark):**

$$ R_4 = 14 \, \text{ms} \le D_4 = 50 \, \text{ms} \quad \checkmark $$

The lowest-priority task (camera) completes in 14 ms — well within its 50 ms budget. The drone has a **healthy 36 ms scheduling headroom** for aperiodic interrupts and OS background tasks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks — KTU Examiner's Pet Peeves:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Fast" with "Real-Time":** Writing "real-time means the system responds quickly" will fetch **zero marks** in the definition. The correct answer is "the system responds *within a deterministic deadline*." Speed without determinism is *not* real-time.
>
> 2. **Skipping Units:** Always write the unit after every numerical answer (e.g., "R₃ = 10 **ms**", not just "R₃ = 10"). KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
>
> 3. **Confusing Liu–Layland with "Necessary and Sufficient":** The bound $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is **sufficient**, **not necessary**. A task set with $U > 0.693$ may still be schedulable (as in Q.A(b) above). Saying it is "necessary" will lose 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Verify $R_i \le D_i$:** Computing $R_i$ is *not enough*. You **must** explicitly state the comparison "$R_i \le D_i$" and mark it with a ✓.
>
> 5. **Wrong RTA Iteration Initial Value:** RTA must start with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$, not with $0$ or $T_i$. Starting wrongly is a **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 6. **Mixing Priority Order in RMS:** Priority is by **period**, not by execution time. A task with $C_i = 10$ ms and $T_i = 5$ ms has *higher* priority than one with $C_i = 1$ ms and $T_i = 100$ ms.
>
> 7. **Omitting the Block Diagram in 7-mark Theory Questions:** A "neat block diagram" explicitly asked for carries **1–2 marks** of the 7. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basics of Embedded Systems and Real-Time Constraints**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Embedded system = dedicated, resource-constrained computer inside a physical device.
- ☐ CPS = closed-loop integration of cyber (compute) and physical (sensors + actuators) processes.
- ☐ Real-time correctness = *temporal* correctness, not just *logical* correctness.
- ☐ Hard real-time = zero deadline miss tolerance (e.g., ABS, pacemaker).
- ☐ Soft real-time = graceful degradation on miss (e.g., video streaming).

**Architecture:**
- ☐ Von Neumann: single bus → bottleneck → poor determinism.
- ☐ Harvard: separate buses → low jitter → *preferred* for hard real-time.
- ☐ ARM Cortex-M (used in CPS): modified Harvard.

**Task Model $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i)$:**
- ☐ $C_i$ = WCET, $T_i$ = period, $D_i$ = relative deadline.
- ☐ Default assumption: $D_i = T_i$.

**Scheduling:**
- ☐ **RMS** = static, by *increasing* period; optimal among fixed-priority schemes.
- ☐ **EDF** = dynamic, by *earliest absolute deadline*; optimal overall, achieves $U \le 1$.
- ☐ **DMS** (Deadline Monotonic) = by *increasing* deadline; used when $D_i < T_i$.

**Analysis Formulas:**
- ☐ Utilization: $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$.
- ☐ Liu–Layland: $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; limit $= \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- ☐ RTA iteration: $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil C_j$.
- ☐ Schedulable iff $R_i \le D_i$ for all $i$.

**Sanity Numbers to Memorize:**
- ☐ $U_{bound}(2) \approx 0.828$, $U_{bound}(3) \approx 0.780$, $U_{bound}(4) \approx 0.757$, $U_{bound}(\infty) = 0.693$.

**Real-World Hooks:**
- ☐ AUTOSAR → RMS-style automotive ECUs.
- ☐ ARINC 653 → partitioned hard real-time avionics.
- ☐ FreeRTOS → open-source RTOS for ARM Cortex-M class MCUs.
- ☐ Watchdog Timer → hardware safety net for embedded firmware hangs.

**Exam-Day Golden Rule:** *Always state the assumption, the formula, the substitution, the numerical evaluation, and the final verdict with a ✓ or ✗ marker. The examiner should be able to follow your logic line-by-line without inference.*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling Techniques for CPS (Discrete-Event, State-Based)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modeling Techniques for CPS (Discrete-Event, State-Based)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is CPS Modeling?

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems built from, and dependent upon, the seamless integration of computational algorithms (cyber components) and physical processes (physical components). A *model* of a CPS is an abstract, mathematical, or graphical representation that captures the structure, behavior, and interaction of these two domains so that engineers can *predict*, *analyze*, and *verify* system behavior before physical deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 1):** *CPS Modeling is the process of formally describing the structure and dynamic behavior of a cyber-physical system through abstract mathematical/graphical notations, enabling simulation, verification, and correct-by-construction design.*

The two pillars of behavioral modeling emphasized in **Module 1** of the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Discrete-Event (DE) Modeling**
2. **State-Based Modeling** (Finite State Machines, Statecharts)

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen as a CPS:

- **Cyber part** = the head chef reading order tickets, calling out "Order 12 ready!"
- **Physical part** = the stove, the pans, the food cooking.

Now consider how you would *describe* what is happening:

- **Discrete-Event view:** You only care about *moments when something changes* — the moment an order is placed, the moment the stove is switched on, the moment the dish is plated. Between these events, the kitchen is "quiet" (nothing changes). This is exactly the **Discrete-Event** approach.
- **State-Based view:** You instead care about *modes* — the kitchen is in state **"Taking Orders"**, then **"Cooking"**, then **"Plating"**, then back to **"Taking Orders"**. Transitions happen when an event (a new order, a finished dish) occurs. This is the **State-Based** approach.

Both views describe the *same* kitchen, but they highlight different aspects of behavior. KTU expects students to master both.

---

### 1.3 Formal Definitions (KTU Board Terminology)

| # | Term | Formal Definition |
|---|------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Discrete Event** | An instantaneous occurrence that changes the state of the system. |
| 2 | **State** | A distinct configuration of the system at an instant in time. |
| 3 | **Transition** | A change from one state to another, triggered by an event/condition. |
| 4 | **FSM (Finite State Machine)** | A computational model with a finite number of states, transitions, and actions. |
| 5 | **Statechart** | An extension of FSMs (Harel, 1987) supporting hierarchy, concurrency, and broadcast. |
| 6 | **Guard Condition** | A Boolean predicate that must be true for a transition to fire. |
| 7 | **Action** | An operation executed when a transition fires, or on entry/exit of a state. |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **2024 KTU Scheme**, Module 1 explicitly tags these two modeling paradigms as foundational, since nearly every high-level CPS design language (Stateflow, Ptolemy, Simulink Stateflow, SCADE) is built on top of one or both of them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Discrete-Event (DE) Modeling

A **Discrete-Event System (DES)** is a system whose state changes *only* at a countable set of time instants (events). Between events, the system is idle.

**Core components of a DE model:**

1. **State variables** — discrete-valued variables describing the system.
2. **Events** — instantaneous occurrences that may change state.
3. **Event queue** — a priority queue (often time-ordered) that schedules the next event to be processed.
4. **Event-driven clock** — time advances to the timestamp of the next event, not by fixed time-steps (as in time-driven simulation).
5. **Event-handling routines** — code/logic that runs when an event fires.

**Operational cycle of a DE simulator (the famous *three-phase* approach):**

- **Phase A — Scan:** Identify all imminent events whose time ≤ current clock.
- **Phase B — Execute:** Run the event routine; update state variables.
- **Phase C — Update:** Insert new events generated during Phase B into the event queue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical DE simulation steps the simulation clock from one event timestamp to the next — this is why DE models are computationally efficient for *sparse-event* CPS such as a packet router, an elevator controller, or a manufacturing line.

---

### 2.2 State-Based Modeling

State-based models describe CPS behavior as a directed graph of **states** and **transitions**.

#### 2.2.1 Finite State Machine (FSM) — Classical Definition

A classical FSM is a 5-tuple:

$$
M = (S,\ \Sigma,\ \delta,\ s_0,\ F)
$$

where:

- $S$ is a finite, non-empty set of **states**.
- $\Sigma$ is a finite set of **input symbols** (events).
- $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$ is the **transition function**.
- $s_0 \in S$ is the **initial state**.
- $F \subseteq S$ is the set of **final (accepting) states**.

#### 2.2.2 Mealy vs. Moore Machines (OUTPUT Semantics)

The classical FSM is purely *language-accepting*. To model **outputs** (e.g., turning on a motor), we extend it to a **transducer**:

| Aspect | Mealy Machine | Moore Machine |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Output depends on | Current state **and** input | Current state **only** |
| Output function | $\lambda : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow Y$ | $\lambda : S \rightarrow Y$ |
| Reactivity | Output changes *instantly* with input | Output changes *after* state transition |
| Typical CPS use | Controllers reacting fast to sensor spikes | Controllers needing stable, glitch-free outputs |
| Model | $Y_t = \lambda(S_t, X_t)$ | $Y_t = \lambda(S_t)$ |
| Transition | $S_{t+1} = \delta(S_t, X_t)$ | $S_{t+1} = \delta(S_t, X_t)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board answers, if the question says *"the motor should turn on as soon as the button is pressed"*, you should choose **Mealy**. If it says *"the motor should turn on only after entering the RUN state"*, choose **Moore**.

#### 2.2.3 Statecharts (Harel Extension)

Classical FSMs explode in size for realistic CPS. Harel's **Statecharts** (1987) — the formalism behind MathWorks Stateflow, UML state machines, and Yakindu — add three orthogonal features:

- **Hierarchy (Nesting):** States can contain sub-states. *e.g.,* `OPERATIONAL` contains `IDLE`, `WARMING-UP`, `RUNNING`.
- **Orthogonality (Concurrency):** The system can be in *multiple states simultaneously* in different regions. *e.g.,* a washing machine is in `(WASHING, HEATING)` in one region and `(DOOR-LOCKED)` in another.
- **Broadcast Communication:** Events broadcast from one region can trigger transitions in another.

Additionally, Statecharts support:

- **Entry / Exit Actions** (`entry /`, `exit /`) — fire automatically when entering/exiting a state.
- **Internal Transitions** — change actions without exiting the state.
- **History Connectors** (`H`, `H*`) — return to the most-recently-visited sub-state on re-entry.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Notation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | FSM tuple | $M = (S,\Sigma,\delta,s_0,F)$ | $S$ finite |
| 2 | Transition | $S_{t+1} = \delta(S_t, X_t)$ | Discrete-time index |
| 3 | Mealy output | $Y_t = \lambda(S_t, X_t)$ | Output reacts same step |
| 4 | Moore output | $Y_t = \lambda(S_t)$ | Output lags one step |
| 5 | Guard condition | $g : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow \{\text{true},\text{false}\}$ | Boolean predicate |
| 6 | Event-driven clock advance | $t_{new} = \min\{\tau_i \;\vert\; \tau_i \in \text{EventQueue},\ \tau_i > t_{now}\}$ | Continuous time |
| 7 | Composite state (Statechart) | $s_{composite} = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| 8 | Number of states (binary encoding) | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | For $N$ logical states |
| 9 | Number of transitions (worst case) | $T_{max} = N^2$ | $N$ states, complete digraph |
| 10 | Reachability (state $q$ from $q_0$) | $q \in \text{Reach}(q_0)$ | Boolean reachability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU answers, write the **tuple form** first, then define *each* symbol. Examiners award step-marks for definitions.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Typical Modeling Technique | Why? |
|--------------------|----------------------------|------|
| Automotive ECU (ABS, airbag) | Statecharts (Stateflow) | Hierarchy + concurrency |
| Medical infusion pump | Mealy FSM with guards | Hard real-time safety |
| Industrial assembly line | Discrete-Event Simulation | Sparse event stream |
| Smart-grid controller | Hybrid (DE + continuous) | Discrete faults + continuous power |
| IoT sensor node | Moore FSM | Low-power state retention |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Logical Requirements to FSM States

**Problem statement (KTU-style):**
*A traffic light controller cycles GREEN (30 s) → YELLOW (5 s) → RED (40 s) → GREEN. An emergency input `E` instantly forces RED regardless of current state. Model this as a Mealy machine.*

**Step 1 — Identify the finite set of states.**
The system has three operating modes: GREEN, YELLOW, RED. So:

$$
S = \{G,\ Y,\ R\}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the input alphabet.**
Two logical inputs are present:

- $T$ = timer expired (1 when time-out occurs, else 0).
- $E$ = emergency (1 when active, else 0).

So the input alphabet is:

$$
\Sigma = \{00,\ 01,\ 10,\ 11\}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the outputs.**
Outputs are the lamp-driving signals: $Y = (G_{lamp}, Y_{lamp}, R_{lamp})$.

**Step 4 — Define the transition function $\delta$.**

| Current State $S_t$ | Input $(T,E)$ | Next State $S_{t+1}$ | Output $\lambda$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $G$ | $00$ | $G$ | $(1,0,0)$ |
| $G$ | $10$ | $Y$ | $(0,1,0)$ |
| $G$ | $x1$ | $R$ | $(0,0,1)$ |
| $Y$ | $10$ | $R$ | $(0,0,1)$ |
| $Y$ | $x1$ | $R$ | $(0,0,1)$ |
| $R$ | $10$ | $G$ | $(1,0,0)$ |
| $R$ | $x1$ | $R$ | $(0,0,1)$ |

(Here $x$ denotes "don't care" — emergency input has priority.)

**Step 5 — Write the closed-form transition function.**
Using Boolean minimization (K-map) on the input bits, we can write:

$$
S_{t+1} = E \cdot R \;\cup\; \bar{E} \cdot \delta_{nominal}(S_t, T)
$$

where $\delta_{nominal}$ encodes the normal cycle $G \rightarrow Y \rightarrow R \rightarrow G$.

**Step 6 — Derive the next-state encoding.**
Let us assign binary codes: $G=00$, $Y=01$, $R=10$. The two-bit next-state logic for the MSB $S_{t+1}^{(1)}$ is:

$$
S_{t+1}^{(1)} = E \;\lor\; (S_t^{(1)} \oplus S_t^{(0)} \cdot \bar{E} \cdot T)
$$

This is a standard *registered next-state equation* suitable for hardware (FPGA/ASIC) or HDL realization.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Output Function for Mealy Traffic Light

Since it is a **Mealy** machine, the output depends on *both* state and input:

$$
Y = \lambda(S, X) = \begin{cases}
(1,0,0) & \text{if } S=G \text{ and } E=0 \\
(0,1,0) & \text{if } S=Y \text{ and } E=0 \\
(0,0,1) & \text{if } E=1 \\
(0,0,1) & \text{if } S=R \text{ and } E=0
\end{cases}
$$

Compact Boolean form (treating $Y=(g,y,r)$ bitwise):

$$
g = \bar{E} \cdot \bar{S^{(1)}} \cdot \bar{S^{(0)}}
$$

$$
y = \bar{E} \cdot \bar{S^{(1)}} \cdot S^{(0)}
$$

$$
r = E \;\lor\; S^{(1)}
$$

**Step-by-step Boolean simplification (K-map for $r$):**

| $S^{(1)} \backslash S^{(0)}$ | 0 | 1 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **0** | 0 | 0 |
| **1** | 1 | 1 |

The map for $r$ is "1" everywhere except $(0,0)$ and $(0,1)$. Combined with the OR-with-$E$ clause, the minimal SOP is exactly $r = E \lor S^{(1)}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Number of States from Requirements (Analytical Matrix)

A common KTU question is: *"How many flip-flops are required to implement a system with $N$ distinct behaviors?"*

**Derivation:**
Each flip-flop stores 1 bit. With $n$ flip-flops we can represent at most $2^n$ distinct codes. To represent $N$ *legal* states, we need:

$$
2^n \geq N \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n \geq \log_2 N
$$

Since $n$ must be an integer:

$$
\boxed{\,n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil\,}
$$

**Example:** A vending machine that dispenses coffee after ₹20 (assuming ₹5, ₹10, ₹20 coins) needs $N = 4$ states (`S0=0`, `S1=5`, `S2=10`, `S3=15`, `S4=20`→`dispense`). With $N=5$:

$$
n = \lceil \log_2 5 \rceil = \lceil 2.32 \rceil = 3 \text{ flip-flops}
$$

---

### 3.4 Reachability Matrix (Analytical)

The reachability matrix $R$ for a state transition graph with $N$ states is defined as:

$$
R[i][j] = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if there exists a path from } s_i \text{ to } s_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

It is computed as the transitive closure of the adjacency matrix $A$:

$$
R = (A \lor A^2 \lor A^3 \lor \ldots \lor A^N)
$$

In Boolean algebra:

$$
R = (A \lor I)^+ \quad \text{(positive closure)}
$$

where $I$ is the $N \times N$ identity matrix (added to include "reach self in 0 steps").

This is the mathematical basis for *model checking* tools used in CPS verification (SPIN, NuSMV, UPPAAL).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Discrete-Event Simulation Loop (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Simulation t0] --> B[Initialize Clock to 0]
    B --> C[Schedule Initial Events into Event Queue]
    C --> D{Pull next event e from queue}
    D --> E[Advance Clock to e.timestamp]
    E --> F[Execute Event Handler]
    F --> G[Update State Variables S]
    G --> H[Generate New Events f S, e]
    H --> I[Insert New Events into Queue]
    I --> J{Simulation Horizon reached}
    J -- No --> D
    J -- Yes --> K[Stop and Report Statistics]

    styleA style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    styleC style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    styleE style E fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    styleG style G fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    styleK style K fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
```

### 4.2 Mealy State Diagram — Traffic Light with Emergency Input

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> GREEN
    GREEN --> GREEN: T=0, E=0 / G=1,Y=0,R=0
    GREEN --> YELLOW: T=1, E=0 / G=0,Y=1,R=0
    GREEN --> RED: E=1 / G=0,Y=0,R=1
    YELLOW --> YELLOW: T=0, E=0 / G=0,Y=1,R=0
    YELLOW --> RED: T=1 or E=1 / G=0,Y=0,R=1
    RED --> RED: T=0, E=0 / G=0,Y=0,R=1
    RED --> GREEN: T=1, E=0 / G=1,Y=0,R=0
    RED --> RED: E=1 / G=0,Y=0,R=1
```

### 4.3 Statechart (Hierarchical + Concurrent) — Washing Machine Controller

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> ON: powerButton / entry: powerOn
    state ON {
        [*] --> IDLE
        IDLE --> WASHING: startWash
        WASHING --> RINSING: timerWashElapsed
        RINSING --> SPINNING: timerRinseElapsed
        SPINNING --> IDLE: timerSpinElapsed
        WASHING --> PAUSED: pauseButton
        PAUSED --> WASHING: resumeButton
    }
    ON --> OFF: powerButton / exit: powerOff
    OFF --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Mealy vs Moore — Side-by-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MEALY[Mealy Machine]
        I1[Input X] --> C1[Combinational Logic delta + lambda]
        C1 --> F1[Flip-Flop S]
        F1 --> C1
        C1 --> O1[Output Y]
    end
    subgraph MOORE[Moore Machine]
        I2[Input X] --> C2[Combinational Logic delta]
        C2 --> F2[Flip-Flop S]
        F2 --> C2
        F2 --> C3[Output Logic lambda only S]
        C3 --> O2[Output Y]
    end
    styleC1 style C1 fill:#bbdefb
    styleC2 style C2 fill:#c8e6c9
    styleC3 style C3 fill:#fff9c4
```

### 4.5 CPS Modeling Hierarchy (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Process Plant] -->|Sensors| B[Cyber Controller]
    B -->|Actuators| A
    B --> C{Modeling Paradigm}
    C --> D[Discrete-Event DES]
    C --> E[State-Based FSM]
    C --> F[Hybrid Automata]
    D --> D1[Events, Queue, Time-Advance]
    D --> D2[Used in packet routers, batch plants]
    E --> E1[FSM, Mealy, Moore]
    E --> E2[Statecharts hierarchical, concurrent]
    E --> E3[Used in ECU, medical devices]
    F --> F1[Continuous plus discrete]
    F --> F2[Used in automotive, aerospace CPS]

    styleA style A fill:#ffe0b2
    styleB style B fill:#b2dfdb
    styleC style C fill:#d1c4e9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper PCCPT303]**
*Differentiate between Mealy and Moore machines in the context of CPS modeling. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Mealy Machine | Moore Machine |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Output function | $\lambda : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow Y$ | $\lambda : S \rightarrow Y$ |
| Output depends on | Current state **and** input | Current state **only** |
| Output timing | Changes in *same* clock cycle as input | Changes only on *state transition* |
| Latency | Lower (combinational output path) | Higher (registered output) |
| Glitch susceptibility | Higher (input glitches propagate) | Lower (output is registered) |
| CPS example | Button-press activates buzzer *immediately* | Machine enters RUN state → *then* motor turns on |

> **Verdict for KTU:** Mealy gives faster response; Moore gives cleaner, more predictable outputs. *(1 mark for the difference in output dependence, 1 mark for timing/latency, 1 mark for an example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper PCCPT303]**
*Define a Discrete-Event System. Why is it preferred for sparse-event CPS such as a manufacturing line? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Discrete-Event System (DES)** is a system whose state changes *only* at discrete, instantaneous moments called **events**. Between events, the state remains constant, and the simulation clock advances to the timestamp of the next imminent event.

**Why preferred for sparse-event CPS:**

1. The system is *idle* between events — no need to integrate continuous dynamics.
2. The event-driven clock skips the empty intervals, yielding **O(events)** time complexity instead of O(time-steps).
3. Each event is processed atomically, which is ideal for sequencing, scheduling, and resource-conflict analysis in manufacturing.

*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: idle-between-events property, 1 mark: efficiency argument with example.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Each 14-mark question contains two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice]**
*(a) Design a Mealy FSM for a 2-bit sequence detector that detects the overlap-allowed pattern "1011". Draw the state diagram and write the transition table. (7 marks)*
*(b) Explain the three orthogonal extensions of Statecharts over classical FSMs. Show, with a CPS example, how concurrency is modelled. (7 marks)*

##### Model Solution (a)

A **sequence detector** outputs `1` exactly when the last four input bits equal `1011`. Overlap is allowed (so after `1011` the next `1` can begin a new match).

**Step 1 — Define states (each = "what suffix have I matched so far?"):**

$$
S_0 : \text{no useful suffix (last bits = "" or "0")}
$$

$$
S_1 : \text{last bit = "1" (suffix "1")}
$$

$$
S_2 : \text{last bits = "10" (suffix "10")}
$$

$$
S_3 : \text{last bits = "101" (suffix "101")}
$$

**Step 2 — Transition table (Mealy, output = $Z$):**

| Present State | Input $X=0$ | Next State, $Z$ | Input $X=1$ | Next State, $Z$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $S_0$ | $S_0,\ 0$ | — | $S_1,\ 0$ | — |
| $S_1$ | $S_2,\ 0$ | — | $S_1,\ 0$ | — |
| $S_2$ | $S_0,\ 0$ | — | $S_3,\ 0$ | — |
| $S_3$ | $S_2,\ 0$ | — | $S_1,\ 1$ | — |

**Step 3 — State diagram (Mealy annotations in `/output`):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0 --> S0: 0 / 0
    S0 --> S1: 1 / 0
    S1 --> S2: 0 / 0
    S1 --> S1: 1 / 0
    S2 --> S0: 0 / 0
    S2 --> S3: 1 / 0
    S3 --> S2: 0 / 0
    S3 --> S1: 1 / 1
```

The output `Z=1` is produced only on the transition $S_3 \xrightarrow{1} S_1$, confirming the detection of `1011`.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for state identification, 2 for table, 2 for diagram, 1 for overlap justification.)*

##### Model Solution (b)

Harel's **Statecharts** extend classical FSMs with three orthogonal mechanisms:

**1. Hierarchy (Nesting):**
States can contain substates. The parent `OPERATIONAL` may contain `IDLE`, `WARMING-UP`, `RUNNING`. This avoids *state explosion* by letting common behavior be factored at the parent level (e.g., a single exit action that logs the timestamp).

**2. Concurrency (Orthogonality):**
The CPS can be in multiple states simultaneously in *different regions*. The notation is a state box divided by a **dashed line** into concurrent regions.

**3. Broadcast Communication:**
Events emitted in one region are *broadcast globally* and may trigger transitions in other regions (subject to local guard conditions).

**CPS Example — Autonomous Vehicle Cruise Controller:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state CRUISE {
        state POWERTRAIN {
            IDLE_P --> ACCELERATING: accelCmd
            ACCELERATING --> CRUISING: targetSpeedReached
            CRUISING --> ACCELERATING: speedBelowThreshold
            CRUISING --> IDLE_P: brakePressed
        }
        state PERCEPTION {
            SCANNING --> ALERT: obstacleDetected
            ALERT --> SCANNING: obstacleCleared
        }
    }
    CRUISE --> OFF: killSwitch
```

- **Region 1 (POWERTRAIN)** controls the engine.
- **Region 2 (PERCEPTION)** runs the LIDAR/radar scan.
- They run **concurrently** — a `obstacleDetected` event from Region 2 can trigger a transition in Region 1 (broadcast).

*(Marks distribution: 1+1+1 for the three features, 2 for the diagram, 2 for the concurrent-region explanation.)*

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Alt]**
*(a) Explain the Discrete-Event Simulation (DES) paradigm. With a clear block diagram, describe the three-phase approach. (7 marks)*
*(b) A car-wash CPS has three stations: Pre-wash, Wash, Dry. Cars arrive every 4 ± 2 min (uniform). Pre-wash takes 3 min, Wash takes 5 min, Dry takes 4 min. Build an event-scheduling table for the first 5 cars and compute the average system time. (Use First-In-First-Out discipline.) (7 marks)*

##### Model Solution (a)

A **Discrete-Event Simulation (DES)** models a system as a chronological sequence of **events**. Time does not flow uniformly — instead, the simulation clock jumps from one event timestamp to the next. Between events, nothing changes.

**Why DES for CPS:**
- Many CPS are *event-sparse* (sensors sample at 100 Hz, but the response of the controller is fast; large idle periods dominate).
- DE skips these idle periods, giving orders-of-magnitude speedup.

**The Three-Phase Approach (Fishman, 1978):**

**Phase A — Scan** the event list, find the most imminent event(s).
**Phase B — Execute** the event routine, update state.
**Phase C — Generate** new (future) events caused by the executed event, insert into the event list.

The block diagram (already shown in **Section 4.1**) is the operational blueprint.

**Key features of DES:**

- Asynchronous time advance.
- Stochastic event timing (e.g., Poisson arrivals).
- Easily extended to *process-interaction* or *activity-scanning* world views.
- Commercially used in: Arena, AnyLogic, SimPy, FlexSim.

*(Marks: 2 for definition, 2 for the three phases, 2 for the block diagram, 1 for an example.)*

##### Model Solution (b)

**Assumptions:**

- Inter-arrival $A \sim U[2, 6]$ min (mean = 4).
- Service times: Pre-wash $P=3$ min, Wash $W=5$ min, Dry $D=4$ min.
- Car moves sequentially: Pre-wash → Wash → Dry → Exit.
- All servers initially idle at $t=0$.

**Generate inter-arrival times for 5 cars (deterministic illustrative sequence):**

| Car | Inter-arrival $A$ | Arrival time $T_{arr}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | — | 0 |
| 2 | 3 | 3 |
| 3 | 5 | 8 |
| 4 | 4 | 12 |
| 5 | 2 | 14 |

**Event-scheduling table (with FIFO):**

For each car we track *Service-Start* and *Service-End* at each station. Service-Start = max(arrival, server-free time).

| Car | Arr | Pre Start | Pre End | Wash Start | Wash End | Dry Start | Dry End | Exit |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 8 | 8 | 12 | **12** |
| 2 | 3 | 3 | 6 | 8 | 13 | 13 | 17 | **17** |
| 3 | 8 | 8 | 11 | 13 | 18 | 18 | 22 | **22** |
| 4 | 12 | 12 | 15 | 18 | 23 | 23 | 27 | **27** |
| 5 | 14 | 15 | 18 | 23 | 28 | 28 | 32 | **32** |

(Note: car 5 waits 1 min for Pre-wash since car 4 still there.)

**System time = Exit − Arrival:**

| Car | System time (min) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 12 − 0 = 12 |
| 2 | 17 − 3 = 14 |
| 3 | 22 − 8 = 14 |
| 4 | 27 − 12 = 15 |
| 5 | 32 − 14 = 18 |

**Average system time:**

$$
\bar{T}_{sys} = \frac{12+14+14+15+18}{5} = \frac{73}{5} = 14.6\ \text{minutes}
$$

*(Marks: 2 for assumptions & arrival schedule, 3 for event table, 2 for system-time formula and final answer.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Mealy & Moore output function signatures** — examiners will deduct 2 marks if you write $Y=\lambda(S)$ for a Mealy question.
> 2. **Forgetting to label outputs in the state diagram** — every transition of a Mealy machine must be annotated as `input / output`.
> 3. **In DE simulation, mixing time-driven and event-driven clocks** — do not write "$\Delta t = 0.1$ s" for a DE model.
> 4. **In Statecharts, drawing concurrency as parallel sub-states *without* a dashed divider** — the dashed line is the formal syntax; missing it = −1 mark.
> 5. **In reachability/transitions, claiming $N!$ transitions** — the bound is $N^2$ (complete digraph), not $N!$.
> 6. **In sequence detectors, ignoring the *overlap-allowed* clause** — KTU explicitly tests the difference between Moore- and Mealy-style "sticky" outputs here.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Discrete-Event Modeling** = event-driven, asynchronous clock, three-phase loop, ideal for sparse-event CPS.
- **State-Based Modeling** = FSM, Mealy (output from $S$+input), Moore (output from $S$ only), Statecharts (Harel extension).
- **FSM tuple:** $M = (S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F)$ — *memorize this verbatim* for KTU.
- **Statecharts add three things:** hierarchy, orthogonality, broadcast.
- **Flip-flop count:** $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ — used whenever you encode states in hardware.
- **Reachability matrix:** transitive closure of adjacency matrix — used in CPS verification.
- **Mealy** = fast, glitchy; **Moore** = slow, clean — pick based on real-time safety.
- For **CPS**, hybrid (DE + continuous) models are the gold standard, but Module 1 tests the **two foundational paradigms** separately.
- Always define the **set of states** before drawing the diagram — examiners award 2–3 marks for a clear state-identification step.
- Use **guards** (`[condition]`) and **actions** (`/ action`) correctly in diagrams.
- Highlight the **boundary/limitation** of classical FSMs (state explosion) to justify Statecharts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation Tools and Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simulation Tools and Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber Physical Systems (CPS)**, a **Simulation Tool** is a software-based computational environment that emulates the dynamic behavior of a coupled system comprising physical processes (sensors, actuators, mechanical/electrical plants) and computational/cyber components (controllers, networks, embedded software). A **Simulation Environment** is the integrated workspace—including model libraries, solvers, visualization kernels, and hardware interfaces—that allows engineers to design, test, validate, and verify CPS designs before physical deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block (Board-Expected Wording):**
> "Simulation tools and environments for CPS are integrated software platforms that combine multi-domain physical modeling, discrete-event cyber modeling, numerical solvers, and real-time I/O interfaces to enable Model-in-the-Loop (MIL), Software-in-the-Loop (SIL), and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing of cyber-physical systems."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **self-driving car**. Before you ever weld a single piece of metal, you want to know:
- Will the **brake** apply fast enough when the **camera** (cyber) detects a pedestrian?
- Will the **network delay** between the sensor and the ECU cause a crash?

A simulation environment is like a **"digital twin playground"**—a risk-free, cost-free, time-free testing ground where the **physical car (plant)** and the **software brain (controller)** are both represented as mathematical models on a computer, communicating just like they would in reality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of it like a **flight simulator** used to train pilots. The cockpit (cyber) responds to the virtual environment (physical world) in real time. Similarly, CPS simulators let "virtual controllers" fly through "virtual plants" to catch design bugs early.

## 1.3 Why Simulation is Indispensable in CPS

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Safety** | Avoids testing dangerous scenarios on real hardware (e.g., autonomous braking failure). |
| **Cost** | Physical prototypes are expensive; simulation costs only compute time. |
| **Reproducibility** | Same scenario can be replayed with identical conditions. |
| **Time Scaling** | Compresses weeks of physical behavior into minutes. |
| **Co-Design** | Allows simultaneous development of physical and cyber subsystems. |

## 1.4 Core Classification of CPS Simulators

CPS simulators are broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis 1 — Time Representation:**
- **Continuous-time simulators** (model differential equations, e.g., Simulink)
- **Discrete-event simulators** (model event-driven systems, e.g., NS-3)
- **Hybrid simulators** (combine both, e.g., Ptolemy II, Modelica)

**Axis 2 — Integration Mode:**
- **Standalone (offline) simulation** — purely software, non-realtime
- **Real-time simulation** — synchronized with wall-clock (e.g., dSPACE, OPAL-RT)
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** — couples real hardware with simulated plant
- **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)** — runs actual controller code against simulated plant

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "list types of CPS simulators," always mention **continuous, discrete-event, and hybrid** as the master classification — this is the exact wording found in KTU 2024 syllabus descriptors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Layers of a CPS Simulation Environment

A typical CPS simulation framework operates across **four logical layers**:

### Layer 1 — Modeling Layer
- Provides domain-specific libraries: mechanical (mass-spring-damper), electrical (RLC circuits), thermal, fluid, and cyber (FSMs, task schedulers).
- Models are expressed in **continuous** form (ODEs), **discrete** form (state machines), or as **hybrid automata**.

### Layer 2 — Solver Layer
- The numerical engine that integrates the model equations forward in time.
- **ODE Solvers:** Explicit Euler, Runge-Kutta 4 (RK4), Backward Differentiation Formula (BDF).
- **Discrete-Event Engine:** Maintains an event queue sorted by time of next occurrence.
- **Hybrid Engine:** Switches between continuous integration and discrete transitions based on guard conditions.

### Layer 3 — Communication / Co-Simulation Layer
- Manages data exchange between heterogeneous simulators via standardized interfaces:
  - **FMI (Functional Mock-up Interface)** — model exchange and co-simulation standard.
  - **HLA (High-Level Architecture)** — IEEE 1516 distributed simulation standard.
  - **TCP/IP or shared memory** — for loosely coupled tools.

### Layer 4 — Visualization & Verification Layer
- Time-series plots, 3D animations, scope displays.
- Formal verification hooks: model checking, reachability analysis.
- **Key performance metrics:** latency, jitter, deadline miss ratio, energy consumption.

## 2.2 Master Formula Table for CPS Simulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** No vertical pipe `|` is used inside the table. For absolute value, the LaTeX `\vert` is used.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Euler Integration | $x_{k+1} = x_k + h \cdot f(x_k, t_k)$ | First-order ODE solver, step size $h$ |
| RK4 Integration | $x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$ | 4th-order accurate ODE solver |
| BDF-2 Integration | $x_{k+2} = \frac{4}{3}x_{k+1} - \frac{1}{3}x_k + \frac{2h}{3}f(x_{k+2})$ | Stiff system solver |
| Real-Time Constraint | $T_{sim} \le T_{wall}$ | Simulation step must finish within wall-clock time |
| Latency Metric | $L = t_{event} - t_{response}$ | CPS timing criticality measure |
| Jitter | $J = \sigma(L)$ | Standard deviation of latency |
| Stability (Euler) | $\vert 1 + h\lambda \vert < 1$ | Region of absolute stability for integrator |
| Co-simulation Error | $e = \frac{\vert y_{coupled} - y_{ref} \vert}{\vert y_{ref} \vert}$ | Accuracy of coupled simulation vs reference |
| Discrete-Event Advance | $t_{next} = \min(\{t_i \mid i \in \text{pending events}\})$ | Event queue mechanism |
| Lyapunov Stability | $\dot{V}(x) < 0, \quad V(0) = 0$ | Condition for asymptotic stability of CPS |

## 2.3 Detailed Tool-by-Tool Engineering Utility

### (a) MATLAB / Simulink
- **Origin:** MathWorks, commercial, ubiquitous in academia.
- **Engineering Utility:** Block-diagram modeling of continuous, discrete, and hybrid systems; auto-code generation; stateflow for FSMs.
- **CPS Relevance:** De-facto standard for control-theoretic CPS design (driveline, robotics, power electronics).

### (b) Modelica / Dymola / OpenModelica
- **Origin:** Open standard (Modelica Association).
- **Engineering Utility:** **Acausal**, equation-based, object-oriented multi-domain modeling. Models physical systems as DAEs (Differential-Algebraic Equations).
- **CPS Relevance:** Excellent for plant modeling; can be co-simulated with discrete cyber tools via FMI.

### (c) NS-2 / NS-3
- **Origin:** Open-source, academic.
- **Engineering Utility:** Discrete-event network simulators for wired/wireless protocols.
- **CPS Relevance:** Models the **network** component of CPS (IoT, VANETs, WSNs) — critical for latency analysis.

### (d) OMNeT++
- **Origin:** Open-source, modular.
- **Engineering Utility:** General-purpose discrete-event simulator with INET framework for networks.
- **CPS Relevance:** Used for wireless CPS, smart grid communication, vehicular networks.

### (e) Ptolemy II
- **Origin:** UC Berkeley, open-source.
- **Engineering Utility:** Models of computation (MoC) — continuous-time, discrete-event, FSM, dataflow, synchronous/reactive — hierarchically composed.
- **CPS Relevance:** Purpose-built for **heterogeneous CPS** where multiple MoCs coexist.

### (f) TrueTime (MATLAB toolbox)
- **Origin:** Lund University, free.
- **Engineering Utility:** Simulates real-time kernels, network transmission, and battery dynamics alongside continuous plants.
- **CPS Relevance:** Industry-academic favorite for studying scheduling, jitter, and network-induced delay in control loops.

### (g) dSPACE / OPAL-RT / National Instruments (NI) VeriStand
- **Origin:** Commercial, real-time.
- **Engineering Utility:** Real-time HIL platforms with FPGA and I/O boards.
- **CPS Relevance:** Final validation before physical deployment; certified for automotive (ISO 26262) and aerospace (DO-178C).

## 2.4 The Three V-Model Testing Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS Verification V-Cycle (Examiner Favorite):**
>
> 1. **MIL (Model-in-the-Loop):** Pure simulation of controller model + plant model.
> 2. **SIL (Software-in-the-Loop):** Generated/handwritten C code runs against plant model.
> 3. **HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop):** Real controller hardware runs against real-time plant simulation.
>
> Each step increases **realism** but also **cost** and **risk** — simulation tools enable the cheap MIL/SIL stages to flush out bugs before HIL.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive:** Engine ECU design (Modelica + Simulink + dSPACE HIL).
- **Aviation:** Fly-by-wire validation (Simulink + NI VeriStand, DO-178C compliance).
- **Smart Grid:** Communication latency studies (OMNeT++ + power system simulator).
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemaker firmware validation (Ptolemy II + HIL rigs).
- **Robotics:** Swarm coordination (NS-3 + ROS/Gazebo).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Goal:** The derivations below are the most commonly tested numerical/analytical questions in KTU exams on simulation. We derive (i) the explicit Euler stability bound, (ii) the real-time deadline constraint, and (iii) the co-simulation error bound.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Euler Stability Region for a First-Order CPS

**Problem:** A physical plant in a CPS is modeled as a first-order linear system:
$$\dot{x}(t) = \lambda x(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$
where $\lambda < 0$ is the (negative) pole of the plant. The simulation environment uses **explicit Euler integration** with step size $h$. Find the **stability condition** on $h$.

### Step 1 — Discretize the ODE using Euler
The explicit Euler method approximates the derivative as:
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + h \cdot f(x_k, t_k)$$

For our linear system, $f(x_k, t_k) = \lambda x_k$, so:
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + h \lambda x_k$$

### Step 2 — Factor out $x_k$
$$x_{k+1} = (1 + h\lambda) x_k$$

### Step 3 — Find the amplification factor
The amplification factor per step is:
$$A = 1 + h\lambda$$

### Step 4 — Apply the stability condition
For the numerical solution to converge to zero (mirroring the real stable plant where $x(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$), we require:
$$\vert A \vert < 1$$

Substituting:
$$\vert 1 + h\lambda \vert < 1$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $h$
For $\lambda < 0$, let $\lambda = -\vert\lambda\vert$:
$$\vert 1 - h\vert\lambda\vert \vert < 1$$

This gives:
$$-1 < 1 - h\vert\lambda\vert < 1$$

The right inequality $1 - h\vert\lambda\vert < 1$ yields:
$$-h\vert\lambda\vert < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h > 0 \;\; \text{(always true)}$$

The left inequality $1 - h\vert\lambda\vert > -1$ yields:
$$-h\vert\lambda\vert > -2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h < \frac{2}{\vert\lambda\vert}$$

### Step 6 — Final Stability Bound
$$\boxed{0 < h < \frac{2}{\vert\lambda\vert}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** If the plant has a fast pole (large $\vert\lambda\vert$), the simulation **must** use a small step size $h$. This is the classic *stiffness* problem in CPS simulation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Real-Time HIL Deadline Constraint

**Problem:** In a Hardware-in-the-Loop test, the simulated plant computes one step in $T_{sim}$ seconds. The wall-clock budget for one step is $T_{wall}$. Derive the **feasibility condition** for real-time execution.

### Step 1 — Define the variables
- $T_{sim}$: simulation computation time per step.
- $T_{wall}$: physical (wall-clock) time span the step represents.
- $\rho$: **CPU utilization** fraction of the real-time simulator.

### Step 2 — Relate utilization to times
$$\rho = \frac{T_{sim}}{T_{wall}}$$

### Step 3 — Real-time feasibility
The simulation step must finish **before** the next wall-clock deadline:
$$T_{sim} \le T_{wall}$$

### Step 4 — Utilization form
Dividing by $T_{wall}$:
$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{T_{sim}}{T_{wall}} \le 1}$$

### Step 5 — Generalization to N concurrent tasks
For $N$ real-time tasks (e.g., plant, network, controller co-simulated together):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{T_{sim,i}}{T_{wall,i}} \le 1$$

This is the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** widely used in CPS real-time scheduling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $T_{sim} < T_{wall}$ (strict). The correct non-strict inequality allows zero-jitter saturation at the boundary — it is **inclusive** $\le$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Co-Simulation Error Bound

**Problem:** Two simulators exchange data every macro-step $H$. The local integration error of each is $O(h^p)$ where $h$ is the local micro-step and $p$ is the solver order. Find the **global co-simulation error**.

### Step 1 — Local error in one macro-step
Each macro-step contains $H/h$ micro-steps. The local error per micro-step is bounded by $C h^{p+1}$.

### Step 2 — Accumulate over one macro-step
$$E_{macro} = \frac{H}{h} \cdot C h^{p+1} = C H h^{p}$$

### Step 3 — Accumulate over total simulation time $T$
Number of macro-steps: $T/H$.

$$E_{global} = \frac{T}{H} \cdot C H h^{p} = C T h^{p}$$

### Step 4 — Interface error from data exchange
Between macro-steps, the data exchange introduces an additional term proportional to $H^{q+1}$ where $q$ is the interpolation order:

$$E_{interface} = K \frac{T}{H} H^{q+1} = K T H^{q}$$

### Step 5 — Total co-simulation error
$$\boxed{E_{total} = C T h^{p} + K T H^{q}}$$

### Step 6 — Optimal macro-step
To minimize $E_{total}$ for fixed computational cost, set derivatives with respect to $h$ and $H$ to balance terms, typically yielding $H \propto h^{p/q}$. The detailed optimization is beyond KTU scope, but the bound above is **exam-ready**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Simulation Tools

| Tool | Type | License | CPS Domain | Key Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MATLAB / Simulink | Continuous / Hybrid | Commercial | Control, plant | Industry standard, codegen |
| Modelica / Dymola | Acausal multi-domain | Open / Commercial | Physical plant | Equation-based, FMU export |
| NS-3 | Discrete-event network | Open-source | Networked CPS | Wireless, IoT protocol modeling |
| OMNeT++ | Discrete-event | Open-source (academic) | WSN, VANET | Modular, INET framework |
| Ptolemy II | Heterogeneous MoC | Open-source | Heterogeneous CPS | Hierarchical MoC composition |
| TrueTime | Real-time kernel sim | Free (academic) | Networked control | Scheduling + network delay |
| dSPACE | Real-time HIL | Commercial | Automotive, aero | Certified HIL platform |
| Gazebo | 3D physics | Open-source | Robotics, drones | 3D rendering + physics |
| ns-2 (legacy) | Discrete-event | Open-source | Older WSN studies | Historical reference |
| Cooja (Contiki) | Networked emul. | Open-source | IoT/WSN | Contiki OS co-simulation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Q: Define a Cyber Physical System (CPS) simulation environment. List any two popular CPS simulation tools.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A CPS simulation environment is an integrated software platform that models, simulates, and analyzes the combined behavior of physical processes and computational/cyber components in a unified framework. It supports continuous, discrete, and hybrid modeling, along with network, real-time, and hardware-in-the-loop testing.

**Two popular tools (any two):**
1. **MATLAB/Simulink** — for control-theoretic CPS design with continuous/hybrid block diagrams.
2. **NS-3** — for discrete-event simulation of CPS communication networks.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two tools. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Q: Differentiate between MIL, SIL, and HIL testing in CPS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MIL | SIL | HIL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plant** | Modeled in software | Modeled in software | Real-time simulated |
| **Controller** | Modeled in software | Real C code | Real hardware ECU |
| **Realism** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Cost & Time** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

*(1 mark for the distinction statement + 1 mark for table = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical CPS simulation environment. List its four major layers and describe the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a first-order physical plant modeled as $\dot{x}(t) = -5x(t)$, $x(0) = 1$. The simulation environment uses explicit Euler integration with step size $h = 0.3$ s. Determine whether the simulation is **stable**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw the diagram from SECTION 4.1 — the 4-layer architecture: Modeling, Solver, Communication, Visualization.)*

**Layer-wise Explanation (5 marks — 1.25 marks each):**

1. **Modeling Layer:** Provides libraries for physical (mechanical, electrical, thermal) and cyber (FSM, task) domains. Models are expressed as ODEs, DAEs, discrete automata, or hybrid automata.

2. **Solver Layer:** The numerical integration engine. Continuous systems use ODE/DAE solvers (Euler, RK4, BDF). Discrete-event systems maintain a sorted event queue. Hybrid systems switch between the two based on guard conditions.

3. **Communication / Co-Simulation Layer:** Manages data exchange between heterogeneous simulators using standards like **FMI**, **HLA**, or shared memory interfaces. Enables multi-tool workflows.

4. **Visualization & Verification Layer:** Provides time-series plots, 3D animations, and formal verification (model checking, reachability). Outputs key metrics — latency, jitter, deadline miss ratio.

**Engineering Utility (closing line, 0 marks — for context):**
Such environments are used in automotive ECU design, smart grid communication, and medical device validation.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
$$\dot{x}(t) = -5x(t), \quad x(0) = 1, \quad h = 0.3 \text{ s}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the pole (1 mark):**
The pole is $\lambda = -5$, so $\vert\lambda\vert = 5$.

**Step 2 — Stability bound for explicit Euler (2 marks):**
From derivation in Section 3.1:
$$0 < h < \frac{2}{\vert\lambda\vert} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compare given $h$ with bound (1 mark):**
$$h = 0.3 \text{ s} < 0.4 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Verdict (1 mark):**
The simulation is **stable**, but the margin is small. If $h$ were increased to $0.5$ s, the simulation would diverge.

**Step 5 — Numerical demonstration (2 marks):**

Amplification factor:
$$A = 1 + h\lambda = 1 + (0.3)(-5) = 1 - 1.5 = -0.5$$

Since $\vert A \vert = 0.5 < 1$, the simulation is **asymptotically stable**. The solution sequence is:
$$x_1 = -0.5, \; x_2 = 0.25, \; x_3 = -0.125, \; x_4 = 0.0625, \ldots \to 0$$

**Conclusion:** The simulation accurately reflects the decaying real plant response. ✔

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **co-simulation** in CPS. Describe the role of the **Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI)** standard with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real-time HIL simulator is configured with the following tasks. Determine whether the schedule is **feasible** using the Liu & Layland utilization bound.
- Task 1: $T_{sim,1} = 4$ ms, $T_{wall,1} = 20$ ms
- Task 2: $T_{sim,2} = 6$ ms, $T_{wall,2} = 30$ ms
- Task 3: $T_{sim,3} = 5$ ms, $T_{wall,3} = 25$ ms **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Co-Simulation (2 marks):**
Co-simulation is the joint execution of two or more simulators — each handling a different subsystem (e.g., physical plant, controller, network) — with synchronized data exchange at discrete communication time steps. It enables modeling of CPS that span multiple domains and tools.

**Role of FMI (3 marks):**
The **Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI)** is an open standard that enables:
- **Model Exchange:** A model packaged as a *Functional Mock-up Unit (FMU)* can be imported into any FMI-compatible simulator.
- **Co-Simulation:** A master algorithm coordinates data exchange between FMUs.
- **Tool Independence:** Allows MATLAB, Modelica, NS-3, etc. to interoperate seamlessly.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to SECTION 4.3 — the three simulators + FMI Master block diagram.)*

**Real-world example:** A Modelica model of an electric vehicle battery exchanges state-of-charge data with a Simulink motor controller and an NS-3 V2X network model — all orchestrated via FMI.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Three real-time tasks with simulation time $T_{sim,i}$ and wall-clock period $T_{wall,i}$.

**Step 1 — Compute utilization per task (3 marks — 1 each):**
$$\rho_1 = \frac{T_{sim,1}}{T_{wall,1}} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20$$
$$\rho_2 = \frac{T_{sim,2}}{T_{wall,2}} = \frac{6}{30} = 0.20$$
$$\rho_3 = \frac{T_{sim,3}}{T_{wall,3}} = \frac{5}{25} = 0.20$$

**Step 2 — Sum the utilizations (1 mark):**
$$\rho_{total} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.60$$

**Step 3 — Apply Liu & Layland feasibility condition (2 marks):**
The condition for schedulability under Rate Monotonic Scheduling is:
$$U_{bound}(N) = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$$

For $N = 3$:
$$U_{bound}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 3(0.2599) = 0.7797$$

**Step 4 — Compare and conclude (1 mark):**
$$\rho_{total} = 0.60 \le U_{bound}(3) = 0.7797 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Verdict:** The schedule is **feasible** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. The CPU has ~18% spare capacity, leaving room for additional tasks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MIL/SIL/HIL order:** Many students write SIL before MIL. The correct V-cycle order is **MIL → SIL → HIL → Physical**.
> 2. **Forgetting the strict/non-strict inequality in real-time:** It is $\le 1$, not $< 1$.
> 3. **Writing $\vert$ in markdown tables:** This breaks the table parser. Use `\vert` in LaTeX-only cells.
> 4. **Omitting units:** Always carry units (ms, s) in numerical HIL problems.
> 5. **Confusing Modelica with Simulink:** Modelica is **acausal/equation-based**; Simulink is **causal/block-based**. Examiners love testing this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exam):**

- [x] **CPS simulation** = joint modeling of physical + cyber + network subsystems.
- [x] **Three simulator classes:** continuous, discrete-event, hybrid.
- [x] **Four architecture layers:** Modeling → Solver → Communication → Visualization.
- [x] **V-cycle:** MIL → SIL → HIL → Physical (each step adds realism + cost).
- [x] **Explicit Euler stability:** $h < \frac{2}{\vert\lambda\vert}$.
- [x] **Real-time feasibility:** $\rho = T_{sim}/T_{wall} \le 1$.
- [x] **Co-simulation standard:** FMI / FMU.
- [x] **Liu & Layland bound:** $U_{bound}(N) = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$.
- [x] **Key tools to remember:** Simulink, Modelica, NS-3, OMNeT++, Ptolemy II, TrueTime, dSPACE, Gazebo.
- [x] **Co-simulation error:** $E_{total} = C T h^{p} + K T H^{q}$.
- [x] **Standard network sims for CPS:** NS-3, OMNeT++ (discrete-event).
- [x] **Standard plant sims for CPS:** Simulink, Modelica (continuous/acausal).
- [x] **Standard real-time HIL rigs:** dSPACE, OPAL-RT, NI VeriStand.
- [x] **Why Modelica is special:** Acausal + multi-domain + equation-based.
- [x] **Why Ptolemy II is special:** Hierarchical Models of Computation.
- [x] **Why TrueTime is special:** Combines real-time kernel + network + plant.

> **End of Module 1 — Simulation Tools and Environments Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types, Principles of Operation, and Applications in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types, Principles of Operation, and Applications in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered, integrated system comprising physical processes (the "physical" component), computational algorithms (the "cyber" component), and communication networks that tightly couple the two through a continuous feedback loop. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCPT303)**, CPS is defined as a system where physical and computational elements are deeply intertwined, where every physical phenomenon is sensed, computed upon, and actuated, often in real-time, and frequently over networked (IoT) infrastructures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (KTU 2024):**
> A CPS is a system that integrates sensing, computation, control, communication, and actuation with physical processes. It transcends a mere embedded system because it requires **networked coordination** and **closed-loop feedback** between the cyber (computation + communication) and physical (sensors, actuators, physical processes) domains.

### 1.2 Key Characteristics (KTU Board Standard)
- **Cyber-Physical Coupling:** Continuous, bidirectional interaction between computational and physical elements.
- **Real-Time Operation:** Deterministic timing constraints (often **hard real-time**, e.g., ABS braking in <10 ms).
- **Distributed & Networked:** Multiple subsystems communicate via wired/wireless protocols.
- **Autonomy & Intelligence:** Often exhibits self-awareness, learning, and adaptability.
- **Heterogeneity:** Combines sensors, actuators, controllers, communication stacks, and physical processes of varying natures.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body" for the IoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: CPS = A Digital Nervous System for a Machine**
> Imagine your hand touching a hot pan:
> - **Sensors (Physical):** Thermoreceptors in skin detect heat.
> - **Computation (Cyber):** Spinal cord + brain processes pain signal.
> - **Communication (Cyber):** Nerves carry the signal electrically.
> - **Actuation (Physical):** Muscles contract → hand withdraws.
> - **Feedback (Loop):** Sensory confirmation that the hand has moved and the heat is reducing.
>
> A CPS does exactly this for machines and engineered systems — a smart grid, autonomous car, or robotic arm.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of CPS Architecture

| Pillar | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing & Actuation** | Interface with physical world | Temperature sensor, DC motor, LIDAR |
| **Computation & Control** | Decision-making logic | Microcontroller, PLC, edge AI |
| **Communication Network** | Connectivity layer | CAN bus, Wi-Fi, 5G, MQTT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU 2024 answer on CPS **must** explicitly establish the **feedback loop** between sensing → computation → actuation → physical process → re-sensing. This is the board examiner's favorite scoring point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content

## 2.1 Classification / Types of CPS

The KTU syllabus broadly categorizes CPS based on **application domain, autonomy level, and architectural style**.

### Type 1: **Safety-Critical CPS** (Hard Real-Time)
- **Definition:** Systems where failure could cause loss of life, environmental damage, or catastrophic financial loss.
- **Core Trait:** Hard real-time deadlines (must respond within a strict, non-negotiable deadline, e.g., airbag deployment in **< 5 ms**).
- **Examples:** Autonomous vehicles, aircraft fly-by-wire, nuclear reactor control, medical ventilators, ABS braking.

### Type 2: **Mission-Critical CPS** (Soft/Firm Real-Time)
- **Definition:** Systems where degraded performance is acceptable, but prolonged failure is not.
- **Examples:** Industrial process control (refineries), SCADA water-treatment plants, smart manufacturing robots.
- **Latency Tolerance:** Typically **100 ms – 1 s**.

### Type 3: **Networked / Distributed CPS** (Multi-Node Coordination)
- **Definition:** Multiple spatially-distributed CPS nodes coordinate to achieve a common objective.
- **Examples:** Smart power grid, intelligent transportation systems (ITS), swarm robotics, multi-robot warehouse fleets.
- **Communication Backbone:** Uses IoT protocols (MQTT, CoAP), industrial buses (CAN, Modbus), or LPWAN (LoRa, NB-IoT).

### Type 4: **Cyber-Physical Systems with Embedded AI / Edge Intelligence**
- **Definition:** CPS that incorporates on-device machine learning for perception, prediction, and control.
- **Examples:** Self-driving cars (CNN-based object detection), smart surveillance (edge vision), predictive maintenance (LSTM on vibration data), Industry 4.0 predictive quality.

### Type 5: **Large-Scale / Infrastructure CPS (Smart City/Grid)**
- **Definition:** City- or nation-scale CPS with thousands of heterogeneous nodes.
- **Examples:** Smart grids, water distribution networks, traffic management systems, building automation (BAS).

### Type 6: **Human-in-the-Loop CPS**
- **Definition:** Systems where humans are an integral decision-making node in the loop.
- **Examples:** Tele-surgery robots (Da Vinci), AR-assisted maintenance, smart prosthetics, drone swarm control stations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "classify CPS," use the **3-tier classification** based on *timing criticality* (Hard / Firm / Soft real-time) and the **application-based classification** above. Always cite at least 2 examples per type.

## 2.2 Principles of Operation of a CPS

The operation of any CPS is governed by a strict, layered control loop. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the **CPS feedback triad: Sense → Compute → Actuate**.

### Principle 1: **Continuous Sensing & State Estimation**
- Sensors (temperature, pressure, motion, vision) transduce physical quantities into analog/digital signals.
- **Sampling theorem (Nyquist-Shannon)** must be satisfied:
  $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
  where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency and $f_{max}$ is the highest physical signal frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Under-sampling causes *aliasing*, a common pitfall in KTU design problems. Always specify sampling rate 2× the signal bandwidth.

### Principle 2: **Real-Time Computation & Control Law Execution**
- The embedded controller executes a **control law** (e.g., PID, state-space) at deterministic intervals.
- The general continuous-time PID control law is:
  $$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
  where $u(t)$ is the control signal, $e(t)$ is the error, and $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are gains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A CPS must execute this control law within a **deadline**. Exceeding the deadline is a **deadline miss**, a defining concept in real-time CPS.

### Principle 3: **Actuation & Physical Response**
- The actuator converts the controller's command into physical energy (electrical, hydraulic, mechanical).
- Examples: motor torque, valve position, robotic joint angle, LED brightness (PWM).

### Principle 4: **Feedback Closure & Stability**
- The new physical state is sensed again, closing the loop.
- For a stable system, the **Lyapunov stability condition** or **poles in left-half-plane (LHP)** for linear systems applies.
- For a linear time-invariant (LTI) closed-loop system with characteristic polynomial $p(s)$, stability requires:
  $$\text{Re}(s_i) < 0 \quad \forall \text{ roots } s_i \text{ of } p(s)$$

### Principle 5: **Networked Communication & Synchronization**
- Multiple CPS nodes share state and commands via a network bus.
- **Key performance metrics:** latency, jitter, bandwidth, packet loss.
- **Time synchronization** (e.g., IEEE 1588 PTP, NTP) is essential in distributed CPS.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling rate, $f_{max}$ = signal bandwidth | Hz |
| Continuous PID | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ | $u$ = control, $e$ = error | V, mA, PWM duty |
| Discrete PID (KTU favourite) | $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p(e[k]-e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k] + \tfrac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2])$ | $T_s$ = sample time, $k$ = sample index | seconds |
| Stability (LTI discrete) | $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all closed-loop poles in z-plane | $z_i$ = closed-loop pole | unitless |
| Bandwidth-Latency Product | $BP = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$ | Useful for network-CPS tradeoffs | bit |
| Deadzone (CPS) | $|e| < \epsilon \Rightarrow u = 0$ | $\epsilon$ = threshold | domain unit |
| Rate-Monotonic Bound | $U = \sum \tfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | $C_i$ = WCET, $T_i$ = period | utilization ratio |
| Energy per Sense | $E_s = V \cdot I \cdot t_s$ | power draw per sample | Joules |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol hygiene:** All absolute-value and modulus expressions use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert z_i \vert$) to remain markdown-table safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Lane-keeping, adaptive cruise control, engine management.
- **Healthcare:** Closed-loop insulin pumps, MRI machines, robotic surgery.
- **Energy:** Smart grid load balancing, demand response, wind turbine pitch control.
- **Manufacturing:** Robotic assembly, vision-based quality inspection, CNC control.
- **Aerospace:** Fly-by-wire, UAV swarm coordination, satellite attitude control.

## 2.5 Mapping to KTU Course Outcomes (COs)

| CO | Bloom Level | What students must demonstrate |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Understand | Classify CPS based on timing criticality and architecture |
| **CO3** | Apply | Draw a CPS feedback loop and label sensing/actuation blocks |
| **CO4** | Analyze | Evaluate the role of network latency on closed-loop stability |
| **CO5** | Evaluate | Justify CPS type selection for a given engineering problem |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous-Time PID to Discrete-Time PID (KTU High-Yield)

> **Why this matters:** The board examiner frequently asks students to "write the discrete equivalent of a PID controller" — a 7-mark standard question.

### Given
A continuous-time PID controller with control law:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

We want the discrete-time form for a digital CPS, where samples are taken at interval $T_s$.

### Step 1 — Discretize the Integral Term
The integral is approximated using the **rectangular (forward-Euler) rule**:
$$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]$$

This means the running sum of error samples, multiplied by $T_s$, approximates the area under the curve.

### Step 2 — Discretize the Derivative Term
The derivative is approximated using the **backward-difference method**:
$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

This is the slope of the error between the current and previous sample.

### Step 3 — Express Each Component at Sample k
Let $e[k]$, $e[k-1]$, $e[k-2]$ be the current, previous, and second-previous error samples.

- Proportional: $u_P[k] = K_p\, e[k]$
- Integral: $u_I[k] = K_i\, T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]$
- Derivative: $u_D[k] = \dfrac{K_d}{T_s} \left(e[k] - e[k-1]\right)$

### Step 4 — Combine into Discrete PID Output
$$u[k] = K_p\, e[k] + K_i\, T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\left(e[k] - e[k-1]\right)$$

### Step 5 — Convert to **Incremental (Velocity) Form** (Board Favourite)
To avoid storing the entire error history, take the difference $u[k] - u[k-1]$:

$$u[k-1] = K_p e[k-1] + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e[j] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\left(e[k-1] - e[k-2]\right)$$

Subtracting gives the **velocity-form discrete PID**:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1] = \;& K_p\bigl(e[k] - e[k-1]\bigr) \\
& + K_i T_s\, e[k] \\
& + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation
- $K_p$ term: reacts to **current error**.
- $K_i$ term: reacts to **accumulated past error** (eliminates steady-state offset).
- $K_d$ term: reacts to **predicted future error** (rate of change; damps overshoot).
