# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Canonical Negative Feedback Loop

The most fundamental control structure in CPS. The error signal $E(s)$ drives the controller, which feeds the plant; the output is sensed and fed back negatively.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r1[Reference r of s] --> sum1{Comparator SUM}
    sum1 -->|Error E s| ctrl1[Controller C of s]
    ctrl1 -->|Control U s| plant1[Plant G of s]
    plant1 -->|Output Y s| out1[Physical Output]
    out1 --> sensor1[Sensor H of s]
    sensor1 -->|Feedback B s| sum1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative** sign convention at the comparator enforces *subtraction*: $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$. Without this sign, the system becomes a **positive feedback / regenerative** system, which is almost always **unstable** (used only in oscillators by design).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PID Controller in the Loop

A practical PID implementation showing how the three terms are computed in parallel from the error signal.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    err1[Error e of t] --> pterm1[P gain Kp]
    err1 --> iterm1[Integral K_i over s]
    err1 --> dterm1[Derivative K_d times s]
    pterm1 --> sum2{Adder}
    iterm1 --> sum2
    dterm1 --> sum2
    sum2 --> uout1[Control signal u of t]
    uout1 --> plant2[Plant]
    plant2 --> yact1[Actual output y of t]
    yact1 --> refcomp1[Compare with reference]
    refcomp1 --> err1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram Reduction Flow (Worked Concept)

This shows the systematic process of simplifying a multi-loop block diagram into a single equivalent transfer function, which is the KTU-expected methodology for solving block-diagram reduction problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1 Identify all loops and forward paths] --> stepB[Step 2 Reduce inner feedback loops using G over 1 plus GH]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Combine cascade blocks by multiplication]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Reduce parallel blocks by addition]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Move take-off points and summing junctions if needed]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Repeat until single block remains]
    stepF --> stepG[Final Equivalent Transfer Function T of s]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow Graph — Mason's Gain Formula Visualization

A canonical example showing two forward paths and three loops, two of which are non-touching. This is the graph-theoretic representation of the same system as a block diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nR[Input R] --> nE[Node E]
    nE -->|"G1"| nA[Node A]
    nA -->|"G2"| nY[Output Y]
    nA -.->|"H1"| nE
    nY -.->|"H2"| nA
    nY -.->|"H3"| nE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Path 1:** $P_1 = G_1 G_2$ (forward, $R \to E \to A \to Y$).
> **Path 2:** None other in this simple graph.
> **Loops:** $L_1 = -G_1 H_1$ (loop $E \to A \to E$), $L_2 = -G_2 H_2$ (loop $A \to Y \to A$), $L_3 = -G_1 G_2 H_3$ (loop $E \to A \to Y \to E$).
> All three loops touch the forward path, so $\Delta_1 = 1$. The graph determinant is $\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2 + L_3)$. Mason's formula gives $T = \dfrac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_1 H_1 + G_2 H_2 + G_1 G_2 H_3}$.

## 4.5 CPS Control Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

A high-level CPS view showing how control theory integrates with sensing, computation, and networking layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    phy1[Physical Plant] --> sen1[Sensor Layer]
    sen1 --> net1[Network Communication]
    net1 --> comp1[Cyber Computation Layer]
    comp1 --> ctrl1[Controller C of s]
    ctrl1 --> act1[Actuator]
    act1 --> phy1
    ref1[Reference Setpoint] --> ctrl1
    ref1 --> comp1
    style phy1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style sen1 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style net1 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
    style comp1 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style ctrl1 fill:#c9f,stroke:#333
    style act1 fill:#f99,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS Insight:** The **Controller block** is the cyber-physical interface. It receives **digitally sampled** error $e[k]$ from the network and outputs a **continuous or discrete** control signal $u(t)$ to the actuator. Latency in the network directly degrades phase margin, potentially destabilizing the physical layer.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define transfer function. State its significance in control system analysis.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:** A *transfer function* is the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system, assuming **zero initial conditions**: $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$. It is significant because: (i) it algebraically describes the input-output relationship independent of the input, (ii) the **poles** determine stability and natural modes, (iii) the **zeros** shape the transient response, and (iv) it enables the use of **block diagram reduction** and **signal flow graph (Mason's gain)** techniques to analyze complex interconnected systems.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with two examples each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:** An **open-loop** system has no feedback path; the control action is independent of the output. Examples: a toaster, a traffic light timer, a washing machine. A **closed-loop** system continuously measures the output, compares it with the reference, and uses the **error** to drive the controller. Examples: a room temperature thermostat, a car's cruise control, an autopilot. Closed-loop systems offer **disturbance rejection**, **accuracy**, and **stability** at the cost of increased complexity and possible oscillatory behavior.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, Module 3 carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (either A or B). Each part below carries **7 marks** for sub-part (a) and **7 marks** for sub-part (b).

### Part B — Question A: Stability Analysis via Routh-Hurwitz

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]**

The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

**(a)** Determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is **stable**. (7 marks)

**(b)** For $K = 10$, find the **damped frequency of oscillation** $\omega_d$ and the **time constant** $\tau$ of the dominant poles. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Stability Range**

The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + G(s) = 0$:

$$s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

Expand the cubic:

$$s(s^2 + 6s + 8) + K = 0$$

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

Construct the **Routh array**:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $8$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{6 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot K}{6} = \dfrac{48 - K}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | $0$ |

For stability, all first-column entries must be **strictly positive**:

1. $1 > 0$ ✓
2. $6 > 0$ ✓
3. $\dfrac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 48$
4. $K > 0$

Combining the conditions:

$$\boxed{0 < K < 48}$$

At $K = 48$, the system is **marginally stable** with sustained oscillation (pure imaginary poles).

**(b) Damped Frequency and Time Constant at K = 10**

Substitute $K = 10$ into the characteristic equation:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + 10 = 0$$

Use the Routh array at $K = 10$:

| Row | Col 1 |
|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{48 - 10}{6} = \dfrac{38}{6} = 6.333$ |
| $s^0$ | $10$ |

The auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row gives the oscillation frequency:

$$6s^2 + 10 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -\dfrac{10}{6} = -\dfrac{5}{3}$$

$$s = \pm j\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{3}} = \pm j \cdot 1.291 \text{ rad/s}$$

The auxiliary equation for the $s^1$ row gives the decay rate. The auxiliary equation corresponding to the row above $s^1$ is $6s^2 + 10 = 0$, whose roots $s = \pm j\sqrt{5/3}$ are the imaginary-axis roots at marginal stability. For a more accurate estimate, we use the **dominant-pole approximation**: at $K = 10$, since $K$ is well below $48$, the real pole and the complex pair are separable.

The cubic $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + 10 = 0$ has one real root near $s \approx -5.14$ and a complex pair $s = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d$. The sum of roots = $-6$ (coefficient rule), so $-5.14 + 2(-\sigma) = -6$, giving $\sigma \approx 0.43$. The product of roots = $-K = -10$, so $(-5.14)(\sigma^2 + \omega_d^2) = -10$, giving $\sigma^2 + \omega_d^2 \approx 1.945$. With $\sigma \approx 0.43$:

$$\omega_d \approx \sqrt{1.945 - 0.185} = \sqrt{1.76} \approx 1.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Damped frequency of oscillation:**

$$\boxed{\omega_d \approx 1.33 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Time constant** (decay envelope $e^{-\sigma t}$):

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\sigma} = \frac{1}{0.43} \approx 2.33 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. Students often **forget to write the closed-loop characteristic equation** before applying Routh-Hurwitz; marks are lost if you skip the step $1 + G(s) = 0$.
> 2. When finding $b_1 = (a_3 a_2 - a_4 a_1)/a_3$, mixing up the sign gives a wrong stability range. **Always re-substitute the boundary value** to verify.
> 3. The auxiliary equation method only works at the **boundary of stability** ($K = 48$); for non-boundary $K$ (like 10), you must solve the cubic numerically or use sum-of-roots / product-of-roots. Marks are lost for blindly applying the auxiliary equation at non-boundary values.

---

### Part B — Question B: Transfer Function and Block Diagram Reduction

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]**

**(a)** For a mechanical system governed by the differential equation:

$$\frac{d^2y(t)}{dt^2} + 7 \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 12 y(t) = 4 \frac{du(t)}{dt} + 6 u(t)$$

Derive the **transfer function** $Y(s)/U(s)$ and identify the **poles** and **zeros**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Reduce the following block diagram to obtain the closed-loop transfer function $Y(s)/R(s)$. (7 marks)

**Block diagram description:**

- Reference $R(s)$ enters a summing junction $\Sigma_1$ (negative feedback from the right).
- Output of $\Sigma_1$ goes into block $G_1$ (gain $2/s$).
- Output of $G_1$ enters a second summing junction $\Sigma_2$ which subtracts the feedback signal from a parallel branch.
- Output of $\Sigma_2$ enters block $G_2$ (gain $1/(s+3)$).
- Output of $G_2$ is the final output $Y(s)$.
- An **inner feedback loop** exists: $Y(s)$ is fed back through $H_1 = 1/s$ to $\Sigma_2$ (negative).
- An **outer feedback loop** exists: $Y(s)$ is fed back through $H_2 = 1$ (unity) to $\Sigma_1$ (negative).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Transfer Function Derivation**

Apply the Laplace transform to both sides, assuming **zero initial conditions**:

$$s^2 Y(s) + 7 s Y(s) + 12 Y(s) = 4 s U(s) + 6 U(s)$$

Factor out $Y(s)$ on the left and $U(s)$ on the right:

$$Y(s) \left( s^2 + 7s + 12 \right) = U(s) \left( 4s + 6 \right)$$

Therefore the transfer function is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{4s + 6}{s^2 + 7s + 12}$$

**Poles** (roots of denominator $s^2 + 7s + 12$):

$$s^2 + 7s + 12 = (s+3)(s+4)$$

$$\boxed{s = -3, \quad s = -4}$$

**Zeros** (roots of numerator $4s + 6$):

$$4s + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow s = -\dfrac{6}{4} = -\dfrac{3}{2}$$

$$\boxed{s = -\dfrac{3}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Note:** Both poles have negative real parts ($-3$ and $-4$), so the system is **asymptotically stable**.

**(b) Block Diagram Reduction**

**Step 1**: Identify the inner feedback loop. The block $G_2 = \dfrac{1}{s+3}$ has feedback $H_1 = \dfrac{1}{s}$ (negative feedback).

The equivalent block of the inner loop is:

$$G_{2,eq} = \frac{G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{s+3}}{1 + \dfrac{1}{s+3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{s}} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{s+3}}{1 + \dfrac{1}{s(s+3)}}$$

Simplify the denominator:

$$1 + \frac{1}{s(s+3)} = \frac{s(s+3) + 1}{s(s+3)} = \frac{s^2 + 3s + 1}{s(s+3)}$$

Therefore:

$$G_{2,eq} = \frac{1}{s+3} \cdot \frac{s(s+3)}{s^2 + 3s + 1} = \frac{s}{s^2 + 3s + 1}$$

**Step 2**: The system now becomes a single-loop feedback with forward path $G_1 \cdot G_{2,eq}$ and unity feedback $H_2 = 1$:

$$G_1 \cdot G_{2,eq} = \frac{2}{s} \cdot \frac{s}{s^2 + 3s + 1} = \frac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 1}$$

**Step 3**: Apply the closed-loop formula for unity negative feedback:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_1 G_{2,eq}}{1 + G_1 G_{2,eq}} = \frac{\dfrac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 1}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(s^2 + 3s + 1)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{2}{(s^2 + 3s + 1) + 2} = \frac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 3}$$

**Final closed-loop transfer function:**

$$\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 3}}$$

**Poles of the closed-loop system:** $s = \dfrac{-3 \pm \sqrt{9 - 12}}{2} = \dfrac{-3 \pm j\sqrt{3}}{2} = -1.5 \pm j \cdot 0.866$

The poles are complex with negative real part → **stable underdamped response**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. In part (a), students often **skip the "zero initial conditions" statement**; it is mandatory for transfer function definition per KTU 2024 scheme.
> 2. In part (b), a very common error is **forgetting to identify the inner loop first**. Students who try to combine the entire diagram at once make sign errors.
> 3. **Sign convention at summing junctions:** KTU examiners deduct marks for using $+GH$ in the denominator instead of $1 + GH$ when feedback is *negative*. Always state the sign explicitly: "$1 + GH$ for negative feedback."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fundamentals of Control Theory**

- **Open-loop** = no feedback; **closed-loop** = feedback with error-driven correction. Closed-loop improves disturbance rejection but risks instability.
- **Transfer function** $G(s) = Y(s)/R(s)$ (with **zero initial conditions**) is the core mathematical model of an LTI system.
- The closed-loop transfer function for negative feedback is $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)H(s)}$.
- The **characteristic equation** $1 + C(s)G(s)H(s) = 0$ governs stability.
- **Poles** determine stability and natural modes; **zeros** shape the response.
- **BIBO stability** requires **all poles in the left half of s-plane** (negative real parts).
- **Routh-Hurwitz criterion**: build the array from characteristic polynomial coefficients; **all first-column entries must be positive** for stability. The number of sign changes = number of RHP poles.
- **Block diagram reduction rules**: series = multiply, parallel = add, negative feedback = $\dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$.
- **Mason's gain formula**: $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum P_k \Delta_k$ — useful for signal flow graphs.
- **Second-order system specs**: $t_r \approx 1.8/\omega_n$, $t_p = \pi/\omega_d$, $M_p = 100 e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$, $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$.
- **Steady-state error** depends on system **type number** (number of pure integrators) and the input form (step, ramp, parabolic).
- **PID controller**: $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$. P → speed, I → accuracy, D → damping.
- In **CPS**, control theory bridges the cyber layer (computation) and the physical layer (actuator/plant). Network latency directly reduces phase margin — a critical CPS design consideration.
- **Final Value Theorem** is valid **only** when all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the LHP. Always check before using $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$.
- For a **3rd-order** characteristic equation $a_3 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$, the necessary (but not sufficient) conditions for stability are: **all coefficients positive AND** $a_2 a_1 > a_3 a_0$.
- The **auxiliary equation** from the Routh array gives the **frequency of sustained oscillation** at the **marginally stable** boundary (where the first column has a zero).
- **Block diagram reduction order**: always reduce the **innermost (smallest) loop first**, then work outward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Systems for CPS Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Systems for CPS Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Control System** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is an engineered, cyber-mediated feedback architecture that continuously senses the state of a physical process, computes a corrective decision through embedded computational logic, and actuates changes back onto the physical world — all within deterministic real-time bounds. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a CPS control system is a closed-loop **Sense → Compute → Actuate** triad governed by a feedback law that ensures the physical plant tracks a desired reference trajectory while satisfying safety, stability, and performance constraints.

The mathematical abstraction is given by a **plant model** $G(s)$ (or $G(z)$ in discrete domain), a **controller** $C(s)$, and a **feedback sensor** $H(s)$, forming the classical **closed-loop transfer function**:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) \cdot G(s)}{1 + C(s) \cdot G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A CPS is *not* a classical control system. The "cyber" layer adds networking, software adaptation, real-time guarantees, and autonomy. Therefore, the controller in a CPS is typically **distributed, networked, and software-defined**, not a standalone analog circuit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control of a Car

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. You want to maintain a **constant speed of 80 km/h** (this is the *reference input / setpoint*). 

- The **speedometer** (sensor) continuously measures the actual car speed (the *plant output*).
- Your **brain + ECU** (the cyber controller) compares the actual speed with 80 km/h.
- If the car climbs a hill, the speed drops; the controller **increases the throttle angle** (actuator command) to bring the speed back to 80.
- This cycle repeats hundreds of times per second — that is **feedback control**.

Now, in a **Cyber-Physical** version of this same car, the controller is not just a chip on the dashboard — it is a **software agent running in the cloud**, communicating with road-side infrastructure (V2X), GPS maps, and traffic databases. The "cyber" half makes it smart, adaptive, and connected; the "physical" half keeps the car moving.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Expected):**
> - **Plant** — The physical process being controlled (motor, robot arm, thermal chamber).
> - **Setpoint (r)** — The desired value we want the output to reach.
> - **Error (e)** — The difference $e = r - y$.
> - **Controller (C)** — The cyber component computing the control law.
> - **Actuator** — Converts controller output into physical action (valve, motor driver).
> - **Sensor** — Converts physical quantity into an electrical/cyber signal.

### 1.3 Why Control Systems Are Indispensable in CPS

CPS applications are characterized by **tight coupling between computation and physical dynamics**. Without feedback control:

1. Disturbances cannot be rejected (wind on a drone, load on a motor).
2. Model uncertainties cause drift (robot cannot reach target precisely).
3. Stability is not guaranteed (a simple proportional gain too high → oscillations).
4. Energy efficiency collapses (always full-throttle on a motor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Constants to Memorize:**
> - Sampling rate in CPS real-time control: typically **1 kHz to 100 kHz**.
> - Control loop latency budget in hard real-time CPS: **< 1 ms** for automotive, **< 100 µs** for aerospace.
> - Standard reference frame for s-domain analysis: Laplace variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$.
> - Standard discrete-time shift: $z = e^{sT}$ where $T$ is the sampling period.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a CPS Control Loop

A CPS control loop is organized as a **hierarchical, layered architecture**:

1. **Physical Layer (Plant)** — Sensors, actuators, mechanical/electrical/chemical process.
2. **Communication Layer** — CAN bus, EtherCAT, TSN, 5G, MQTT, OPC-UA.
3. **Cyber Layer (Controller)** — Embedded software, PLC, edge computer, cloud AI.
4. **Decision Layer** — Higher-level optimization, planning, machine learning.
5. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** — SCADA, dashboards, alarms.

The interplay is what makes CPS fundamentally different from traditional control theory: the **network and software become first-class citizens**, not afterthoughts.

### 2.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control

| Feature | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Output has no influence on input | Output is continuously fed back to input |
| **Disturbance rejection** | None | Yes — controller compensates |
| **Accuracy** | Depends on calibration | Self-correcting |
| **Stability** | Always stable | Can become unstable if poorly designed |
| **Example** | Toaster, washing machine timer | Cruise control, autopilot, pacemaker |
| **Use in CPS** | Rare — used only for cheap, deterministic tasks | Dominant — almost every CPS uses feedback |

### 2.3 The PID Controller — The Workhorse of CPS

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is the most deployed control algorithm in industry — over **95% of industrial control loops** use some variant of PID. Its control signal is:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

The corresponding transfer function (in the Laplace domain) is:

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

#### Role of Each Term

- **$K_p$ (Proportional):** Reacts to *current* error. Higher $K_p$ → faster response but more overshoot; too high → oscillation.
- **$K_i$ (Integral):** Eliminates *steady-state error* (the persistent offset left over after a step disturbance). Removes bias.
- **$K_d$ (Derivative):** Reacts to the *rate of change* of error → damps oscillations, anticipates future error. Improves stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tuning Methods to Remember:**
> - **Ziegler-Nichols (Ultimate Gain):** Increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation; measure period $T_u$; set $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
> - **Cohen-Coon:** Better for first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) models.
> - **Model Predictive Control (MPC):** Used in modern CPS where constraints and multi-variable interactions dominate.

### 2.4 Stability — The Cardinal Rule of CPS

A control system is **stable** if, for any bounded input, the output remains bounded (BIBO stability). Mathematically, all poles of the closed-loop transfer function must lie in the **left half of the s-plane** (i.e., $\text{Re}(s) < 0$), or equivalently **inside the unit circle** in the z-plane for discrete systems.

**Stability criteria you must know:**

- **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** — Algebraic test on the characteristic polynomial $1 + C(s)G(s)H(s) = 0$.
- **Nyquist Criterion** — Frequency-domain test using the open-loop Nyquist plot.
- **Bode Plot** — Gain and phase margins give stability robustness.

### 2.5 Discrete-Time (Digital) Control in CPS

Modern CPS controllers are software, so the control law is **discrete**. The continuous plant $G(s)$ is sampled with period $T$, giving a discrete equivalent $G(z)$ via:

$$G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\} = (1 - z^{-1}) \mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{G(s)}{s}\right\}$$

The discrete-time equivalent of a PID controller is computed using approximations of the integral and derivative:

$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + \frac{K_d}{T}(e[k] - e[k-1])$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon):** To faithfully reconstruct a continuous signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz, the sampling frequency must satisfy $f_s \geq 2B$. In CPS, designers typically use $f_s \geq 10 \times$ the closed-loop bandwidth to avoid aliasing and instability.

### 2.6 Networked Control Systems (NCS) — The CPS Reality

In a real CPS, the sensor, controller, and actuator are often **connected via a shared network** (Wi-Fi, CAN, 5G, Ethernet). This introduces:

- **Time delays** ($\tau_{sc}$, $\tau_{ca}$) that degrade phase margin.
- **Packet loss** that may freeze the control input.
- **Jitter** that breaks deterministic behavior.

The stability condition for an NCS with delay $\tau$ is approximately:

$$\tau < \frac{\pi - \phi_m}{\omega_c}$$

where $\phi_m$ is the phase margin and $\omega_c$ the gain crossover frequency.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closed-loop transfer function | $T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)H(s)}$ | Negative feedback assumed |
| 2 | Open-loop transfer function | $L(s) = C(s)G(s)H(s)$ | Used in Nyquist/Bode |
| 3 | Error signal | $E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + L(s)}$ | For unity feedback |
| 4 | PID controller | $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Laplace form |
| 5 | Steady-state error (step) | $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$ for P-control | $\frac{1}{1+K_p}$ for type-0 system |
| 6 | Steady-state error (ramp) | $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v}$, where $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} sL(s)$ | Velocity error constant |
| 7 | Routh array — first column | All entries $> 0$ ⇒ stable | Sign change ⇒ unstable |
| 8 | Z-transform of $e^{-sT}$ | $\mathcal{Z}\{e^{-sT}\} = z^{-1}$ | Unit delay of $T$ seconds |
| 9 | Sampling relation | $z = e^{sT}$ | Maps s-plane to z-plane |
| 10 | Stability in z-plane | $\vert z \vert < 1$ for all closed-loop poles | Inside unit circle |
| 11 | NCS delay bound | $\tau < (\pi - \phi_m)/\omega_c$ | Maximum tolerable latency |
| 12 | Shannon sampling | $f_s \geq 2B$ | $B$ = signal bandwidth in Hz |
| 13 | Ziegler-Nichols (PID) | $K_p = 0.6K_u$, $T_i = 0.5T_u$, $T_d = 0.125T_u$ | Empirically derived |
| 14 | First-order plant step response | $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Time constant $\tau$ |
| 15 | Second-order system | $\omega_n$, $\zeta$ | $\zeta<1$ underdamped, $\zeta=1$ critical, $\zeta>1$ overdamped |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Lateral control uses PID/Stanley controllers; longitudinal control uses MPC.
- **Smart Grid:** Frequency regulation of generators uses droop control + secondary PI control.
- **Robotic Surgery (Da Vinci):** Haptic feedback loops at 1 kHz with sub-millisecond latency.
- **Quadrotor Drones:** Cascaded PID — outer loop for position, inner loop for attitude, runs at 250 Hz to 8 kHz.
- **Industrial CPS (Industry 4.0):** PLCs with PID auto-tuning driving conveyor belts, CNC machines, chemical reactors.
- **Medical CPS:** Insulin pumps use Model Predictive Control to maintain blood glucose levels.
- **Building Automation:** HVAC control loops use adaptive PI to maintain thermal comfort while minimizing energy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Unity Feedback System

Consider the canonical feedback loop: a plant $G(s)$ with a controller $C(s)$ in the forward path, and a unity feedback $H(s) = 1$. Let the reference input be $R(s)$ and the output be $Y(s)$.

**Step 1: Express the error signal.**
The error is the difference between the reference and the (negated) feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$$

This follows from the negative feedback convention — the sensor subtracts the measured output from the desired input.

**Step 2: Express the control action.**
The controller takes the error and produces the actuation signal:

$$U(s) = C(s) \cdot E(s)$$

This is the "cyber" decision — the software in the PLC computing "what to do" based on the discrepancy.

**Step 3: Express the plant response.**
The actuator-plant cascade converts the actuation into the measured physical output:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot U(s)$$

This is the "physical" layer — the actual dynamics of the motor, valve, or process.

**Step 4: Substitute the control action into the plant equation.**

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 5: Substitute the error expression.**

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot [R(s) - Y(s)]$$

**Step 6: Expand and collect terms.**

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot Y(s)$$

$$Y(s) + G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot R(s)$$

$$Y(s) \cdot [1 + C(s)G(s)] = C(s)G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 7: Solve for the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = Y(s)/R(s)$.**

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s)}$$

This is the **canonical closed-loop transfer function for unity feedback**, and it is the foundation of every CPS controller design. The denominator $1 + C(s)G(s)$ is called the **characteristic polynomial**, and its roots determine the closed-loop poles — and therefore stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The closed-loop system "shapes" the open-loop dynamics. If $C(s)G(s)$ is large in the bandwidth of interest, then $T(s) \to 1$, meaning the output tracks the reference with near-zero error — this is the *high-gain principle* that underpins most feedback design.

---

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: Stability via Routh-Hurwitz

Consider a CPS controlling a DC motor position with the following closed-loop characteristic polynomial:

$$q(s) = s^3 + 2 s^2 + 4 s + K$$

We need to find the range of $K$ for which the system is stable. (This is a typical KTU numerical problem.)

**Step 1: Construct the Routh array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $2$ | $K$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $b_1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | | |

**Step 2: Compute the $s^1$ row entry $b_1$.**

$$b_1 = \frac{(2)(4) - (1)(K)}{2} = \frac{8 - K}{2}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Routh-Hurwitz stability condition — all entries in the first column must be strictly positive.**

$$\frac{8 - K}{2} > 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad K < 8$$

$$K > 0$$

**Step 4: Combine the inequalities.**

$$0 < K < 8$$

Therefore, the CPS is stable for all gain values $K$ in the open interval $(0, 8)$. At $K = 8$, the system is **marginally stable** (pure imaginary poles).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Many students forget to apply the $K > 0$ condition for the last row. The characteristic polynomial must have all positive coefficients for the first column to start positive — a violation of this gives immediate instability without needing the array.

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: PID Design for a First-Order Plant

A thermal chamber (a CPS plant) has the transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{10s + 1}$$

The plant is a first-order lag with time constant $\tau = 10$ s and DC gain $K = 1$. We need to design a PID controller $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ such that:

- Closed-loop bandwidth $\omega_c \approx 0.1$ rad/s.
- Zero steady-state error to a step reference.

**Step 1: Choose $K_p$ to set the bandwidth.**

For a first-order plant with P-control, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p}{10s + 1 + K_p}$$

The closed-loop time constant becomes $\tau_{cl} = \frac{10}{1 + K_p}$, and the bandwidth is $\omega_c = \frac{1+K_p}{10}$.

Setting $\omega_c = 0.1$ rad/s:

$$0.1 = \frac{1 + K_p}{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_p = 0$$

That gives a critically damped response — too slow. Let us set $\omega_c = 0.5$ rad/s (a typical engineering choice for $5\times$ speedup):

$$0.5 = \frac{1 + K_p}{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad 1 + K_p = 5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_p = 4$$

**Step 2: Choose $K_i$ to eliminate steady-state error.**

For zero steady-state error to a step, the open-loop transfer function must be at least **type 1**, i.e., have a pole at the origin. The integral term $K_i/s$ provides exactly this. A common choice is:

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{\tau_i} = \frac{K_p}{4\tau} = \frac{4}{40} = 0.1$$

**Step 3: Choose $K_d$ to dampen overshoot.**

$$K_d = \frac{K_p \cdot \tau_d}{1} = K_p \cdot 0.25 \cdot \tau = 4 \cdot 2.5 = 10$$

But $K_d = 10$ is too aggressive for a thermal plant (causes actuator chattering). Reduce to $K_d = 2$.

**Step 4: Final PID gains.**

$$K_p = 4, \quad K_i = 0.1, \quad K_d = 2$$

**Step 5: Closed-loop transfer function verification.**

$$C(s) = 4 + \frac{0.1}{s} + 2s = \frac{2s^2 + 4s + 0.1}{s}$$

$$L(s) = C(s) G(s) = \frac{2s^2 + 4s + 0.1}{s(10s + 1)}$$

$$T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{2s^2 + 4s + 0.1}{20s^3 + 12s^2 + 5s + 0.1}$$

The characteristic polynomial is $20s^3 + 12s^2 + 5s + 0.1 = 0$. Applying Routh-Hurwitz, all first-column entries are positive, so the system is stable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a real CPS, you would never stop at analytical design. You would simulate in MATLAB/Simulink, then commission the controller on the actual plant, perform bump-tests, and retune $K_p, K_i, K_d$ empirically (often using auto-tuners like those from Honeywell or Siemens).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Controller Choices vs CPS Application

| Application | Latency Budget | Recommended Controller | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smart Grid frequency** | 100 ms | Droop + PI | Simple, distributed, robust |
| **Autonomous car lateral** | 10 ms | Stanley / Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) | Path-tracking precision |
| **Drone attitude** | 1–4 ms | Cascaded PID | High bandwidth, low latency |
| **Chemical reactor temp** | 1 s | PID with auto-tuning | Slow process, large time constant |
| **Robotic surgery** | < 1 ms | Haptic impedance control | Safety-critical, precise force feedback |
| **Building HVAC** | 30 s | Adaptive PI | Energy optimization over hours |
| **CNC machine tool** | 0.5 ms | Feed-forward + PID | Tracking fast trajectories |
| **Pacemaker** | < 1 ms | State machine + PI | Discrete event-driven, life-critical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Canonical CPS Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference r t] --> sumJunction{Sum Node}
    sumJunction -- error e t --> controller[Cyber Controller C s]
    controller -- control signal u t --> networkA[Communication Network Delay T sc]
    networkA --> actuator[Actuator Plant G s]
    actuator -- physical output y t --> process[Physical Process]
    process --> sensor[Sensor H s]
    sensor --> networkB[Network Delay T ca]
    networkB --> sumJunction
    disturbance[Disturbance d t] -.-> actuator
    feedbackLoop[Feedback Path]:::highlight -.-> sumJunction
    classDef highlight fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The reference $r(t)$ enters the summing junction, the error is computed, the cyber controller produces a signal, which is sent (with possible network delay) to the actuator. The plant produces a physical output that is sensed, sent back through the network, and compared to the reference. Disturbances $d(t)$ are unknown inputs that the feedback must reject.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical CPS Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L5["LAYER 5: HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE"]
        scada[SCADA Dashboard]
        hmi[Operator HMI]
    end
    subgraph L4["LAYER 4: SUPERVISORY AND OPTIMIZATION"]
        mpc[Model Predictive Controller]
        optimizer[Energy Optimizer]
    end
    subgraph L3["LAYER 3: CYBER CONTROL COMPUTATION"]
        pid1[PID Loop 1]
        pid2[PID Loop 2]
        stateMachine[State Machine Logic]
    end
    subgraph L2["LAYER 2: COMMUNICATION NETWORK"]
        tsn[Time Sensitive Network]
        canBus[CAN Bus]
        wireless[5G Wi Fi 6E]
    end
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL PLANT"]
        motor1[Drive Motor]
        motor2[Cooling Pump]
        sensorPack[Sensor Array]
    end
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Feedback Loop Stability Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([System Start]) --> check{Is Open Loop Stable}
    check -- Yes --> checkTwo{Is Closed Loop Stable}
    check -- No --> redesign[Redesign Plant or Add Compensation]
    redesign --> checkTwo
    checkTwo -- Yes --> margins[Compute Gain and Phase Margins]
    checkTwo -- No --> tuning[Apply Routh Hurwitz or Root Locus]
    tuning --> checkTwo
    margins --> performance{Meets Bandwidth Spec}
    performance -- Yes --> implement[Deploy to CPS]
    performance -- No --> controllerTune[Retune PID or Switch to MPC]
    controllerTune --> performance
    implement --> endNode([CPS Operational])
    style start fill:#cce5ff
    style endNode fill:#ccffcc
    style redesign fill:#ffcccc
    style tuning fill:#fff4cc
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Textual Schematic)

For CPS engineers, the **functional flow** of a control system maps as follows:

```
                +----------------------+
                |   REFERENCE INPUT    |
                |   r(t) = setpoint    |
                +----------+-----------+
                           |
                           v
                  +------------------+
                  |   COMPARATOR     |  <-- SUMMING JUNCTION
                  |   e = r - y      |
                  +--------+---------+
                           |
                           v
        +--------------------------------------+
        |     CYBER CONTROLLER (SOFTWARE)      |
        |  C(s) = Kp + Ki/s + Kd*s            |
        |  Runs on PLC / Embedded Linux        |
        +-----------------+--------------------+
                          |
                          v
                +-------------------+
                |  ACTUATOR + PLANT |
                |  G(s): physical   |
                |  process dynamics |
                +---------+---------+
                          |
                          v
                +-------------------+
                |  PHYSICAL OUTPUT  |
                |  y(t): measured   |
                |  (e.g., position, |
                |  speed, temp)     |
                +---------+---------+
                          |
                          v
                  +----------------+
                  |    SENSOR      |
                  |  H(s) = 1     |
                  +-------+--------+
                          |
        +-----------------+
        |
        +----> back to COMPARATOR (FEEDBACK)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners often ask you to **draw the block diagram of a CPS control system** or to **identify the components** in a given diagram. Memorize the signal flow: $R \to \Sigma \to C \to G \to Y \to H \to \Sigma$. The summing junction with the negative sign is what makes the loop "negative feedback."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with a CPS example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent — output does not influence input | Present — output fed back to input |
| Accuracy | Depends on calibration; degrades with disturbance | Self-corrects disturbances |
| Stability | Always stable | Can become unstable if misdesigned |
| Cost / Complexity | Low | Higher (sensors, software) |
| **CPS Example** | A microwave oven running on a fixed timer (no temperature sensor) | A smart thermostat regulating room temperature using a temperature sensor and PI control |

Open-loop is used in CPS only for **deterministic, low-cost, safety-tolerant** tasks; nearly all safety-critical CPS (autonomous vehicles, medical devices, aircraft) use closed-loop.

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the three terms of a PID controller and explain the role of the derivative term with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A PID controller has three components:
- **Proportional ($K_p e(t)$):** Reacts to the current error magnitude.
- **Integral ($K_i \int e\, dt$):** Accumulates past errors; eliminates steady-state offset.
- **Derivative ($K_d \frac{de}{dt}$):** Reacts to the *rate of change* of error.

**Role of Derivative Term — Example:** Consider a car approaching a wall in autonomous emergency braking. The proportional term reacts to "how far" the car is from the wall. The derivative term reacts to "how fast the distance is shrinking." A high derivative gain anticipates imminent collision and applies brakes earlier than a pure P-controller would. It acts as a **damping force** that prevents overshoot and oscillation in a CPS — essential when the plant has inertia (motors, drones, robots).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking tip:** Mentioning the *anticipatory* nature of the derivative action is worth 1 mark; the example is worth 1 mark; the formula is worth 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Part B questions may be either **full 14-mark problems** or may be split into **two 7-mark sub-parts**. The format below matches the **2024 Scheme ESE pattern** with an internal choice between two full-length questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A CPS controls the speed of a DC motor with plant transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+5)}$$

A proportional controller $C(s) = K_p$ is used in unity feedback. Determine the range of $K_p$ for which the closed-loop system is stable. Comment on the steady-state error for a unit ramp input.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Networked Control System (NCS) in a CPS with a neat block diagram. Discuss two challenges introduced by the network and how they affect closed-loop stability.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1: Form the open-loop transfer function.**

$$L(s) = C(s) \cdot G(s) = K_p \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+5)} = \frac{10 K_p}{s(s+5)}$$

**Step 2: Form the characteristic equation $1 + L(s) = 0$.**

$$1 + \frac{10 K_p}{s(s+5)} = 0$$

$$s(s+5) + 10 K_p = 0$$

$$s^2 + 5s + 10 K_p = 0$$

**Step 3: Apply Routh-Hurwitz.**

The characteristic polynomial $s^2 + 5s + 10K_p$ is second-order, so for stability we need all coefficients positive:

$$5 > 0 \quad\checkmark$$

$$10 K_p > 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_p > 0$$

Therefore, the system is stable for **all $K_p > 0$**.

**Step 4: Steady-state error for a unit ramp input.**

The ramp input has Laplace transform $R(s) = 1/s^2$. The open-loop transfer function $L(s) = \frac{10K_p}{s(s+5)}$ is a **type-1** system (one pole at origin). The velocity error constant is:

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot L(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{10 K_p}{s(s+5)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10 K_p}{s+5} = \frac{10 K_p}{5} = 2 K_p$$

The steady-state error for a unit ramp is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{2 K_p}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.** As $K_p$ increases, $e_{ss}$ decreases. In the limit $K_p \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$. However, in practice, increasing $K_p$ too much amplifies noise and reduces phase margin — so a trade-off is required. To achieve **exactly zero** steady-state error to a ramp, we would need a **type-2** system, i.e., add an integral term (PI or PID controller).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Architecture of an NCS in CPS:**

A Networked Control System is a closed-loop control system in which the communication between sensor, controller, and actuator occurs over a **shared digital network** (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, CAN, 5G, etc.) rather than dedicated point-to-point wiring.

**Block diagram:**

```
        r(t)        +         u(t)
   ----> (+) ----> | CONTROLLER | ----> [Network τ_ca] ----> | ACTUATOR/PLANT | ---- y(t)
         ^ -                                                                             |
         |                                                                               |
         |   +---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
         |   |
         |   v
         | [Network τ_sc] <---- | SENSOR | <---- y(t)
         |                     +--------+
         +------------------------+
```

**Two Key Challenges:**

1. **Time Delays (Latency):** The network introduces $\tau_{sc}$ (sensor-to-controller) and $\tau_{ca}$ (controller-to-actuator) delays. Total round-trip delay $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ reduces the **phase margin** by $\omega_c \cdot \tau$ radians. If the delay becomes too large, the closed-loop system becomes unstable. The maximum tolerable delay is:
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\pi - \phi_m}{\omega_c}$$

2. **Packet Loss and Jitter:** Real networks drop packets (Wi-Fi interference, congestion). When a control packet is lost, the controller may need to use a **zero-order hold** (oldest value) or **prediction** (model-based). High jitter — variability in packet arrival time — also breaks deterministic behavior. CPS that need hard real-time guarantees (e.g., automotive drive-by-wire) use **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** or **CAN-FD** to bound delays.

**Mitigation strategies:** Event-triggered control, model-based prediction, network-aware controller design, redundant communication channels.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a unity feedback control system. A CPS thermal process has plant $G(s) = \frac{2}{s+3}$. With a PI controller $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$, find the steady-state error for a unit step input as a function of $K_p$ and $K_i$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of a CPS control system. Discuss why real-time guarantees are essential in CPS, giving two examples of failures when deadlines are missed.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

The forward path consists of the controller $C(s)$ and plant $G(s)$. The feedback path is $H(s) = 1$ (unity). Let $E(s)$ be the error and $Y(s)$ the output.

The error is:
$$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$$

The control action is:
$$U(s) = C(s) E(s)$$

The plant output is:
$$Y(s) = G(s) U(s) = C(s) G(s) E(s)$$

Substituting $E(s)$:
$$Y(s) = C(s) G(s) [R(s) - Y(s)]$$

$$Y(s) [1 + C(s)G(s)] = C(s) G(s) R(s)$$

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s)}}$$

**Steady-state error for the thermal process:**

The open-loop transfer function with the PI controller is:

$$L(s) = C(s) G(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{2}{s+3} = \frac{2(K_p s + K_i)}{s(s+3)}$$

This is a **type-1** system (one integrator from the PI controller). For a unit step input $R(s) = 1/s$, the steady-state error is given by the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{1/s}{1 + \frac{2(K_p s + K_i)}{s(s+3)}}$$

$$E(s) = \frac{s+3}{s(s+3) + 2(K_p s + K_i)}$$

Applying the Final Value Theorem:
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s(s+3)}{s(s+3) + 2(K_p s + K_i)}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s(s+3)}{s^2 + 3s + 2K_p s + 2K_i} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{0}{2K_i} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** The steady-state error to a unit step is **exactly zero** for any $K_p > 0$ and $K_i > 0$. This is the key benefit of including an integral term — it guarantees **zero offset** to a step reference, which is why PI control is dominant in industrial CPS (temperature, pressure, level control).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Layered CPS Architecture:**

A CPS control system is organized into the following layers:

| Layer | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **5. Human-Machine Interface** | Visualization, alarms, manual override | SCADA dashboards, control room |
| **4. Supervisory / Optimization** | Setpoint generation, MPC, energy mgmt | Cloud-based scheduler, AI planner |
| **3. Cyber Control** | Software-based feedback law (PID, state machine) | PLC code, edge computer |
| **2. Communication** | Real-time data exchange between nodes | CAN, TSN, EtherCAT, 5G, OPC-UA |
| **1. Physical Plant** | The actual process and I/O | Sensors, actuators, motors, valves |

Data flows **downward** (commands) and **upward** (measurements). Each layer has its own time scale: physical layer is **fastest** (sub-millisecond), supervisory layer is **slowest** (seconds to minutes).

**Why Real-Time Guarantees Are Essential:**

A CPS is real-time when the correctness of a control action depends not only on its *value* but also on the *time* at which it is delivered. Missing a deadline can mean physical harm or system damage.

**Two Examples of Failures:**

1. **Automotive Drive-by-Wire:** If the brake-by-wire packet arrives 50 ms late, the brake actuator applies force too late, the stopping distance increases, and a collision becomes possible. The 2024 KTU syllabus emphasizes ISO 26262, which mandates **deterministic latency** for safety-critical vehicle functions.

2. **Aircraft Flight Control:** A modern fly-by-wire aircraft (Boeing 777, Airbus A320) runs control loops at 50–100 Hz. If the deadline is missed, the surfaces may not move when commanded, leading to **loss of control**. The famous case of the **Patriot missile defense failure in 1991** (Dhahran, Saudi Arabia) was caused by a 0.34 s clock drift accumulating over 100 hours — leading to a 500 m tracking error and 28 casualties. This is a canonical textbook example of real-time failure in a CPS.

**Design implication:** Designers must perform **worst-case execution time (WCET) analysis**, schedule tasks with **rate-monotonic or deadline-monotonic scheduling**, and use **formal verification** to prove that no deadline is ever missed under all operating conditions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** at the summing junction in block diagrams. The closed-loop transfer function is $\frac{CG}{1 + CG}$ for **negative** feedback. If the diagram shows positive feedback, the formula is $\frac{CG}{1 - CG}$ — and stability analysis is reversed.
> 2. **Conflating poles of open-loop with closed-loop.** Students often say "the plant has a pole at the origin, so the system is unstable." This is wrong — the *closed-loop* poles determine stability, not the open-loop poles.
> 3. **Skipping the final verification step.** After designing a PID, you must apply Routh-Hurwitz or check pole locations to confirm stability. Designing gains without stability analysis loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up $K_v$, $K_p$, $K_a$** (position, velocity, acceleration error constants). For a type-1 system, $K_p = \infty$ (zero error to step), $K_v$ finite, $K_a = 0$. Writing $K_v = \infty$ is wrong.
> 5. **In NCS questions, ignoring the delay term** when writing the characteristic equation. The delay $e^{-s\tau}$ must appear in the denominator for accurate stability analysis.
> 6. **Using SI units inconsistently.** If $\omega$ is in rad/s, $f$ must be converted as $f = \omega/(2\pi)$ Hz. Sampling frequency units are Hz; time constants are in seconds.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Control Systems for CPS:**

- ☐ **Closed-loop transfer function** for unity feedback: $T(s) = \frac{CG}{1 + CG}$.
- ☐ **Open-loop transfer function** $L(s) = CG$; **characteristic equation** $1 + L(s) = 0$.
- ☐ **PID controller formula:** $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
- ☐ **$K_p$** reduces rise time, **$K_i$** removes steady-state error, **$K_d$** damps overshoot.
- ☐ **BIBO stability** requires all closed-loop poles in the left half of the s-plane.
- ☐ **Routh-Hurwitz:** all first-column entries of the Routh array must be positive.
- ☐ **Steady-state error** for type-N system: zero to polynomial inputs of degree $< N$.
- ☐ **Velocity error constant** $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} sL(s)$; ramp error $e_{ss} = 1/K_v$.
- ☐ **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2B$; discrete equivalent $z = e^{sT}$.
- ☐ **Stability in z-plane:** all closed-loop poles inside the unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$).
- ☐ **NCS delay bound:** $\tau < (\pi - \phi_m)/\omega_c$ for stability.
- ☐ **Ziegler-Nichols PID:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- ☐ **CPS is not classical control:** network, software, and real-time constraints are first-class.
- ☐ **CPS layers:** Physical → Communication → Cyber Control → Supervisory → HMI.
- ☐ **Real-time deadlines are safety-critical** — missing one can mean physical harm.
- ☐ **Laplace transform** of $\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$ is $E(s)/s$; of $de/dt$ is $sE(s)$.
- ☐ **Final Value Theorem** applies only if all poles of $sE(s)$ are in the open left half-plane.
- ☐ **Common CPS applications:** autonomous vehicles, drones, smart grid, robotics, medical devices, Industry 4.0.
- ☐ **MPC** is preferred over PID for multi-variable, constrained, slow processes.
- ☐ **Cascaded control** is used for fast inner loops (current/voltage) and slow outer loops (position/speed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Advice:** For Part A, expect a 3-mark question on **definitions, PID terms, or open vs closed loop**. For Part B, expect a **14-mark question combining derivation + stability + real-world CPS context**. Always draw a **block diagram** wherever possible — it earns easy marks and demonstrates conceptual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) and Scheduling Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) and Scheduling Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS):** A specialized operating system designed to manage hardware resources and execute tasks within strict, deterministic timing constraints, where the *correctness* of the system depends not only on the logical output but also on the **time at which the output is produced**.

In the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), an RTOS acts as the **deterministic computational bridge** between the physical sensing/actuation layer and the software decision-making layer. Unlike General-Purpose Operating Systems (GPOS) such as Windows or Linux—which prioritize *throughput* and *fairness*—an RTOS prioritizes *worst-case latency*, *bounded jitter*, and *temporal predictability*.

### 1.2 Key Characteristics of an RTOS

- **Determinism:** The system responds to inputs within a guaranteed, bounded, and predictable time window.
- **Bounded Response Time:** Maximum execution latency is mathematically provable (used in schedulability analysis).
- **Reliability:** Must operate flawlessly for long durations (often years without reboot), as in automotive ECUs and avionics.
- **Minimal Interrupt Latency:** Hardware interrupts are serviced within microseconds.
- **Preemptive, Priority-Based Scheduling:** Higher-priority tasks can immediately preempt lower-priority ones.
- **Small Footprint:** Designed for resource-constrained embedded targets (often 32 KB – 512 KB RAM).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The ICU Trauma Ward"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a hospital's Emergency Room. A GPOS is like a regular clinic where patients are treated on a first-come, first-served basis, with a goal of *treating the maximum number of people per day (throughput)*. An RTOS is like an **ICU Trauma Ward**, where a critical patient arriving at 2:00 PM with a gunshot wound **must** be treated before the patient who arrived at 9:00 AM with a sprained ankle—even if the latter has been waiting longer. The system does not optimize for *average wait time*; it optimizes for *guaranteed response within a deadline*. Missing a deadline is equivalent to patient death.

### 1.4 Hard vs. Soft Real-Time Systems

| Parameter | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Deadline miss consequence** | **Catastrophic** (system failure, safety hazard) | **Degraded quality** (acceptable, not ideal) |
| **Guarantee required** | Absolute, mathematically proven | Statistical / best-effort |
| **Typical CPS example** | Airbag deployment ECU, Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) | Smartphone video streaming, smart thermostat |
| **Latency tolerance** | Microseconds to milliseconds | Tens of milliseconds acceptable |
| **Operating system class** | VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS (hard config) | Embedded Linux, RTLinux |

### 1.5 The Three Task States in RTOS

Every task in an RTOS cycles through three fundamental states:

1. **Running:** Currently executing on the CPU.
2. **Ready:** Eligible to execute but waiting because a higher-priority task is running.
3. **Blocked / Waiting:** Cannot execute until a specific event occurs (e.g., I/O completion, semaphore release, timer expiry).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scheduling Classification in CPS

Scheduling in RTOS is the act of deciding *which ready task* should be allocated to the *CPU* at any given instant. The taxonomy is as follows:

- **Preemptive vs. Non-Preemptive:**
  - *Non-preemptive:* Once a task starts executing, it runs to completion or until it blocks. Simple, but poor for hard real-time.
  - *Preemptive:* A higher-priority task can immediately *steal* the CPU from a running lower-priority task. Industry standard for CPS.

- **Static vs. Dynamic Priority:**
  - *Static:* Priority assigned at design time (e.g., Rate Monotonic).
  - *Dynamic:* Priority changes at runtime (e.g., Earliest Deadline First).

- **Off-line vs. On-line:** Whether the schedule is computed at compile time or runtime.

### 2.2 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS / RM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** Liu and Layland (1973) proposed RMS as a *static-priority* preemptive scheduling algorithm. The core rule is: **The task with the shortest period (highest request rate) is assigned the highest priority.**

Assumptions of the Liu–Layland Model:
1. All tasks are periodic and independent.
2. All tasks have a deadline equal to their period ($D_i = T_i$).
3. The execution time $C_i$ is constant and known.
4. Context switch overhead is negligible.
5. Tasks are preemptable.

### 2.3 Earliest Deadline First (EDF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Earliest Deadline First (EDF):** A *dynamic-priority* preemptive algorithm. At every scheduling decision point, the task whose absolute deadline is *closest* is given the CPU. It is optimal in the sense that if any algorithm can schedule a task set, EDF can.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit | Constraint / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_i$ | Period of task $i$ | ms | Time between successive activations |
| $C_i$ | Worst-case execution time (WCET) of task $i$ | ms | Assumed constant and known |
| $D_i$ | Relative deadline of task $i$ | ms | For RM/EDF: $D_i = T_i$ |
| $U_i$ | CPU utilization of task $i$ | dimensionless | $U_i = C_i / T_i$ |
| $U_{total}$ | Total CPU utilization | dimensionless | $U_{total} = \sum (C_i / T_i)$ |
| $U_{RMS}$ | RMS schedulability bound | dimensionless | $U_{RMS} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ |
| $U_{EDF}$ | EDF schedulability bound | dimensionless | $U_{EDF} \leq 1.0$ |
| $R_i$ | Worst-case response time of task $i$ | ms | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ |
| $P_i$ | Priority of task $i$ | rank | Inversely proportional to $T_i$ for RM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liu–Layland RMS Bound:** A set of $n$ periodic tasks is *guaranteed* to be schedulable under RMS if and only if the total utilization satisfies: $U_{total} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. As $n \to \infty$, the bound converges to $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. EDF, in contrast, can achieve $100\%$ utilization (theoretically $U_{total} \leq 1.0$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern automotive CPS (e.g., AUTOSAR architecture), the *Electronic Control Unit (ECU)* runs multiple tasks: engine control at 10 ms period, brake-by-wire at 5 ms, body controller at 100 ms. RMS guarantees the 5 ms brake task always wins, ensuring the safety-critical deadline is met. In aerospace (DO-178C certified systems), QNX RTOS uses EDF-style scheduling for flight control surfaces.

### 2.6 Priority Inversion Problem

> [!WARNING]
> **Priority Inversion:** A pathological scenario where a *low-priority* task holds a resource needed by a *high-priority* task, while *medium-priority* tasks preempt the low-priority task, indirectly blocking the high-priority one. This famously caused the **Mars Pathfinder rover anomaly (1997)**, requiring a remote software patch.

**Solution — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a low-priority task, the low-priority task *temporarily inherits* the high priority until it releases the resource, preventing medium-priority tasks from preempting it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Liu–Layland RMS Utilization Bound

We will derive the sufficient (but not necessary) schedulability condition for $n$ periodic tasks under RMS.

**Step 1 — Define the critical instant.**
The worst-case scheduling scenario for any task $\tau_i$ occurs at the **critical instant**—the moment when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with $\tau_i$. This is because the queue of pending jobs is maximal.

**Step 2 — Construct the worst-case response time.**
At the critical instant, the response time $R_i$ of task $\tau_i$ must satisfy the recursive fixed-point equation. For $n$ tasks with priority order such that $\tau_1$ has the highest priority:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

where the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ counts the number of times a higher-priority task preempts $\tau_i$ within the window $R_i^{(k)}$.

**Step 3 — Termination condition.**
Iterate until convergence: $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$. The task is schedulable if and only if $R_i \leq D_i$.

**Step 4 — Sufficient utilization bound (single task).**
For $n=1$, the bound is $U \leq 1(2^{1/1} - 1) = 1.0$. Intuitively, one task can use the entire CPU.

**Step 5 — Two-task case ($n=2$).**
The worst case for $n$ tasks occurs when utilization approaches the bound, requiring the harmonic relationship $T_2 = k \cdot T_1$. Through induction (see Liu & Layland, 1973):

$$
U_{bound}(2) = 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.828
$$

**Step 6 — General case induction.**
For general $n$, applying the inductive hypothesis and bounding the ceiling terms:

$$
U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)
$$

**Step 7 — Limiting behavior.**
Applying L'Hôpital's rule as $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n}
$$

$$
= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{-\frac{\ln 2}{n^2} 2^{1/n}}{-1/n^2} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

$$
\boxed{\;U_{RMS} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1) \quad \text{and} \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} U_{RMS} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693\;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Schedulability Test

**Problem:** A CPS has three periodic tasks. Determine if they are schedulable under RMS.

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 | 10 |

**Step 1 — Assign priorities by period (shortest = highest).**
- $\tau_1$ ($T=4$) → Highest
- $\tau_2$ ($T=6$) → Middle
- $\tau_3$ ($T=10$) → Lowest

**Step 2 — Compute individual utilizations.**

$$
U_1 = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250, \quad U_2 = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333, \quad U_3 = \frac{3}{10} = 0.300
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total utilization.**

$$
U_{total} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 = 0.883
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Liu–Layland bound for $n=3$.**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 5 — Compare.**

$$
U_{total} = 0.883 \; > \; U_{bound} = 0.780
$$

The utilization test **fails** the sufficient condition. However, the sufficient test is *conservative*. We must perform **response-time analysis (RTA)** for a definitive answer.

**Step 6 — Response-Time Analysis for $\tau_3$ (the lowest priority, longest period).**

$$
R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil (2) = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil (2) = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil (2) = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9
$$

$$
R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil (2) = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10
$$

$$
R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil (2) = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10
$$

Converged: $R_3 = 10$ ms.

**Step 7 — Compare with deadline.**

$$
R_3 = 10 \; \leq \; D_3 = 10 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable** under RMS, even though the utilization test failed. This demonstrates that the Liu–Layland bound is a *sufficient* (conservative) condition, not a necessary one. Exact schedulability requires the response-time fixed-point analysis.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RM vs. EDF Comparison

| Criterion | Rate Monotonic (RM) | Earliest Deadline First (EDF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Priority assignment** | Static (design-time) | Dynamic (runtime) |
| **Optimality** | Optimal among static-priority | Optimal among all algorithms |
| **Maximum utilization** | $\ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$ | $100\%$ |
| **Implementation complexity** | Low (fixed priorities) | High (deadline tracking per task) |
| **Runtime overhead** | Negligible | Moderate (sorted deadline queue) |
| **Predictability** | Excellent (static analysis) | Good (dynamic analysis) |
| **Industry adoption** | AUTOSAR, OSEK/VDX, VxWorks | Research / avionics (limited) |
| **Schedulability test** | $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ or RTA | $U \leq 1.0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RTOS Architecture in a Cyber-Physical System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph physLayer["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        sensor1["TEMPERATURE SENSOR"]
        sensor2["PRESSURE SENSOR"]
        actuator1["MOTOR DRIVER"]
        actuator2["VALVE CONTROL"]
    end

    subgraph rtosKernel["RTOS KERNEL"]
        scheduler["PREEMPTIVE SCHEDULER"]
        task1["TASK A PERIOD 5MS"]
        task2["TASK B PERIOD 20MS"]
        task3["TASK C PERIOD 100MS"]
        isr["INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINES"]
        timer["HARDWARE TIMER TICK"]
    end

    subgraph appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        controlLoop["CONTROL ALGORITHM PID"]
        comms["UART CAN ETHERNET STACK"]
        safety["WATCHDOG SAFETY MONITOR"]
    end

    sensor1 --> isr
    sensor2 --> isr
    timer --> scheduler
    scheduler --> task1
    scheduler --> task2
    scheduler --> task3
    task1 --> controlLoop
    task2 --> comms
    task3 --> safety
    controlLoop --> actuator1
    comms --> actuator2
    safety --> scheduler
```

### 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready : Task Created
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by Higher Priority Task
    Running --> Blocked : Wait for Event or Resource
    Blocked --> Ready : Event Occurs or Resource Released
    Running --> [*] : Task Terminated
```

### 4.3 RMS Scheduling Timeline (Gantt Chart)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rate Monotonic Schedule for 3 Tasks over 60ms
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section High Priority TAU1
    TAU1 Instance 1 :a1, 0, 1
    TAU1 Instance 2 :a2, 4, 1
    TAU1 Instance 3 :a3, 8, 1
    section Mid Priority TAU2
    TAU2 Instance 1 :b1, 1, 2
    TAU2 Instance 2 :b2, 7, 2
    section Low Priority TAU3
    TAU3 Instance 1 :c1, 3, 3
    TAU3 Instance 2 :c2, 13, 3
```

### 4.4 Priority Inversion Scenario

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as HIGH PRIORITY TASK TAU1
    participant M as MEDIUM PRIORITY TASK TAU2
    participant L as LOW PRIORITY TASK TAU3
    participant R as SHARED RESOURCE

    L->>R: Lock Resource
    Note over L: LOW holds Resource
    H->>H: Preempts LOW
    H->>R: Request Resource
    Note over H: HIGH Blocks waiting
    M->>L: Preempts HIGH
    Note over M: MEDIUM runs and blocks HIGH
    L->>L: Inherits HIGH Priority via PIP
    L->>R: Release Resource
    M->>M: Preempts LOW again
    H->>R: Acquires Resource
    H->>H: Executes and Finishes
    L->>L: Resumes Original LOW Priority
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) intervention happens at the line marked *"Inherits HIGH Priority via PIP"*, breaking the inversion by elevating LOW above MEDIUM temporarily.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Hard Real-Time and Soft Real-Time Systems. Give one CPS example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|
| Deadline miss | System failure / safety hazard | Performance degradation only |
| Guarantee | Absolute, deterministic | Statistical, best-effort |
| Example | Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) in a car | Adaptive cruise control display update |

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition each, 0.5 mark example each, 0.5 mark distinguishing line — totaling 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Rate Monotonic Scheduling algorithm and write the Liu–Layland sufficient schedulability condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Algorithm (1.5 marks):** RMS is a *static-priority*, *preemptive* scheduling algorithm for periodic tasks. The task with the **shortest period** is assigned the **highest priority**. The task set is assumed to have $D_i = T_i$.
- **Sufficient Condition (1.5 marks):** A set of $n$ independent periodic tasks is schedulable under RMS if

$$
U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)
$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the three task states of an RTOS with a neat state transition diagram. Discuss how a preemptive scheduler performs a context switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 2 | 10 |
| $\tau_2$ | 3 | 15 |
| $\tau_3$ | 4 | 20 |

Apply **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**. Check schedulability using (i) the Liu–Layland utilization bound, and (ii) response-time analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Task States (7 marks):**

The three fundamental task states are:

1. **Running:** The task is currently executing on the CPU. Only *one* task per core can be in this state.
2. **Ready:** The task is eligible to execute but is waiting because the CPU is allocated to a higher-priority running task.
3. **Blocked (Waiting):** The task is suspended until a specific event occurs—such as I/O completion, semaphore acquisition, timer expiry, or message arrival.

**Transitions:**
- *Ready → Running:* The scheduler dispatches the task.
- *Running → Ready:* A higher-priority task preempts it.
- *Running → Blocked:* Task voluntarily yields (e.g., `taskDelay()`, semaphore wait).
- *Blocked → Ready:* The awaited event is signaled.

**Context Switch Mechanism (preemptive):** When a hardware interrupt or scheduler tick occurs while task $\tau_A$ is running:
1. The RTOS kernel saves $\tau_A$'s context (Program Counter, Stack Pointer, General-Purpose Registers, Status Register) onto its Task Control Block (TCB) in memory.
2. The scheduler selects the highest-priority *Ready* task $\tau_B$.
3. The kernel loads $\tau_B$'s context from its TCB into CPU registers.
4. Execution resumes at $\tau_B$'s last saved Program Counter.

The entire operation is **deterministic and bounded**—typically 1–10 microseconds in modern RTOS kernels like FreeRTOS.

---

**(b) Schedulability Test (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Priority assignment (RM rule):** Shortest period = highest priority.
- $\tau_1$ ($T=10$) → **Priority 1 (highest)**
- $\tau_2$ ($T=15$) → Priority 2
- $\tau_3$ ($T=20$) → **Priority 3 (lowest)**

**Step 2 — Utilization Test (3 marks):**

$$
U_1 = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20, \quad U_2 = \frac{3}{15} = 0.20, \quad U_3 = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20
$$

$$
U_{total} = 0.60
$$

Liu–Layland bound for $n = 3$:

$$
U_{bound} = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798
$$

Since $0.60 \leq 0.7798$, the **sufficient condition is satisfied**. The task set is *guaranteed* schedulable under RMS.

**Step 3 — Response-Time Analysis for $\tau_3$ (2 marks):**

$$
R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 4
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{4}{15} \right\rceil (3) = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{9}{15} \right\rceil (3) = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9
$$

Converged at $R_3 = 9$ ms. Since $R_3 = 9 \leq D_3 = 20$, the task set is **definitively schedulable**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The system is schedulable. In fact, the worst-case response time (9 ms) is comfortably below the deadline (20 ms), leaving 11 ms of slack.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** scheduling algorithm. Compare it with **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** on the basis of priority assignment, optimality, maximum utilization, and overhead. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Priority Inversion** problem with a clear sequence diagram. Describe how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves it. Mention the real-world case study where this caused a major failure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EDF vs. RMS (7 marks):**

**EDF (3.5 marks):**
- Dynamic-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm.
- At every scheduling decision point, the task whose **absolute deadline is closest** is given the CPU.
- Tasks are sorted in a *min-heap* keyed on deadline.
- **Optimality:** EDF is optimal for uniprocessor real-time scheduling—if *any* algorithm can schedule a task set, EDF can.
- **Utilization Bound:** $U \leq 1.0$ (i.e., up to 100% CPU utilization is achievable).

**RMS (1.5 marks):**
- Static-priority, preemptive algorithm (Liu & Layland, 1973).
- Priority inversely proportional to period: shortest period = highest priority.
- Optimal among *static-priority* algorithms only.
- Sufficient utilization bound: $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, asymptotic limit $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Criterion | RMS | EDF |
|---|---|---|
| Priority type | Static | Dynamic |
| Maximum utilization | 69.3% (asymptotic) | 100% |
| Implementation | Simple fixed priority | Sorted deadline queue (complex) |
| Runtime overhead | Low | Moderate |
| Industry use | AUTOSAR, automotive | Avionics, research |

**Conclusion:** RMS is preferred when simplicity and certifiability matter; EDF is preferred when CPU resources are constrained and dynamic adaptation is required.

---

**(b) Priority Inversion and PIP (7 marks):**

**Problem Definition (2 marks):** Priority Inversion occurs when a high-priority task is *indirectly* preempted by a lower-priority task via a shared resource and intervening medium-priority tasks. The high-priority task is *inverted* below medium-priority tasks in the effective execution order.

**Sequence (3 marks):**
1. Task $\tau_L$ (low priority) acquires shared Resource $R$.
2. Task $\tau_H$ (high priority) is released and preempts $\tau_L$.
3. $\tau_H$ requests Resource $R$, finds it locked by $\tau_L$, and **blocks**.
4. Task $\tau_M$ (medium priority) is released and preempts $\tau_L$ (since $\tau_L$ is still running).
5. $\tau_M$ runs to completion, indirectly blocking $\tau_H$ even though $\tau_M$ has lower priority.
6. $\tau_L$ resumes, finishes, releases $R$.
7. $\tau_H$ finally acquires $R$ and completes.

The *effective* duration of inversion is unbounded because it depends on how long $\tau_M$ runs.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) — Solution (1.5 marks):**
When $\tau_H$ blocks on $R$ held by $\tau_L$, the kernel **dynamically raises** $\tau_L$'s priority to that of $\tau_H$ for the duration of resource holding. This prevents $\tau_M$ from preempting $\tau_L$, bounding the inversion time to the duration of the *critical section* in $\tau_L$.

**Real-World Case Study (0.5 mark):** The **NASA Mars Pathfinder rover (1997)** experienced repeated system resets on Mars due to priority inversion in its VxWorks-based bus management task. The VxWorks `vport` semaphore was not initially configured with priority inheritance. Engineers remotely uploaded a patch enabling PIP, resolving the issue and saving the $327 million mission.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — RTOS Scheduling Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit check on $U$:** If $C_i = 2$ ms and $T_i = 10$ ms, $U_i = 0.2$, *not* 20%. Always normalize to a dimensionless ratio.
> 2. **Confusing sufficient with necessary conditions:** The Liu–Layland bound is *sufficient* (conservative). Failure does *not* mean unschedulable; you must follow up with response-time analysis.
> 3. **Wrong ceiling function application:** In RTA, $\lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil$ must be applied per *higher-priority* task iteration. Skipping the ceiling gives a non-integer and is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Wrong RMS rule:** Some students assign *longest period* = highest priority. This is the *inverse monotonic* rule, which is *not* RMS. Always shortest period = highest priority.
> 5. **Skipping the convergence check:** RTA requires the iteration to *terminate* (converge). If you stop after one iteration, you risk reporting a non-tight response time and losing 1 mark.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RTOS** = Determinism + Bounded Latency + Predictability, not just "fast."
- **Hard real-time** = deadline miss is catastrophic (e.g., ABS, pacemakers). **Soft real-time** = miss degrades quality (e.g., video streaming).
- **Three task states:** Running, Ready, Blocked. Transitions are deterministic.
- **RMS rule:** Shortest period → highest priority. Static, preemptive, optimal among static-priority algorithms.
- **Liu–Layland bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$, asymptotic $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. This is *sufficient*, not necessary.
- **EDF rule:** Closest absolute deadline → next to run. Dynamic, preemptive, optimal overall. Bound: $U \leq 1.0$.
- **Response-Time Analysis (RTA):** Fixed-point equation $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$. Task is schedulable iff $R_i \leq D_i$.
- **Priority Inversion:** Indirect preemption via shared resources. **PIP** is the standard fix; **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** is the canonical case study.
- **CPS context:** RTOS is the *temporal brain* of any cyber-physical system—missed deadlines in control loops lead to instability, oscillation, or physical damage.
- **Common KTU trick:** Give a task set with $U_{total} > 0.693$. The student must recognize the bound fails but perform RTA to prove schedulability (showing understanding beyond the bound).
- **Engineering tip:** In practice, RTOS designers target $U_{total} \approx 0.6$ to leave headroom for interrupts, ISRs, and system overhead.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Control Techniques and Security in CPS: Advanced Control Strategies (Adaptive Control, Model Predictive Control)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advanced Control Techniques and Security in CPS: Advanced Control Strategies (Adaptive Control, Model Predictive Control)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
