## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Advanced Control in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)?

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** integrates computation, networking, and physical processes. The controllers inside these systems are rarely static — they must handle **time-varying dynamics, disturbances, and model uncertainty**. Classical PID or fixed-gain controllers fall short here, which leads us to **Advanced Control Strategies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *Advanced Control Strategies* are control methodologies that dynamically modify their own parameters or optimize future behavior to handle non-linear, time-varying, or partially known systems. The two principal families are **Adaptive Control** and **Model Predictive Control (MPC)**.

### 1.2 Adaptive Control — Intuitive Overview

**Formal Definition:**
> **Adaptive Control** is a class of control techniques in which the controller parameters are automatically adjusted in real time to compensate for variations in plant dynamics or the environment, ensuring consistent closed-loop performance.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Self-Learning Driver:**

Imagine a person learning to drive a car. Initially, they know nothing about that specific car — its braking distance, steering sensitivity, or acceleration response. As they drive, they **continuously observe** the car's response to their inputs and **modify** their driving style (gentler braking, smoother steering) to keep the ride safe and comfortable. They are *adapting* their control behavior to a poorly known, changing plant.

- The **driver's brain** = the adaptive algorithm
- The **car** = the physical plant (the CPS)
- The **sensory feedback** (speed, road feel) = measurement/observation
- The **adjustment of driving style** = parameter update law

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adaptive Control in CPS?** Cyber-Physical Systems (autonomous vehicles, smart grids, robotic manipulators) operate across wide operating envelopes where a single set of controller gains cannot guarantee stability or performance. Adaptive control guarantees robust performance under parameter drift.

#### 1.2.1 Main Variants of Adaptive Control

| Variant | Full Name | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **MRAC** | Model Reference Adaptive Control | Force plant to behave like a *reference model* |
| **STR** | Self-Tuning Regulator | Online system identification + controller redesign |
| **GS** | Gain Scheduling | Pre-computed gain table indexed by operating point |

### 1.3 Model Predictive Control (MPC) — Intuitive Overview

**Formal Definition:**
> **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** is an advanced control strategy that uses an explicit dynamic model of the plant to **predict future behavior** over a finite horizon, then computes the control action by solving a constrained **optimization problem** at every sampling instant, applying only the first element of the optimal sequence (receding horizon principle).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Chess Grandmaster:**

A chess grandmaster does not plan a single move and commit. At every turn, they:
1. **Predict** the next several moves (for both sides) using knowledge of the game.
2. **Optimize** the move sequence that maximizes their advantage.
3. **Execute** only the *first* move of that optimal plan.
4. After the opponent's response, **re-plan** from the new state.

The grandmaster's "mental simulation" is the **prediction model**; the look-ahead is the **prediction horizon $N_p$**; the optimization is the **cost function $J$**; and the re-planning cycle is the **receding horizon**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MPC Dominates Modern CPS?** Modern CPS must respect **physical constraints** (valve saturation, battery current limits, lane boundaries). MPC handles these **constraints systematically** by embedding them in the optimization — classical control cannot.

### 1.4 Adaptive vs. MPC — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Control** adjusts *how the controller behaves* when the plant changes. **MPC** adjusts *what the controller does right now* by predicting the future under hard constraints. They are **complementary**, not competing — modern CPS often combine both (Adaptive MPC).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Adaptive Control — Operational Architecture

An adaptive controller augments a **standard feedback loop** with a second loop — the **parameter adaptation loop**.

#### 2.1.1 Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Define the Plant**
The unknown plant is modelled as a first-order system:
$$\dot{y}_p = -a_p \, y_p + b_p \, u$$

where $a_p, b_p$ are **unknown** (or slowly time-varying) plant parameters, $u$ is the control input, and $y_p$ is the plant output.

**Step 2 — Define the Reference Model**
A stable, well-damped reference model is chosen to specify the *desired* closed-loop behavior:
$$\dot{y}_m = -a_m \, y_m + b_m \, r$$

where $r$ is the reference command, and $a_m, b_m$ are **known** and chosen by the designer.

**Step 3 — Define the Tracking Error**
$$e = y_p - y_m$$

The adaptation law drives $e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

**Step 4 — Choose the Control Law**
A linear-in-parameters control law is selected:
$$u = \theta_1 \, r - \theta_2 \, y_p$$

where $\theta_1, \theta_2$ are the **adjustable controller parameters**.

**Step 5 — Derive the Error Dynamics**
Substituting and simplifying yields:
$$\dot{e} = -a_m \, e + \tilde{\theta}^T \phi$$

where:
- $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \theta^*$ is the **parameter error**
- $\phi = [r, \; y_p]^T$ is the **regressor vector**

**Step 6 — Apply the MIT Rule (Gradient Descent on a Cost)**
Define the cost $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^2$. The MIT rule updates parameters as:
$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

Using the **sensitivity derivative** $\partial e / \partial \theta \approx \phi$, the **MIT adaptation law** becomes:
$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \phi$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lyapunov-based MRAC** is more rigorous and guarantees stability. The MIT rule is intuitive but requires *small* adaptation gain $\gamma$ for stability.

#### 2.1.2 Self-Tuning Regulator (STR) — Operational Architecture

**Step 1 — Recursive Identification**
The plant parameters are estimated online using **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)**:
$$\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \left[ y(t) - \phi^T(t) \hat{\theta}(t-1) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Controller Redesign**
At every step, the controller gains are recomputed using the current $\hat{\theta}$ via a pole-placement, LQR, or minimum-variance law.

**Step 3 — Certainty Equivalence Principle**
The re-designed controller is applied to the plant as though $\hat{\theta}$ were the true parameter.

### 2.2 Model Predictive Control (MPC) — Operational Architecture

#### 2.2.1 The Three Pillars of MPC

**Pillar 1 — Prediction Model**
A discrete-time state-space model is used:
$$x(k+1) = A \, x(k) + B \, u(k)$$
$$y(k) = C \, x(k)$$

**Pillar 2 — Cost Function (Optimization Objective)**
The standard quadratic cost over a **prediction horizon $N_p$** and **control horizon $N_c$**:
$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \left[ \hat{y}(k+i \vert k) - r(k+i) \right]^T Q \left[ \hat{y}(k+i \vert k) - r(k+i) \right] + \sum_{i=0}^{N_c - 1} \Delta u(k+i)^T R \, \Delta u(k+i)$$

where:
- $\hat{y}(k+i \vert k)$ = predicted output at step $k+i$, given information up to step $k$
- $r(k+i)$ = reference trajectory
- $Q$ = output tracking weight (positive semi-definite)
- $R$ = control effort weight (positive definite)
- $\Delta u(k+i) = u(k+i) - u(k+i-1)$ = control increment

**Pillar 3 — Constraints**
$$\begin{aligned}
u_{\min} &\le u(k+i) \le u_{\max}, \quad i = 0, \dots, N_c - 1 \\
\Delta u_{\min} &\le \Delta u(k+i) \le \Delta u_{\max} \\
y_{\min} &\le \hat{y}(k+i) \le y_{\max}, \quad i = 1, \dots, N_p
\end{aligned}$$

#### 2.2.2 The Receding Horizon Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

**At every sampling instant $k$:**

1. **Measure** the current state $x(k)$.
2. **Predict** future states over the horizon $[k+1, k+N_p]$ using the model.
3. **Solve** the constrained QP:
$$U^*(k) = \arg\min_{U} \; J(x(k), U)$$
4. **Apply** only the first element $u^*(k) = U^*(k)[0]$.
5. **Shift** horizon forward: $k \leftarrow k+1$ and **repeat**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Receding Horizon = Move Blocking.** Even though we solve for $N_c$ future moves, we execute only the first. This provides **implicit feedback** against unmeasured disturbances.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Plant (1st order)** | $\dot{y}_p = -a_p y_p + b_p u$ | Unknown plant dynamics |
| **Reference model** | $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$ | Desired response |
| **Tracking error** | $e = y_p - y_m$ | Drives adaptation |
| **MIT rule** | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \phi$ | Gradient-descent update |
| **Lyapunov MRAC** | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e B \phi$ | Stability-guaranteed update |
| **RLS identifier** | $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t{-}1) + K(t)[y(t) - \phi^T(t) \hat{\theta}(t{-}1)]$ | Online parameter estimation |
| **MPC prediction** | $x(k{+}1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ | Discrete state update |
| **MPC cost** | $J = \sum \vert \hat{y} - r \vert_Q^2 + \sum \vert \Delta u \vert_R^2$ | Quadratic objective |
| **Prediction horizon** | $N_p$ | Number of future steps predicted |
| **Control horizon** | $N_c$ | Number of control moves optimized |
| **Receding horizon** | Apply $u^*(k)$, replan at $k{+}1$ | Implicit feedback |
| **Stability margin** | $N_p \to \infty$ | Guarantees stability (infeasible) |
| **Terminal constraint** | $x(k{+}N_p) = 0$ | Finite-horizon stability trick |
| **Terminal cost** | $x(k{+}N_p)^T P x(k{+}N_p)$ | Lyapunov function surrogate |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in CPS Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial CPS:** MPC dominates **process control** (oil refineries, chemical plants) because it natively respects actuator saturation, temperature limits, and pressure bounds.
>
> **Autonomous Vehicles:** Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) and lane-keeping use **MPC** for trajectory optimization and **MRAC** for handling vehicle mass variation (passengers, cargo).
>
> **Smart Grids:** Adaptive MPC balances renewable intermittency, forecasting solar/wind output and dynamically re-planning dispatch.
>
> **Robotic Surgery:** Adaptive control compensates for tissue compliance variation; MPC enforces force/torque safety limits.
>
> **Aerospace:** MRAC handles aircraft with **battle damage or fuel-mass variation**; MPC enforces actuator and structural-load constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the MRAC Control Law (MIT Rule)

**Given:**
- Plant: $\dot{y}_p = -a_p y_p + b_p u$, with $a_p, b_p$ unknown.
- Reference model: $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$, with $a_m, b_m$ known.
- Control law: $u = \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y_p$.
- Tracking error: $e = y_p - y_m$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Substitute the Control Law into the Plant Equation.**
The plant dynamics become:
$$\dot{y}_p = -a_p y_p + b_p (\theta_1 r - \theta_2 y_p)$$

Rearranging:
$$\dot{y}_p = -(a_p + b_p \theta_2) y_p + b_p \theta_1 r$$

**Step 2 — Express the Error Dynamics.**
Take the time-derivative of $e = y_p - y_m$:
$$\dot{e} = \dot{y}_p - \dot{y}_m$$

Substitute the expressions:
$$\dot{e} = -(a_p + b_p \theta_2) y_p + b_p \theta_1 r - (-a_m y_m + b_m r)$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Ideal Parameter Vector $\theta^*$.**
We want the closed-loop plant to match the reference model. This requires:
$$\begin{aligned}
a_p + b_p \theta_2^* &= a_m \\
b_p \theta_1^* &= b_m
\end{aligned}$$

Solving:
$$\theta_1^* = \frac{b_m}{b_p}, \quad \theta_2^* = \frac{a_m - a_p}{b_p}$$

**Step 4 — Express $\dot{e}$ in Terms of $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \theta^*$.**
Subtracting and simplifying (with significant algebra):
$$\dot{e} = -a_m e + b_p \, \tilde{\theta}^T \phi$$

where:
- $\tilde{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_1 - \theta_1^* \\ \theta_2 - \theta_2^* \end{bmatrix}$
- $\phi = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ -y_p \end{bmatrix}$

This is the **error dynamics** of MRAC.

**Step 5 — Apply Gradient Descent on $J = \tfrac{1}{2} e^2$.**
The MIT rule is:
$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Sensitivity Derivatives $\partial e / \partial \theta$.**
From the error dynamics in Step 4 (treating $\tilde{\theta} \approx 0$ at convergence):
$$\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_1} \approx \frac{b_p \, r}{s + a_m}, \quad \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_2} \approx \frac{-b_p \, y_p}{s + a_m}$$

**Step 7 — State the MIT Rule (Simplified).**
For slowly varying parameters, the filter dynamics are ignored, giving:
$$\dot{\theta}_1 = -\gamma \, e \, r, \quad \dot{\theta}_2 = \gamma \, e \, y_p$$

Or in vector form:
$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \phi$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Caveat:** The MIT rule is *not* guaranteed to be stable for large $\gamma$. The **Lyapunov-based MRAC** replaces $\gamma e \phi$ with $\gamma e B \phi$ and uses a Lyapunov function $V = e^2/2 + \tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{\theta}/2$ to prove $\dot{V} \le 0$.

### 3.2 Full Derivation of the Unconstrained MPC Law

**Given:**
- Discrete state-space model: $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$.
- Output: $y(k) = C x(k)$.
- Cost function:
$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \left[ y(k+i) - r(k+i) \right]^2 + \lambda \sum_{i=0}^{N_c - 1} \left[ \Delta u(k+i) \right]^2$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Compact the Future Predictions.**
Iterating the model forward from $x(k)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
x(k+1) &= A x(k) + B u(k) \\
x(k+2) &= A^2 x(k) + A B u(k) + B u(k+1) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
x(k+N_p) &= A^{N_p} x(k) + \sum_{j=0}^{N_p - 1} A^{N_p - 1 - j} B u(k+j)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Stack into Vector Form.**
Define:
- $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x(k+1) \\ x(k+2) \\ \vdots \\ x(k+N_p) \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} u(k) \\ u(k+1) \\ \vdots \\ u(k+N_c-1) \end{bmatrix}$

Then:
$$\mathbf{x} = \mathcal{A} x(k) + \mathcal{B} \mathbf{u}$$

where:
$$\mathcal{A} = \begin{bmatrix} A \\ A^2 \\ \vdots \\ A^{N_p} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathcal{B} = \begin{bmatrix} B & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ A B & B & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ A^{N_p-1} B & A^{N_p-2} B & \cdots & A^{N_p - N_c} B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Express Output Predictions.**
$$\mathbf{y} = \mathcal{C} \mathbf{x} = \mathcal{C} \mathcal{A} x(k) + \mathcal{C} \mathcal{B} \mathbf{u}$$

where $\mathcal{C} = \text{blockdiag}(C, C, \dots, C)$.

**Step 4 — Write the Cost Function in Vector Form.**
Let $\mathbf{r} = [r(k+1), \dots, r(k+N_p)]^T$. Then:
$$J = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{r})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{r}) + \lambda \, \Delta \mathbf{u}^T \Delta \mathbf{u}$$

Substituting $\mathbf{y}$:
$$J = (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{A} x(k) + \mathcal{C} \mathcal{B} \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{r})^T (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{A} x(k) + \mathcal{C} \mathcal{B} \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{r}) + \lambda \, \Delta \mathbf{u}^T \Delta \mathbf{u}$$

**Step 5 — Convert $\Delta u$ to $u$ via the Difference Matrix $\mathcal{D}$.**
Define $\Delta \mathbf{u} = \mathcal{D} \mathbf{u} + d$, where $\mathcal{D}$ is the lower-triangular difference matrix. The exact form:
$$\Delta \mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} u(k) - u(k-1) \\ u(k+1) - u(k) \\ \vdots \\ u(k+N_c-1) - u(k+N_c-2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Take the Gradient $\partial J / \partial \mathbf{u} = 0$.**
Setting the gradient to zero yields the **unconstrained optimal control sequence**:
$$\mathbf{u}^* = - \left( (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{B})^T (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{B}) + \lambda \mathcal{D}^T \mathcal{D} \right)^{-1} \left[ (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{B})^T (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{A} x(k) - \mathbf{r}) + \lambda \mathcal{D}^T d \right]$$

**Step 7 — Apply Only the First Element.**
The first element of $\mathbf{u}^*$ is the control applied to the plant:
$$u^*(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{u}^*$$

This is the **receding horizon** principle: solve, then move.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constrained MPC** uses a Quadratic Programming (QP) solver (active set, interior point) to enforce inequality constraints. The KKT conditions replace the unconstrained gradient condition.

### 3.3 Stability Analysis of MPC (Lyapunov Argument)

Define the optimal cost at time $k$ as $J^*(k)$. After applying $u^*(k)$ and the system moves to $x(k+1)$:
$$J^*(k+1) \le J^*(k) - \ell(x(k), u^*(k))$$

where $\ell$ is the stage cost. This monotonic decrease of $J^*$ serves as a **Lyapunov function**, proving asymptotic stability (under terminal constraint or terminal cost conditions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** MPC is **inherently stable** *only* with sufficient horizon, terminal cost $P$, or terminal constraint $x(k+N_p) \in \mathcal{X}_f$. Without these, only **feasibility** is guaranteed, not stability.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Adaptive Control (MRAC) | Model Predictive Control (MPC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Mechanism** | Parameter update law | Online optimization |
| **Handles Uncertainty** | Plant parameter drift | Disturbances, constraints |
| **Handles Constraints** | No (requires anti-windup) | Yes (native) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (ODE update) | High (QP at every step) |
| **Horizon** | Infinite (continuous) | Finite $N_p$ |
| **Stability Proof** | Lyapunov | Lyapunov (with terminal set) |
| **Implementation in CPS** | Embedded MCU/PLC | Industrial PC / cloud |
| **Tuning Parameters** | Adaptation gain $\gamma$ | $N_p, N_c, Q, R$ |
| **Real-time Suitability** | Excellent | Moderate (ms scale) |
| **Best Use Case** | Unknown parameter systems | Constrained multivariable systems |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Distinguish between Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) and Self-Tuning Regulators (STR). Which one is more suitable for a CPS with rapidly time-varying plant parameters, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **MRAC (1 Mark):** Adjusts controller parameters directly to force the plant to track a reference model's output. Uses an adaptation law (MIT or Lyapunov) driven by the tracking error $e = y_p - y_m$.
- **STR (1 Mark):** Performs online system identification (typically RLS) to estimate plant parameters, then re-designs controller gains (pole-placement or LQR) at every step using the certainty equivalence principle.
- **Suitability (1 Mark):** **STR is more suitable for rapidly time-varying plants** because RLS actively tracks parameter changes via the innovation term $y(t) - \phi^T(t)\hat{\theta}(t-1)$, whereas MRAC relies on slow parameter drift assumptions. However, MRAC is computationally lighter.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Explain the concept of the "receding horizon" in Model Predictive Control. Why is only the first element of the optimal control sequence applied to the plant?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Receding Horizon Definition (1 Mark):** At each sampling instant $k$, MPC solves a finite-horizon optimization over $[k+1, k+N_p]$ and applies only the first control move $u^*(k)$. At $k+1$, the horizon is shifted forward and the optimization is re-solved with the new measurement.
- **Why only the first element (2 Marks):**
  1. **Implicit feedback:** Applying only $u^*(k)$ and replanning incorporates new measurements, providing robustness against unmodeled disturbances and model-plant mismatch.
  2. **Stability:** The receding horizon implements a closed-loop policy; the open-loop plan beyond $k$ is discarded because the true plant response will diverge from predictions due to disturbances.
  3. **Computational tractability:** It avoids long open-loop commitment that would amplify prediction errors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice (either-or). Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each: (a) theory/derivation, (b) numerical/analytical.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)**. Derive the control law and the **MIT adaptation law** for a first-order plant.

**(b)** Consider a first-order plant with nominal parameters $a_p = 2$, $b_p = 1$. The reference model is chosen as $a_m = 5$, $b_m = 2$. The reference input is $r(t) = \sin(t)$. **(i)** Compute the ideal controller parameters $\theta_1^*$ and $\theta_2^*$. **(ii)** If the adaptation law is $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \phi$ with $\gamma = 0.5$, write the closed-loop update equations. **(iii)** Comment on the stability of the MIT rule for large $\gamma$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Block diagram already shown in Section 4.1. Key derivation (already in Section 3.1):

- Plant: $\dot{y}_p = -a_p y_p + b_p u$
- Reference model: $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$
- Tracking error: $e = y_p - y_m$
- Control law: $u = \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y_p$
- Error dynamics: $\dot{e} = -a_m e + b_p \tilde{\theta}^T \phi$
- MIT rule: $\dot{\theta}_1 = -\gamma e r$, $\dot{\theta}_2 = \gamma e y_p$ (sign depends on $\phi$ sign convention)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Ideal parameters (3 Marks):**
From the matching conditions in Section 3.1, Step 3:
$$\theta_1^* = \frac{b_m}{b_p} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$
$$\theta_2^* = \frac{a_m - a_p}{b_p} = \frac{5 - 2}{1} = 3$$

**(ii) Closed-loop update equations (2 Marks):**
With $\gamma = 0.5$ and $\phi = [r, -y_p]^T$:
$$\dot{\theta}_1 = -0.5 \, e \cdot r = -0.5 \, e \cdot \sin(t)$$
$$\dot{\theta}_2 = -0.5 \, e \cdot (-y_p) = 0.5 \, e \, y_p$$

The closed-loop error dynamics:
$$\dot{e} = -5 e + \tilde{\theta}_1 \sin(t) - \tilde{\theta}_2 y_p$$

**(iii) Stability comment (2 Marks):**
> [!WARNING]
> **MIT Rule Stability Caveat:** The MIT rule is stable only for *small* $\gamma$ and *slowly varying* parameters. For large $\gamma$, the closed-loop can become unstable because the parameter update introduces a positive feedback loop through the regressor $\phi$. Lyapunov-based MRAC provides guaranteed stability by using $\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 \le 0$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **three pillars of Model Predictive Control**: prediction model, cost function, and receding horizon. Derive the unconstrained MPC control law for a discrete state-space system.

**(b)** Consider a discrete system $x(k+1) = x(k) + 0.1 u(k)$, $y(k) = x(k)$. The reference is $r = 1$, and the cost function is $J = \sum_{i=1}^{3} (y(k+i) - r)^2 + 0.1 \sum_{i=0}^{1} u(k+i)^2$. **(i)** Compute the matrices $\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}$. **(ii)** Solve for the optimal $u^*(k)$ assuming $x(k) = 0.5$. **(iii)** What is the steady-state value of $u^*$ if the horizon is infinite?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **Three pillars (3 Marks):** (1) Explicit model to predict future outputs; (2) Quadratic cost function penalizing tracking error and control effort; (3) Receding horizon where only the first move is applied and the horizon shifts.
- **Unconstrained MPC derivation (4 Marks):** As derived in Section 3.2. The optimal control sequence is:
$$\mathbf{u}^* = -\left( (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{B})^T (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{B}) + \lambda \mathcal{D}^T \mathcal{D} \right)^{-1} \left[ (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{B})^T (\mathcal{C} \mathcal{A} x(k) - \mathbf{r}) \right]$$
(For unconstrained with $\Delta u$ formulation, the structure follows from the gradient of $J$).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Matrices (2 Marks):**
With $A = 1$, $B = 0.1$, $C = 1$, $N_p = 3$, $N_c = 2$:
$$\mathcal{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathcal{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(ii) Optimal control (3 Marks):**
With $x(0) = 0.5$, $r = 1$, $Q = 1$, $R = 0.1$:
Predicted outputs:
$$y(1) = 1 \cdot 0.5 + 0.1 \cdot u(0) = 0.5 + 0.1 u(0)$$
$$y(2) = 0.5 + 0.1 u(0) + 0.1 u(1)$$
$$y(3) = 0.5 + 0.1 u(0) + 0.1 u(1)$$

Cost:
$$J = (0.5 + 0.1 u_0 - 1)^2 + (0.5 + 0.1 u_0 + 0.1 u_1 - 1)^2 + (0.5 + 0.1 u_0 + 0.1 u_1 - 1)^2 + 0.1 u_0^2 + 0.1 u_1^2$$

Setting $\partial J / \partial u_0 = 0$ and $\partial J / \partial u_1 = 0$ (algebra omitted for brevity but should be shown in exam), one obtains approximately:
$$u_0^* \approx 2.3, \quad u_1^* \approx 2.3$$

The first move $u^*(k) \approx 2.3$ is applied.

**(iii) Steady-state infinite horizon (2 Marks):**
For $N_p \to \infty$, the steady-state $u_{ss}$ satisfies $y_{ss} = r$. From $y_{ss} = x_{ss}$ and $x_{ss} = x_{ss} + 0.1 u_{ss}$ (steady state), this gives $u_{ss} = 0$? Actually, for a stable integrator, the steady state requires $u_{ss} = 0$ if $A = 1$ (marginal), but with regulation cost, $u_{ss}$ converges to the value that maintains $y = r$. The state grows: $x(k) = x(0) + 0.1 k u$. For $y = 1$ asymptotically, the control must decay to zero as the state approaches 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (MPC):** Students commonly:
> 1. **Forget the receding horizon principle** — applying the *entire* sequence $\mathbf{u}^*$ instead of just the first element. This loses implicit feedback.
> 2. **Ignore the terminal constraint/cost** — leading to instability for short horizons.
> 3. **Mix up $N_p$ and $N_c$** — $N_p$ is the *prediction* horizon (how far we look), $N_c$ is the *control* horizon (how many moves we optimize).
> 4. **Confuse $Q$ and $R$** — $Q$ penalizes tracking error (larger $Q$ = faster response), $R$ penalizes control effort (larger $R$ = smoother control).
> 5. **For MRAC:** Students often write $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e$ without the regressor $\phi$, which is dimensionally and structurally wrong.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Advanced Control Strategies**

**Adaptive Control Essentials:**
- MRAC forces the plant to mimic a reference model; STR identifies and re-designs online.
- The MIT rule $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \phi$ is simple but not stability-guaranteed; Lyapunov MRAC is rigorous.
- Ideal parameters for 1st-order plant: $\theta_1^* = b_m/b_p$, $\theta_2^* = (a_m - a_p)/b_p$.
- RLS update: $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t)[y(t) - \phi^T(t)\hat{\theta}(t-1)]$.
- Gain scheduling uses pre-computed lookup tables — not "true" adaptation.

**Model Predictive Control Essentials:**
- MPC requires: (1) prediction model, (2) cost function, (3) constraints, (4) receding horizon.
- The cost function is quadratic: $J = \sum \vert \hat{y} - r \vert_Q^2 + \sum \vert \Delta u \vert_R^2$.
- Solve a QP at every step; apply only $u^*(k)$ — the first element of the optimal sequence.
- $N_p$ = prediction horizon, $N_c$ = control horizon ($N_c \le N_p$).
- Stability requires: terminal cost $P > 0$ OR terminal constraint $x(k+N_p) \in \mathcal{X}_f$.
- $Q$ large = aggressive tracking; $R$ large = conservative control.
- Constraints: $u_{\min} \le u \le u_{\max}$, $y_{\min} \le y \le y_{\max}$, $\Delta u$ rate limits.

**CPS-Specific Points:**
- Adaptive control handles **uncertainty**; MPC handles **constraints** — they are complementary.
- Adaptive MPC combines both: an adaptation law updates the model $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$ used by the MPC.
- Real-time CPS implementations require **fast QP solvers** (active set, interior point, ADMM).
- Latency and jitter in the cyber side of CPS can destabilize both adaptive and predictive controllers — time-stamping and network-aware control are active research areas.

**Common Exam Keywords to Use:**
- "Receding horizon", "Certainty equivalence", "Lyapunov stability", "Quadratic programming", "Prediction model", "MIT rule", "Sensitivity derivative", "Tracking error", "Terminal set", "Feasibility region", "Replanning".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Security Challenges in CPS?

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system that tightly integrates sensing, computation, communication, and physical actuation. Because CPS bridges the cyber (computation/network) and physical (sensors/actuators) worlds, its attack surface is fundamentally wider than that of a pure Information Technology (IT) system. **Security Challenges in CPS** therefore refer to the unique set of threats, vulnerabilities, and countermeasures that arise when malicious actors can manipulate not just data but also the physical environment — potentially causing loss of life, environmental damage, or economic disaster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCPT303 – Module 3):**
> *Security Challenges in CPS* are the confidentiality, integrity, authentication, availability, and safety issues arising from the convergence of networked computation and physical processes, requiring specialized primitives (authentication, encryption, key management) that are lightweight, real-time capable, and resilient to physical-layer attacks.

### The CPS Security Triad (Expanded CIA + S)

| Property | Meaning in IT Systems | Special Twist in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Keep data secret | Sensor streams (health, location) reveal private physical behaviour |
| **Integrity** | Prevent data tampering | Tampered actuator commands can cause physical destruction |
| **Authentication** | Verify user identity | Must also verify **device**, **firmware**, and **sensor** identity |
| **Availability** | Keep service running | Denial-of-service can stall safety-critical control loops |
| **Safety** | Not a classical IT goal | Becomes the *top priority* — failure can kill people |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine a smart insulin pump. If only confidentiality were breached, an attacker could read your sugar levels — embarrassing but harmless. If authentication is bypassed and an attacker sends forged commands telling the pump to deliver a fatal dose, the *physical* world is now compromised. CPS security is therefore less about hiding data and more about **preventing physical harm through forged cyber input**.

---

## 1.2 Authentication in CPS

**Authentication** is the process of *proving the claimed identity* of a communicating entity (user, device, sensor node, actuator, or software component) before any trust is granted.

In CPS, authentication is **not a single act** — it is layered:

1. **User-to-Device Authentication** — Who is operating this controller? (e.g., surgeon logging into a robotic surgical system).
2. **Device-to-Device Authentication** — Is this temperature sensor actually the one deployed in Reactor B-12? (prevents node-spoofing).
3. **Firmware/Software Authentication** — Has the code running on this PLC been tampered with? (verified via secure boot and code signing).
4. **Data Authentication** — Was this measurement stream actually produced by the claimed sensor? (achieved via MACs or digital signatures).

### Authentication Factors (the "something" the prover shows)

| Factor | Type | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Something you know** | Knowledge | Password to log into SCADA HMI |
| **Something you have** | Possession | Hardware token, smart card, TPM chip |
| **Something you are** | Biometric | Fingerprint on a medical device |
| **Something you do** | Behavioural | Stylometry on operator console input |
| **Something inherent to the device** | CPS-specific | PUFs (Physical Unclonable Functions), device fingerprint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** CPS authentication must be **lightweight** (low RAM, low CPU, low energy), **real-time** (within milliseconds for control loops), and **resilient to physical capture** (an attacker who steals a node must not be able to impersonate the whole network).

---

## 1.3 Encryption in CPS

**Encryption** is the cryptographic transformation of plaintext into ciphertext using a mathematical algorithm (cipher) and a secret value (key), so that only entities holding the correct key can recover the original plaintext.

Encryption addresses the **confidentiality** and, indirectly, the **integrity** aspects of CPS security.

### Two Fundamental Families

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Locked Boxes:**
> - **Symmetric encryption** is like a house where the *same physical key* locks and unlocks the front door. Both sender and receiver need identical copies of the key. Fast, but key distribution is a nightmare at scale.
> - **Asymmetric encryption** is like a mailbox with a slot — anyone can drop a letter in using the *public* address, but only the owner with the *private* key can open it and read. Slower, but solves the key-distribution problem elegantly.

### Symmetric Encryption (Single-Key / Secret-Key)

The **same secret key $K$** is used for both encryption and decryption:

$$C = E_K(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_K(C)$$

CPS-relevant symmetric algorithms:
- **AES-128 / AES-256** — the de-facto standard; hardware-accelerated on most modern MCUs.
- **ChaCha20** — software-friendly, constant-time, used in TLS 1.3.
- **PRESENT** and **Simon/Speck** — ultra-lightweight block ciphers designed for IoT/IIoT nodes.

### Asymmetric Encryption (Public-Key / Two-Key)

A **key pair** $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$ is used:

$$C = E_{K_{pub}}(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$$

CPS-relevant asymmetric schemes:
- **RSA** — based on the hardness of integer factorisation.
- **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)** — equivalent security at much smaller key sizes; ideal for constrained CPS nodes.
- **Pairing-based cryptography** — used in advanced CPS/IoT protocols (e.g., attribute-based encryption for smart grids).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Symmetric encryption is used for **bulk data protection** (sensor streams, control commands) because it is 100–1000× faster. Asymmetric encryption is used for **key exchange and digital signatures** because it solves the trust bootstrapping problem. Real CPS protocols (TLS 1.3, DTLS, IKEv2) combine **both** — this is called a *hybrid cryptosystem*.

---

## 1.4 Why CPS Security Is *Harder* Than Classical IT Security

| Classical IT Problem | CPS-Specific Amplification |
|---|---|
| Computing power assumed plentiful | Nodes are resource-constrained (8-bit MCUs, kB of RAM) |
| Network assumed reliable | Wireless links are noisy, intermittent |
| Updates are routine | Firmware OTA updates can brick safety-critical devices |
| Attackers target data | Attackers target **physical processes** |
| Time-scale: hours/days OK | Control loops demand **sub-millisecond** response |
| Trust model: server-client | Trust model: **many-to-many, multi-domain** |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Authentication Pipeline in CPS

Authentication in a CPS context is performed in five logical stages. Every stage maps to a specific cryptographic primitive.

### Stage 1: Identity Establishment
Each device $D_i$ is provisioned with a **unique identity** $ID_i$ at manufacturing time, often bound to a hardware root-of-trust (TPM, secure element, or PUF).

### Stage 2: Credential Generation
A cryptographic credential is derived. For symmetric schemes:
$$C_i = H(ID_i \oplus K_{master})$$
where $H(\cdot)$ is a cryptographic hash function and $K_{master}$ is a network-wide master key.

### Stage 3: Challenge–Response Exchange
The verifier sends a **nonce** $N$ (a random number used once). The prover computes:
$$\text{Response} = f_{K_i}(N)$$
where $f_{K_i}$ may be an HMAC, an AES-CBC-MAC, or a digital-signature function.

### Stage 4: Verification
The verifier, knowing the expected function and key, recomputes the expected response and checks equality:
$$\text{Auth} \iff f_{K_i}(N) = \text{Response}$$

### Stage 5: Session Key Derivation
Upon successful authentication, both parties derive a fresh **session key** $K_s$ for bulk encryption:
$$K_s = KDF(K_i \parallel N \parallel ID_A \parallel ID_B)$$
using a Key Derivation Function (KDF) such as HKDF or PBKDF2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *authentication* with *encryption*. **Authentication = identity proof**, **Encryption = data confidentiality**. A message can be authenticated without being encrypted (e.g., broadcast beacons), and encrypted without being authenticated (insecure — vulnerable to ciphertext malleability).

---

## 2.2 Symmetric Encryption — Block Cipher Operating Modes

A block cipher encrypts fixed-size blocks (AES: **128 bits**). To encrypt a long message, an **operating mode** is chosen.

### ECB — Electronic Codebook (INSECURE — do not use in CPS)
$$C_j = E_K(P_j)$$
Each block encrypted independently. Patterns in plaintext leak through.

### CBC — Cipher Block Chaining
$$C_j = E_K(P_j \oplus C_{j-1}), \quad C_0 = IV$$
Each ciphertext block depends on all previous blocks. **Requires an unpredictable IV.**

### CTR — Counter Mode (most common in modern CPS/TLS)
$$C_j = P_j \oplus E_K(\text{counter}_j)$$
Turns a block cipher into a stream cipher. **Parallelisable**, no padding needed.

### GCM — Galois/Counter Mode (AEAD)
Combines CTR encryption with a **GHASH** authenticator:
$$\text{Tag} = \text{GHASH}_{H}(AAD, C)$$
Provides **Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data** — both confidentiality AND integrity in one primitive. **Default choice in TLS 1.3, IPsec, modern CPS protocols.**

---

## 2.3 Asymmetric Encryption — The Math Behind RSA

The security of RSA rests on the **computational hardness of factoring the product of two large primes**.

### RSA Key Generation (Step-by-Step)

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Choose two large random primes $p$ and $q$ (each $\geq 1024$ bits) |
| 2 | Compute modulus $n = p \cdot q$ |
| 3 | Compute Euler totient $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ |
| 4 | Choose public exponent $e$ with $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ |
| 5 | Compute private exponent $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| 6 | Public key $K_{pub} = (n, e)$, Private key $K_{priv} = (n, d)$ |

### RSA Encryption / Decryption
$$C = P^e \bmod n$$
$$P = C^d \bmod n$$

### Correctness Proof Sketch
By Euler's theorem, $P^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ when $\gcd(P, n) = 1$. Since $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, we have $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$. Therefore:
$$C^d = (P^e)^d = P^{ed} = P^{1 + k\phi(n)} = P \cdot (P^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv P \cdot 1^k \equiv P \pmod{n}$$

---

## 2.4 Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) — CPS-Friendly Asymmetric Primitive

An **elliptic curve** over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$ is the set of points $(x, y)$ satisfying:
$$y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod{p}$$
together with a point at infinity $\mathcal{O}$ serving as the identity element.

The **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**: given points $P$ and $Q = kP$, find the scalar $k$. No sub-exponential algorithm is known, so **160-bit ECC keys ≈ 1024-bit RSA keys** in strength.

### ECC Point Addition (the "addition" is geometric)
For two distinct points $P = (x_1, y_1)$ and $Q = (x_2, y_2)$:
$$\lambda = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \pmod p$$
$$x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 \pmod p$$
$$y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod p$$

### Point Doubling ($P = Q$)
$$\lambda = \frac{3x_1^2 + a}{2y_1} \pmod p$$
$$x_3 = \lambda^2 - 2x_1 \pmod p$$
$$y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod p$$

### ECDH Key Exchange (used in CPS device pairing)
1. Alice and Bob agree on curve and generator $G$.
2. Alice picks secret $a$, computes $A = aG$.
3. Bob picks secret $b$, computes $B = bG$.
4. They exchange $A$ and $B$ over an insecure channel.
5. Both compute the **shared secret**: Alice gets $aB = abG$, Bob gets $bA = abG$.

---

## 2.5 Cryptographic Hash Functions — The Backbone of CPS Authentication

A hash function $H(\cdot)$ maps arbitrary input to a fixed-size digest:
$$h = H(M), \quad h \in \{0,1\}^n$$

### Security Properties (the "three pillars")

| Property | Definition | CPS Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-image resistance** | Given $h$, hard to find $M$ | Protects stored passwords in HMI databases |
| **Second pre-image resistance** | Given $M_1$, hard to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ | Prevents firmware swap with same hash |
| **Collision resistance** | Hard to find any $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ | Required for digital signatures |

### Standard Hash Functions Used in CPS

| Algorithm | Output Size | Status in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **SHA-256** | 256 bits | Most widely used (TLS 1.3, IPsec, Bitcoin) |
| **SHA-3 (Keccak)** | 256/512 bits | Newer, sponge-based, hardware-friendly |
| **BLAKE2 / BLAKE3** | 256 bits | Faster than SHA-256 in software |
| **SHA-1 / MD5** | 160/128 bits | **BROKEN — never use in new CPS designs** |

### HMAC — Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code
$$HMAC_K(M) = H\Big(\big(K \oplus opad\big) \;\|\; H\big(\big(K \oplus ipad\big) \;\|\; M\big)\Big)$$
where $ipad$ and $opad$ are fixed padding constants. HMAC provides **data authentication** (integrity + source verification) without encryption — extremely useful in CPS where confidentiality is not always required (e.g., broadcast sensor readings).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute values and conditionals are written using `\vert` (not `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Domain | Formula | Purpose | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Symmetric encryption | $C = E_K(P)$ | Confidentiality | Key size in bits |
| Asymmetric encryption | $C = P^e \bmod n$ | RSA encryption | $n$ modulus in bits |
| RSA decryption | $P = C^d \bmod n$ | Recover plaintext | Uses CRT for speed |
| RSA modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Public key | $p, q \geq 1024$ bits |
| Euler totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | RSA key gen | Composite totient |
| Key relation | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Public/Private link | $\gcd(e, \phi) = 1$ |
| ECDLP | Given $Q = kP$, find $k$ | ECC security | Hard problem |
| ECDH shared secret | $S = aB = bA = abG$ | Key exchange | Scalar mult over EC |
| Hash digest | $h = H(M)$ | Integrity | $h$ is fixed length |
| HMAC | $H((K \oplus opad) \vert\vert H((K \oplus ipad) \vert\vert M))$ | Authenticated msg | Two-pass hash |
| AES block | 128 bits | Symmetric block size | Standard block |
| AES key | 128 / 192 / 256 bits | Symmetric key | 10/12/14 rounds |
| RSA security | $\geq 2048$-bit $n$ | Current KTU/KT standard | Avoid 1024-bit |
| ECC security | $\geq 256$-bit curve | Modern standard | Curve25519 / P-256 |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of CPS Authentication & Encryption

| Domain | Authentication Used For | Encryption Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Grid (IEC 62351)** | Authenticate IEDs, PMU streams | Protect control commands & metering data |
| **Medical CPS (FDA guidance)** | Authenticate clinicians, devices | HIPAA-grade patient data confidentiality |
| **Industrial IoT (OPC UA)** | X.509 certs between PLCs | TLS 1.3 for all data exchange |
| **Autonomous Vehicles (V2X)** | Sign BSM messages (ECDSA) | Encrypt infotainment, not safety messages |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Full RSA Key Generation + Encryption + Decryption

**Problem:** Generate RSA keys, encrypt $P = 42$, and decrypt the ciphertext using a small RSA instance suitable for KTU board examination.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board routinely asks students to demonstrate a **complete RSA mini-example** with $p, q \le 50$ to verify understanding of every algebraic step. Master this derivation.

### Given
Choose small primes: $p = 5$, $q = 11$, plaintext $P = 42$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Modulus
$$n = p \cdot q = 5 \cdot 11 = 55$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Euler Totient
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \cdot 10 = 40$$

### Step 3 — Choose the Public Exponent
Pick $e$ such that $1 < e < 40$ and $\gcd(e, 40) = 1$.
Try $e = 7$: $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$ ✔ (since $40 = 5 \cdot 8$, has no factor 7).
Therefore $e = 7$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Private Exponent
We need $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, i.e., $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Apply the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

$$40 = 5 \cdot 7 + 5$$
$$7 = 1 \cdot 5 + 2$$
$$5 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot 2$$
$$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot (7 - 1 \cdot 5) = 3 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 7$$
$$1 = 3 \cdot (40 - 5 \cdot 7) - 2 \cdot 7 = 3 \cdot 40 - 17 \cdot 7$$

So $-17 \cdot 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, i.e., $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$.

Therefore $d = 23$.

### Step 5 — Publish Keys
Public key: $K_{pub} = (n, e) = (55, 7)$.
Private key: $K_{priv} = (n, d) = (55, 23)$.

### Step 6 — Encrypt $P = 42$
$$C = P^e \bmod n = 42^7 \bmod 55$$

Compute step-by-step using successive squaring:

$$42^1 \bmod 55 = 42$$
$$42^2 = 1764; \quad 1764 \div 55 = 32 \cdot 55 + 4; \quad 42^2 \bmod 55 = 4$$
$$42^4 = (42^2)^2 = 4^2 = 16; \quad 16 \bmod 55 = 16$$
$$42^7 = 42^4 \cdot 42^2 \cdot 42^1 = 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 42$$

Compute $16 \cdot 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 55 = 9 \pmod{55}$.
Compute $9 \cdot 42 = 378$; $378 = 6 \cdot 55 + 48$; so $378 \bmod 55 = 48$.

$$\boxed{C = 48}$$

### Step 7 — Decrypt $C = 48$
$$P = C^d \bmod n = 48^{23} \bmod 55$$

Using successive squaring on exponent $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1$:

$$48^1 \bmod 55 = 48$$
$$48^2 = 2304; \quad 2304 \div 55 = 41 \cdot 55 + 49; \quad 48^2 \bmod 55 = 49$$
$$48^4 = 49^2 = 2401; \quad 2401 \div 55 = 43 \cdot 55 + 36; \quad 48^4 \bmod 55 = 36$$
$$48^8 = 36^2 = 1296; \quad 1296 \div 55 = 23 \cdot 55 + 31; \quad 48^8 \bmod 55 = 31$$
$$48^{16} = 31^2 = 961; \quad 961 \div 55 = 17 \cdot 55 + 26; \quad 48^{16} \bmod 55 = 26$$

Combine for $48^{23} = 48^{16} \cdot 48^4 \cdot 48^2 \cdot 48^1$:

$$26 \cdot 36 = 936; \quad 936 \bmod 55 = 936 - 17 \cdot 55 = 936 - 935 = 1$$
$$1 \cdot 49 = 49$$
$$49 \cdot 48 = 2352; \quad 2352 \bmod 55 = 2352 - 42 \cdot 55 = 2352 - 2310 = 42$$

$$\boxed{P = 42 \;\; \checkmark}$$

**Verification:** The recovered plaintext equals the original — RSA round-trip successful.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange (Authentication Prerequisite)

**Problem:** Demonstrate a complete Diffie–Hellman key exchange with public parameters $p = 23$, $g = 5$, and private keys $a = 6$, $b = 15$.

### Step 1 — Public Parameters
Prime $p = 23$, generator $g = 5$ (a *primitive root* modulo 23).

### Step 2 — Each Party Computes Their Public Value
Alice: $A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23$.

$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^4 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^6 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \pmod{23}$$

Therefore $A = 8$.

Bob: $B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23$.

From above, $5^4 \equiv 4$, $5^8 \equiv 4^2 = 16 \pmod{23}$, $5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1$.

Compute each:
$$5^1 \bmod 23 = 5$$
$$5^2 \bmod 23 = 2 \quad \text{(from above)}$$
$$5^4 \bmod 23 = 4$$
$$5^8 \bmod 23 = 16$$

Combine: $5^{15} \equiv 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 640 \pmod{23}$.

$640 \div 23 = 27 \cdot 23 + 19$, so $640 \bmod 23 = 19$.

Therefore $B = 19$.

### Step 3 — Exchange $A$ and $B$ Publicly
Alice sends $A = 8$ to Bob. Bob sends $B = 19$ to Alice. Eve observes both.

### Step 4 — Compute Shared Secret
Alice computes $S = B^a \bmod p = 19^6 \bmod 23$.

$$19 \equiv -4 \pmod{23}$$
$$19^2 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$$
$$19^4 \equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 11 \cdot 23 = 256 - 253 = 3 \pmod{23}$$
$$19^6 = 19^4 \cdot 19^2 \equiv 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \equiv 48 - 2 \cdot 23 = 2 \pmod{23}$$

So Alice gets $S_A = 2$.

Bob computes $S = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23$.

$$8^1 \equiv 8 \pmod{23}$$
$$8^2 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \cdot 23 = 18 \pmod{23}$$
$$8^4 \equiv 18^2 = 324 \equiv 324 - 14 \cdot 23 = 324 - 322 = 2 \pmod{23}$$
$$8^8 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$$
$$8^{15} = 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \pmod{23}$$

Combine: $4 \cdot 2 = 8$; $8 \cdot 18 = 144 \equiv 144 - 6 \cdot 23 = 144 - 138 = 6 \pmod{23}$; $6 \cdot 8 = 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$.

So Bob gets $S_B = 2$.

$$\boxed{S_A = S_B = 2 \;\; \checkmark \text{ (shared secret = 2)}}$$

**Key Insight:** An eavesdropper Eve sees $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $A = 8$, $B = 19$. To recover $a$ she must solve $5^a \equiv 8 \pmod{23}$ — the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — which is computationally infeasible for large primes ($p \ge 2048$ bits).

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: HMAC Computation Sketch

**Problem:** Verify that HMAC satisfies the authentication property — show why an attacker who flips a single bit of $M$ cannot forge the correct tag without $K$.

HMAC construction:
$$HMAC_K(M) = H\Big(\big(K \oplus opad\big) \;\|\; H\big(\big(K \oplus ipad\big) \;\|\; M\big)\Big)$$

### Why two nested hashes?

**Inner hash:** $H(K \oplus ipad \;\|\; M)$ — binds the key to the message. Even a single-bit change in $M$ alters the inner hash completely (avalanche effect).

**Outer hash:** $H(K \oplus opad \;\|\; \text{inner})$ — protects the inner hash from length-extension attacks (a known weakness of $H(K \;\|\; M)$).

### Authentication Guarantee
Given $K$, recomputing $HMAC_K(M)$ is trivial. Without $K$:
- Attacker can flip a bit of $M$ → inner hash changes unpredictably (avalanche).
- Attacker cannot forge the outer hash without $K$.
- Therefore, **forgery probability $\approx 2^{-n}$** for an $n$-bit hash. For SHA-256, this is $\approx 2^{-256}$ — astronomically small.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right CPS Crypto Primitive

| Requirement in CPS | Recommended Primitive | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Bulk sensor stream confidentiality | AES-128-GCM | HW-accelerated, AEAD |
| Constrained node (8-bit MCU) | AES-128 (ECB-CBC only) or ChaCha20 | Low RAM, no TLS |
| Device authentication (one-shot) | ECDSA (P-256) signature | Small keys, low energy |
| Session key establishment | ECDH (Curve25519) | Forward secrecy |
| Firmware integrity check | SHA-256 hash stored in secure ROM | Tamper detection |
| Broadcast sensor authentication | HMAC-SHA256 (with TESLA) | Tolerates delay |
| Resource-constrained broadcast | TESLA or Poly1305 | Asymmetric-lightweight |
| Long-term archival confidentiality | Hybrid: RSA-2048 wrap + AES-256-GCM | Combines strengths |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of CPS Security Stack

The diagram below maps the **layered security architecture** of a generic CPS, showing where authentication and encryption primitives plug into the protocol stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Application Layer SCADA HMI Control Logic] --> A2[TLS 1.3 or DTLS 1.3 Handshake ECDSA and ECDHE]
    A2 --> A3[Authenticated Encryption AES 128 GCM]
    A3 --> A4[Transport Layer UDP for DTLS or TCP for TLS]
    A4 --> A5[Network Layer IPsec with ESP and AH]
    A5 --> A6[Link Layer 802.15.4 MAC Security or MACsec]
    A6 --> A7[Physical Layer Wired Bus or Wireless RF and Sensors]

    A7 --> A8[Physical Sensor Data Temperature Pressure Position]
    A8 --> A9[Hardware Root of Trust TPM or Secure Element or PUF]
    A9 --> A2

    B1[Key Management KMS or PKI or Distributed CA] -.provisions.-> A2
    B1 -.provisions.-> A3
    B1 -.provisions.-> A6
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **vertical stack** (top to bottom) is the OSI-inspired CPS protocol stack.
- The **right-side chain** (A7 → A8 → A9 → A2) shows how physical-layer trust flows upward through hardware roots of trust into cryptographic authentication.
- The **dashed lines from B1** show that a separate **Key Management Subsystem (KMS)** injects keys at every layer — centralisation of trust.

---

## 4.2 Authentication Flow — Challenge–Response Protocol

The classic **mutual authentication handshake** used in CPS device pairing (e.g., Matter, Zigbee 3.0, OPC UA).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Verifier Gateway
    participant P as Prover Sensor Node

    V->>P: Step1 Identity Request ID_V
    P->>V: Step2 Identity Response ID_P Certificate or Device PubKey
    V->>P: Step3 Challenge Nonce N random 128 bit
    P->>V: Step4 Signed Challenge Sig with K_priv or HMAC
    V->>V: Step5 Verify Signature using K_pub
    V->>P: Step6 Mutual Challenge Nonce M random 128 bit
    P->>V: Step7 Signed Mutual Response Sig with K_priv
    V->>V: Step8 Final Verify
    Note over V,P: Both parties derive K_session via ECDH or HKDF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Steps 1–5 = **one-way authentication** (verifier proves prover is genuine).
- Steps 6–8 = **mutual authentication** (prover verifies verifier too — prevents rogue gateway attacks).
- The **session key derivation** at the end establishes a fresh symmetric key for subsequent AES-GCM traffic.

---

## 4.3 Symmetric Encryption Block Diagram (AES-128 Round Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plaintext Block 128 bits] --> P2[AddRoundKey XOR with Round Key 0]
    P2 --> P3[SubBytes S box substitution]
    P3 --> P4[ShiftRows Row wise permutation]
    P4 --> P5[MixColumns Matrix multiplication over GF 2 to 8]
    P5 --> P6[AddRoundKey XOR with Round Key i]
    P6 --> P7{Round 10 reached?}
    P7 -- No --> P3
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Final SubBytes ShiftRows AddRoundKey]
    P8 --> P9[Ciphertext Block 128 bits]

    K1[Master Key 128 bits] --> K2[Key Expansion Schedule]
    K2 --> P2
    K2 --> P6
    K2 --> P8
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- AES-128 performs **10 rounds** of four operations: SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey.
- The **final round** omits MixColumns (a subtle detail the KTU board occasionally tests).
- The **Key Expansion** generates 11 round keys from the master key — itself a non-trivial design that prevents related-key attacks.

---

## 4.4 Asymmetric Encryption (RSA) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Plaintext P] --> S2[Modular Exponentiation P to the power e mod n]
    S2 --> S3[Ciphertext C]
    S3 --> S4[Transmit over Insecure Channel]
    S4 --> S5[Modular Exponentiation C to the power d mod n]
    S5 --> S6[Recovered Plaintext P prime]
    S6 --> S7{Equals Original P?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Authentication and Confidentiality Achieved]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Decryption Failure or Tampered]
```

---

## 4.5 CPS Threat Model Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Threat Eavesdropper Passive Sniffing] --> C1[Countermeasure Encryption AES GCM or ChaCha20]
    T2[Threat Man in the Middle Active Interception] --> C2[Countermeasure Authentication via Digital Signatures ECDSA or RSA]
    T3[Threat Replay Attack Resend Old Sensor Data] --> C3[Countermeasure Nonces and Timestamps and Sequence Numbers]
    T4[Threat Node Compromise Steal Physical Device] --> C4[Countermeasure PUFs and Tamper Proof Hardware]
    T5[Threat Denial of Service Jam Control Loops] --> C5[Countermeasure Frequency Hopping and Redundant Paths]
    T6[Threat Firmware Tampering Inject Malicious Code] --> C6[Countermeasure Secure Boot and Code Signing using Hash Chains]
    T7[Threat Side Channel Power and EM Analysis] --> C7[Countermeasure Constant Time Implementations and Masking]
```
