# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test **definitions, working principles, and crisp comparisons**. Answers must be 3–5 lines, with the *first line carrying the formal definition*. Aim for 80–120 words.

---

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between authentication and authorization in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Authentication** is the cryptographic process of verifying the *claimed identity* of an entity (user, device, sensor, firmware) in a CPS, typically via passwords, certificates, digital signatures, or HMACs. **Authorization**, in contrast, determines *what an already-authenticated entity is permitted to do* — it enforces access-control policies (read/write/admin) on authenticated principals. In CPS, authentication asks *"Is this the genuine insulin pump from patient A?"* while authorization asks *"May this pump currently adjust the dosage?"* The two are sequential: authentication must succeed before authorization is evaluated. Common implementation: authentication uses ECDSA/RSA signatures, while authorization uses RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) or ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) policies defined in IEC 62443.

**[Marks Distribution: Definition 1 + Contrast 1 + CPS Example 1]**

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain why symmetric encryption alone is insufficient for CPS device authentication, with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

Symmetric encryption uses a **shared secret key** for both encryption and decryption, which means both parties must possess the same $K$ *before* any secure communication. In CPS, provisioning a shared key to thousands of unattended sensor nodes (e.g., in a smart-grid deployment) is operationally infeasible and creates a single point of compromise — whoever holds $K$ can impersonate any device. Furthermore, symmetric schemes **cannot provide non-repudiation**: a sensor that signs a reading with $K$ cannot be distinguished from any other holder of $K$. Therefore, CPS device authentication relies on **asymmetric primitives** (ECDSA on P-256) where the private key never leaves the device, enabling unforgeable identity proofs and supporting Public-Key Infrastructure (PKI) for scalable trust.

**[Marks Distribution: Symmetric limitation 1 + CPS problem 1 + Asymmetric solution 1]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** between Q-A and Q-B on the same module. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The valuation strictly follows: *Statement of principle (2 marks) + Working / Diagram (3 marks) + Justification / Example (2 marks)*.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a CPS security framework**, clearly identifying where authentication and encryption mechanisms are applied at each layer. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Perform **complete RSA key generation, encryption, and decryption** for $p = 3$, $q = 11$, $P = 5$, $e = 7$. Show every modular arithmetic step. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A CPS security framework is organised as a **layered protocol stack** where each layer applies specific cryptographic primitives.

1. **Physical Layer:** Hardware roots of trust (TPM, PUF) generate unique per-device keys. Tamper sensors detect physical intrusion.
2. **Link/MAC Layer (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4):** Provides per-frame authentication via AES-CCM (128-bit keys), ensuring each radio packet is both encrypted and authenticated.
3. **Network Layer (e.g., IPsec / RPL):** End-to-end tunnels using ESP with AES-GCM and AH for header integrity.
4. **Transport Layer (e.g., DTLS 1.3):** Mutual authentication of endpoints via ECDSA certificates; session keys derived via ECDHE for forward secrecy.
5. **Application Layer:** End-to-end authentication using X.509 certificates; encryption using AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305; access control via OPC UA security policies.

```
+--------------------------------+
|  Application (X.509 + AES-GCM) |
+--------------------------------+
|  Transport  (DTLS / TLS 1.3)   |
+--------------------------------+
|  Network    (IPsec ESP + AH)   |
+--------------------------------+
|  Link       (802.15.4 AES-CCM) |
+--------------------------------+
|  Physical   (TPM / PUF)        |
+--------------------------------+
```

**[Marks: Diagram 3 + Layer-wise explanation 4 = 7]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $p = 3$, $q = 11$, $P = 5$, $e = 7$.

**Step 1 — Modulus:**
$$n = p \cdot q = 3 \cdot 11 = 33$$

**Step 2 — Euler totient:**
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 2 \cdot 10 = 20$$

**Step 3 — Public exponent:** $e = 7$ (given). Verify $\gcd(7, 20) = 1$ ✔.

**Step 4 — Private exponent:** Solve $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{20}$.

By inspection: $7 \cdot 3 = 21 \equiv 1 \pmod{20}$. So $d = 3$.

**Step 5 — Keys:**
$K_{pub} = (33, 7)$, $K_{priv} = (33, 3)$.

**Step 6 — Encrypt:**
$$C = P^e \bmod n = 5^7 \bmod 33$$
$$5^2 = 25; \quad 5^4 = 25^2 = 625; \quad 625 \div 33 = 18 \cdot 33 + 31; \quad 5^4 \bmod 33 = 31 \equiv -2 \pmod{33}$$
$$5^7 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \equiv (-2)(25)(5) = -250 \pmod{33}$$
$$-250 = -8 \cdot 33 + 14 = -264 + 14; \quad \text{so } -250 \bmod 33 = 14$$

$$\boxed{C = 14}$$

**Step 7 — Decrypt:**
$$P = C^d \bmod n = 14^3 \bmod 33$$
$$14^2 = 196; \quad 196 \bmod 33 = 196 - 5 \cdot 33 = 196 - 165 = 31 \equiv -2 \pmod{33}$$
$$14^3 = 14^2 \cdot 14 \equiv (-2)(14) = -28 \pmod{33}$$
$$-28 + 33 = 5$$

$$\boxed{P = 5 \;\; \checkmark}$$

**Verification:** $P_{\text{recovered}} = 5 = P_{\text{original}}$ — RSA round-trip verified.

**[Marks: Key gen 2 + Encryption 2 + Decryption 2 + Verification 1 = 7]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **HMAC-based authentication** in CPS. How does it differ from digital-signature-based authentication in terms of computational cost and security guarantees? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Perform a **Diffie–Hellman key exchange** with public parameters $p = 17$, $g = 3$, and private keys $a = 5$ (Alice), $b = 7$ (Bob). Show the shared-secret computation at both ends. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** is a symmetric-key construction that provides data authentication and integrity without asymmetric operations:

$$HMAC_K(M) = H\Big(\big(K \oplus opad\big) \;\|\; H\big(\big(K \oplus ipad\big) \;\|\; M\big)\Big)$$

**Working Principle:**
1. Sender and receiver share a secret key $K$.
2. Sender computes the tag $T = HMAC_K(M)$ and appends it to $M$.
3. Receiver recomputes $T'$ from received $M$ and shared $K$; accepts if $T = T'$.

**Comparison with Digital Signatures:**

| Aspect | HMAC | ECDSA / RSA Signatures |
|---|---|---|
| **Key type** | Symmetric (shared secret) | Asymmetric (private + public) |
| **Computational cost** | ~2 hash evaluations (~µs on MCU) | ~EC scalar mult (~ms on MCU) |
| **Non-repudiation** | NO (any holder of $K$ could have signed) | YES (only private key holder) |
| **Key distribution** | Hard at scale | Easy via PKI / certificates |
| **Use in CPS** | Broadcast sensor tags, TESLA protocol | Device identity, firmware signing |

**Conclusion:** HMAC is preferred for **high-frequency, low-energy CPS authentication** (e.g., per-packet sensor authentication in WSN). Digital signatures are preferred when **non-repudiation** is legally required (e.g., signing a firmware update image).

**[Marks: HMAC working 3 + Comparison table 3 + CPS choice 1 = 7]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $p = 17$, $g = 3$, $a = 5$, $b = 7$.

**Step 1 — Compute Alice's public value $A$:**
$$A = g^a \bmod p = 3^5 \bmod 17$$
$$3^2 = 9$$
$$3^4 = 9^2 = 81 \bmod 17 = 81 - 4 \cdot 17 = 81 - 68 = 13$$
$$3^5 = 3^4 \cdot 3^1 = 13 \cdot 3 = 39 \bmod 17 = 39 - 2 \cdot 17 = 5$$

So $A = 5$.

**Step 2 — Compute Bob's public value $B$:**
$$B = g^b \bmod p = 3^7 \bmod 17$$
$$3^5 = 5 \quad \text{(from above)}$$
$$3^7 = 3^5 \cdot 3^2 = 5 \cdot 9 = 45 \bmod 17 = 45 - 2 \cdot 17 = 11$$

So $B = 11$.

**Step 3 — Exchange $A$ and $B$ over the (insecure) channel.**

**Step 4 — Compute shared secret at Alice's side:**
$$S_A = B^a \bmod p = 11^5 \bmod 17$$

Successive squaring:
$$11^1 \bmod 17 = 11$$
$$11^2 = 121 \bmod 17 = 121 - 7 \cdot 17 = 121 - 119 = 2$$
$$11^4 = 2^2 = 4$$
$$11^5 = 11^4 \cdot 11^1 = 4 \cdot 11 = 44 \bmod 17 = 44 - 2 \cdot 17 = 10$$

So $S_A = 10$.

**Step 5 — Compute shared secret at Bob's side:**
$$S_B = A^b \bmod p = 5^7 \bmod 17$$

Successive squaring:
$$5^1 \bmod 17 = 5$$
$$5^2 = 25 \bmod 17 = 8$$
$$5^4 = 8^2 = 64 \bmod 17 = 64 - 3 \cdot 17 = 13$$
$$5^7 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 = 13 \cdot 8 \cdot 5$$

Compute $13 \cdot 8 = 104$; $104 \bmod 17 = 104 - 6 \cdot 17 = 104 - 102 = 2$.
Compute $2 \cdot 5 = 10$.

So $S_B = 10$.

$$\boxed{S_A = S_B = 10 \;\; \checkmark}$$

**Verification:** Both parties derive the same shared secret **10**, without ever transmitting it on the wire. An eavesdropper knowing only $(p, g, A, B) = (17, 3, 5, 11)$ would need to solve $3^a \equiv 5 \pmod{17}$ to break the exchange — the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**, infeasible for large primes.

**[Marks: Public values 2 + Alice secret 2 + Bob secret 2 + Verification 1 = 7]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in CPS Security questions:**
> 1. **Confusing authentication with encryption** (–2 marks): Authentication is about *identity proof*; encryption is about *confidentiality*. They are independent services. Many students blur them.
> 2. **Forgetting the modulus in modular exponentiation** (–2 marks): Writing $C = P^e$ instead of $C = P^e \bmod n$. The modulus is what makes RSA hard to invert.
> 3. **Not verifying that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$** when choosing RSA exponents (–1 mark): If $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) \neq 1$, then $e$ has no modular inverse and the private key $d$ does not exist.
> 4. **Using ECB mode** in CPS designs (–2 marks): ECB leaks plaintext patterns. KTU board actively penalises this since 2022.
> 5. **Forgetting to include the IV/nonce** in CBC/CTR/GCM modes (–1 mark): Reusing a nonce catastrophically breaks confidentiality and authenticity in GCM.
> 6. **Writing "HMAC = H(M)" instead of the nested construction** (–2 marks): The double-hash structure is the whole point of HMAC; without it, length-extension attacks are possible.
> 7. **Confusing $K_{pub}$ and $K_{priv}$** in RSA examples (–2 marks): Encryption uses $e$ (public); decryption uses $d$ (private). Mixing them up is the #1 most common error.
> 8. **Skipping the final "verification" step** in worked examples (–1 mark): Always show $P_{\text{recovered}} = P_{\text{original}}$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **CPS Security** spans CIA + **Safety**; attacks can cause physical harm, not just data loss.
- **Authentication** = *who are you?*; **Authorization** = *what can you do?*; **Encryption** = *hiding data?* — three distinct services.
- **Symmetric crypto** (AES, ChaCha20) uses **one shared key** — fast, but key distribution is hard.
- **Asymmetric crypto** (RSA, ECC) uses **key pair** — solves distribution; ~100–1000× slower.
- **RSA security** rests on factoring $n = p \cdot q$; key gen requires $\gcd(e, \phi) = 1$ and $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi}$.
- **ECC** offers RSA-equivalent security at **~10× smaller keys** — ideal for constrained CPS nodes.
- **Hash functions** (SHA-256, SHA-3) provide integrity; **HMAC** adds keyed authentication.
- **Diffie–Hellman / ECDH** enables key agreement without prior shared secret — foundation of forward secrecy.
- **Block cipher modes**: never use ECB; prefer **AES-GCM** (AEAD: confidentiality + integrity in one).
- **Mutual authentication** requires **both** parties to prove identity — prevents rogue-gateway attacks.
- **CPS constraints**: lightweight crypto (PRESENT, Simon/Speck), hardware acceleration, AEAD primitives.
- **Real protocols**: TLS 1.3, DTLS 1.3, IPsec, OPC UA Security, Matter, Zigbee 3.0 all combine authentication + encryption.
- **Board tip**: Always state the **principle**, **show the math**, and **verify the result** in any 7-mark question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for this Module:**
> *“A CPS without authentication is a puppet; without encryption, it is a billboard; without integrity, it is a liability.”*
> Master the RSA mini-example (Section 3.1) and the DH key exchange (Section 3.2) — these two derivations alone account for ~60% of marks in Module 3 questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tolerance and Reliability in CPS Designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fault Tolerance and Reliability in CPS Designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) — Recap

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an integrated computational and physical process network in which embedded computers, sensors, and actuators monitor, coordinate, and control physical entities in real time. The cyber component (computation/communication) is tightly coupled with the physical component (sensing/actuation), creating a feedback loop across the digital and physical worlds.

## 1.2 Reliability — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability (R(t))** is formally defined as the *conditional probability that a system will perform its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time (t), given that it was operational at time t = 0*.
>
> Mathematically expressed as: **R(t) = P(T > t)**, where **T** is the random variable denoting the time-to-failure.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, reliability is treated as a *probabilistic measure of failure-free operation*, and is the foundational metric upon which safety-critical CPS engineering decisions (e.g., autonomous vehicles, medical robotics, smart grids, aviation fly-by-wire) are based.

## 1.3 Fault Tolerance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Tolerance (FT)** is the *built-in capability of a CPS to maintain its specified functional and safety behaviour despite the occurrence of internal hardware faults, software bugs, sensor/actuator anomalies, or external disturbances* — by employing redundancy, error detection, isolation, and recovery mechanisms.

A **fault** is the *root cause* of an error, and an **error** is the *manifestation* that may lead to a **failure** (deviation from required behaviour). This causal chain is central to KTU exam questions.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Imagine an aircraft's autopilot system:**

- The **aircraft** is the *physical* system.
- The **flight computer, GPS, inertial sensors, and control surfaces** form the *cyber* layer.

**Reliability** is like asking: *"How long will the autopilot function correctly without breaking down?"* — a probabilistic promise about the system.

**Fault Tolerance** is like asking: *"If one of the three redundant flight computers crashes mid-flight, will the plane still fly safely?"* — a structural property that the system keeps working *even when* something breaks.

In a modern Boeing/Airbus jet, **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** ensures that even if one or two computers disagree due to a fault, the majority vote keeps the plane flying. That is fault tolerance built atop statistical reliability engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (commonly tested in KTU):**
> - **Reliability** is a *probabilistic metric* — measured in time.
> - **Fault Tolerance** is a *design property* — implemented through architectural choices like redundancy and reconfiguration.

## 1.5 Why Fault Tolerance and Reliability Matter in CPS

CPS deployments are often **safety-critical** and **mission-critical**:

- A sensor fault in an **autonomous vehicle** can cause a crash.
- A communication drop in a **smart grid** can trigger a regional blackout.
- An actuator failure in a **surgical robot** can be life-threatening.

Therefore, KTU 2024 emphasizes these concepts under **Course Outcome CO3** (Design fault-tolerant and reliable CPS architectures) and the syllabus frames them as engineering design requirements, not just theoretical metrics.

## 1.6 Core Metrics in Bold (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — Average time between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** — Average time until a non-repairable system fails.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** — Average time to restore a repairable system.
- **Availability (A)** — Fraction of time the system is operational.
- **Failure Rate (λ)** — Number of failures per unit time.
- **FIT (Failures In Time)** — Number of failures per 10⁹ device-hours.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fault–Error–Failure Chain

The KTU syllabus explicitly tests the **causal chain** in CPS fault analysis:

**Fault (cause) → Error (manifestation) → Failure (deviation from spec)**

A *fault* is activated, leading to an *error* in the system state. If the error is not contained, it propagates to cause a *failure* at the system boundary. Fault tolerance aims to **detect the error, isolate it, and recover** before a system-level failure occurs.

## 2.2 Classification of Faults in CPS

| **Fault Category** | **Examples in CPS Context** | **Detection Difficulty** |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Faults** | Sensor drift, actuator wear, CPU bit-flip | Moderate |
| **Software Faults** | Bugs in control law, race conditions | High |
| **Transient Faults** | EMI noise, cosmic ray hits, voltage spikes | High (intermittent) |
| **Permanent Faults** | Burnt-out motor, broken wire | Low (visible) |
| **Byzantine Faults** | Malicious nodes, arbitrary wrong outputs | Very High |
| **Communication Faults** | Packet loss, delay spikes, network partition | High |
| **Sensor Faults** | Bias, freezing, drift, calibration loss | Moderate |
| **Actuator Faults** | Stuck-at, partial loss of effectiveness | High |

## 2.3 Reliability Theory — Building Blocks

### 2.3.1 The Bath-Tub Curve (Reliability over Lifetime)

A system's hazard rate λ(t) follows three phases:

1. **Infant Mortality** — High failure rate, decreasing with time (burn-in).
2. **Useful Life** — Constant low failure rate (exponential reliability regime).
3. **Wear-Out Phase** — Increasing failure rate due to aging.

### 2.3.2 Reliability Function and Failure Rate

For a system with constant failure rate **λ** (the exponential distribution regime):

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **Unreliability** (probability of failure by time *t*) is:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of failure time:

$$f(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

### 2.3.3 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

The expected lifetime of a non-repairable system:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

### 2.3.4 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and Availability

For a repairable system:

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$$

**Steady-State Availability (A):**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$$

Availability can also be expressed as a percentage: **A% = A × 100**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula / Metric** | **Expression** | **Units** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | Constant failure rate |
| Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | Probability of failure |
| MTTF (non-repairable) | $\text{MTTF} = 1 / \lambda$ | Hours | Exponential distribution |
| MTBF (repairable) | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Hours | Repairable systems |
| Availability | $A = \text{MTTF} / (\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR})$ | Dimensionless | Repairable CPS |
| FIT (Failure In Time) | $\text{FIT} = \lambda \times 10^{9}$ | Failures / 10⁹ hours | Industrial component rating |
| Series System Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Dimensionless | All components must work |
| Parallel System Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Dimensionless | At least one must work |
| k-out-of-n Reliability | $R_{k/n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | Dimensionless | TMR, voting systems |
| Hazard Rate (general) | $\lambda(t) = f(t) / R(t)$ | Per hour | Time-varying failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** When expressing the k-out-of-n reliability formula in exam answers, **always expand the binomial sum** (e.g., for 2-out-of-3: $R_{2/3} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$) rather than writing it in compact summation form, as partial credit depends on showing all terms.

## 2.5 Fault Tolerance Strategies (KTU Favourites)

| **Strategy** | **Description** | **CPS Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Redundancy (TMR)** | 3 identical modules + majority voter | Aircraft flight control |
| **Information Redundancy** | Error-correcting codes, checksums | CAN bus, network packets |
| **Time Redundancy** | Re-execute task, compare results | Real-time control loops |
| **Software Redundancy (N-Version)** | Independent code versions, voting | Safety-critical firmware |
| **Recovery Blocks** | Try primary, accept if test passes | Embedded mission systems |
| **Watchdog Timers** | Reset system on hang | IoT edge nodes |
| **Checkpointing** | Save state, rollback on fault | Long-running CPS tasks |
| **Hot Standby** | Backup actively running | Power-grid controllers |
| **Cold Standby** | Backup powered off until needed | Satellite subsystems |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace**: NASA / ISRO use **TMR** in spacecraft computers because radiation-induced single-event upsets (SEUs) are common in orbit.
- **Automotive**: ISO 26262 mandates **ASIL-D** functional safety; CPS ECUs in autonomous cars use redundant braking and steering ECUs.
- **Healthcare**: Patient monitors and infusion pumps use **M-of-N voting** to suppress false alarms.
- **Industrial IoT**: PLCs use **watchdog timers** and **parity checks** to survive harsh electromagnetic environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability Function from Failure Rate

We start from the definition of the **hazard rate** (instantaneous failure rate):

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 1:** Express the conditional probability of failure in the next Δt, given survival up to time *t*:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, so $\frac{dF}{dt} = -\frac{dR}{dt}$:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Separate the variables R and t:

$$\lambda(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from **0 to t**, applying the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (a fresh system is reliable with probability 1):

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0) = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for R(t):

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right)$$

**Step 6 (Special Case):** For the **constant failure rate** regime (useful life), $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant), so the integral simplifies:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the most commonly tested form in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTF for Constant Failure Rate

The **expected time to failure** is the first moment of the failure density function:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Use integration by parts. Let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** The boundary term evaluates as:

$$\left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Step 4:** The remaining integral:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 (Final Result):**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Series System Reliability

Consider **n components connected in series**, all of which must function for the system to work. Each component *i* has reliability $R_i$, and the failures are statistically independent.

**Step 1:** The probability of system survival is the joint probability of all components surviving:

$$R_{\text{system}} = P(C_1 \text{ works} \cap C_2 \text{ works} \cap \dots \cap C_n \text{ works})$$

**Step 2:** Applying the independence assumption:

$$R_{\text{system}} = P(C_1 \text{ works}) \cdot P(C_2 \text{ works}) \cdots P(C_n \text{ works})$$

**Step 3:** Each factor equals the component's reliability:

$$\boxed{R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Series System):**

A CPS has three components in series with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.99$, $R_2 = 0.98$, $R_3 = 0.97$. Find the system reliability.

**Solution:**

$$R_{\text{series}} = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9412$$

(Approx. 94.12% — *each weak link degrades the chain*.)

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parallel System Reliability

In a parallel system, **at least one** component must function for the system to survive. The system fails only if *all* components fail.

**Step 1:** Probability of component *i* failing:

$$Q_i = 1 - R_i$$

**Step 2:** Probability that *all* n components fail (independent):

$$Q_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

**Step 3:** Probability of system survival is the complement:

$$\boxed{R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Parallel System):**

Two identical pumps operate in parallel (hot standby redundancy), each with reliability **R = 0.90**. The system fails only if both fail.

**Solution:**

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.90) = 1 - (0.10)(0.10) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

So even with only 90% reliable individual pumps, the parallel system achieves **99% reliability** — a clear demonstration of the power of redundancy.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) as 2-out-of-3 Voting

TMR uses three identical modules, and the system works correctly if **at least 2 of the 3** produce the correct output. Let each module have reliability **R** and voters be perfect.

**Step 1:** Use the binomial distribution. The system survives if exactly 2 work *or* exactly 3 work:

$$R_{\text{TMR}} = P(\text{exactly 3 work}) + P(\text{exactly 2 work})$$

**Step 2:** The probability of exactly *k* out of *n* working is the binomial:

$$P(\text{exactly } k) = \binom{n}{k} R^k (1-R)^{n-k}$$

**Step 3:** Apply n = 3, k = 2 and k = 3:

$$P(k=3) = \binom{3}{3} R^3 (1-R)^{0} = R^3$$

$$P(k=2) = \binom{3}{2} R^2 (1-R)^{1} = 3 R^2 (1-R)$$

**Step 4 (Final Result):**

$$\boxed{R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R) = 3R^2 - 2R^3}$$

**Numerical Check (R = 0.9):**

$$R_{\text{TMR}} = 3(0.9)^2 - 2(0.9)^3 = 3(0.81) - 2(0.729) = 2.43 - 1.458 = 0.972$$

So with 90% reliable individual modules, TMR achieves **97.2%** — better than one module alone, but not as good as ideal parallel redundancy, because of the "any 2 of 3" requirement.

## 3.6 Worked KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Comprehensive)

> **Problem:** A CPS has two processors in a hot-standby parallel configuration. Each processor has an exponential failure rate of **λ = 0.001 failures/hour**. The repair team takes on average **MTTR = 20 hours** to bring a failed processor back online. Compute (a) MTTF of one processor, (b) Availability of one processor, (c) Approximate Availability of the parallel system.

**Solution:**

**(a) MTTF of one processor:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

**(b) Availability of one processor:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1000}{1000 + 20} = \frac{1000}{1020} \approx 0.9804$$

So single-processor availability is **98.04%**.

**(c) Availability of the parallel system (using unavailability):**

Unavailability of one processor: $U = 1 - A = 0.0196$

Both processors must be down for the system to fail:

$$U_{\text{system}} = U \times U = (0.0196)^2 = 0.000384$$

$$A_{\text{system}} = 1 - U_{\text{system}} = 1 - 0.000384 = 0.999616$$

So the parallel system's availability is **~99.96%** — a near "five-nines" system, suitable for safety-critical CPS deployments.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| **If the question says...** | **Use this formula** |
|---|---|
| "constant failure rate" or "exponential" | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| "all components must work" | Series formula |
| "any one can keep system alive" | Parallel formula |
| "2 out of 3 voting" or "TMR" | $R = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ |
| "MTTF asked" with λ given | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ |
| "MTBF for repairable system" | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ |
| "fraction of uptime" | $A = \text{MTTF}/(\text{MTTF}+\text{MTTR})$ |
| "FIT value" | Convert using $\text{FIT} = \lambda \times 10^9$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fault Tolerance Architecture in CPS (Block Diagram)

This Mermaid block-level functional architecture illustrates how a CPS organizes its fault-tolerance layers, mapping the interactions between sensing, processing, redundancy, voting, and actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph physLayer[PHYSICAL PROCESS LAYER]
        envNode[PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT]
        actuatorNode[ACTUATORS MOTORS VALVES]
    end

    subgraph sensorLayer[SENSOR SUBSYSTEM LAYER]
        s1Node[SENSOR S1 PRIMARY]
        s2Node[SENSOR S2 REDUNDANT]
        s3Node[SENSOR S3 REDUNDANT]
    end

    subgraph cybLayer[CYBER COMPUTATION LAYER]
        c1Node[CONTROLLER C1 PRIMARY]
        c2Node[CONTROLLER C2 BACKUP]
        voterNode[MAJORITY VOTER TMR]
        fdNode[FAULT DETECTOR DIAGNOSIS MODULE]
        ftNode[FAULT TOLERANCE CONTROLLER]
    end

    subgraph commLayer[COMMUNICATION LAYER]
        busNode[REDUNDANT COMMUNICATION BUS CAN ETHERCAT]
    end

    envNode --> s1Node
    envNode --> s2Node
    envNode --> s3Node

    s1Node --> c1Node
    s2Node --> c2Node
    s3Node --> c1Node
    s3Node --> c2Node

    c1Node --> voterNode
    c2Node --> voterNode
    s1Node --> voterNode
    s2Node --> voterNode

    voterNode --> c1Node
    voterNode --> c2Node

    c1Node --> fdNode
    c2Node --> fdNode
    fdNode --> ftNode
    ftNode --> busNode

    busNode --> actuatorNode
    actuatorNode --> envNode
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- The **Sensor Subsystem** uses three redundant sensors (S1, S2, S3) feeding two independent controllers (C1, C2).
- The **Majority Voter (TMR)** is the central fault-detection and isolation node — it accepts two-of-three agreements.
- The **Fault Detector Module** continuously monitors controller outputs and the communication bus.
- The **Fault Tolerance Controller** triggers reconfiguration (e.g., switch to backup controller, isolate faulty sensor).
- The **Redundant Communication Bus** ensures messages reach actuators even if one bus fails.

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram — Series vs Parallel (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph series[SERIES CONFIGURATION - ALL MUST WORK]
        a1[COMPONENT 1] --> a2[COMPONENT 2] --> a3[COMPONENT 3] --> a4[SYSTEM OUTPUT]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph parallel[PARALLEL CONFIGURATION - ANY ONE CAN WORK]
        b1[COMPONENT 1] --> bj[JUNCTION]
        b2[COMPONENT 2] --> bj
        b3[COMPONENT 3] --> bj
        bj --> b4[SYSTEM OUTPUT]
    end
```

## 4.3 FDIR (Fault Detection, Isolation, and Recovery) Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startNode[SYSTEM OPERATION] --> detectNode[FAULT DETECTION MONITORING]
    detectNode --> decisionNode{FAULT PRESENT}
    decisionNode -->|NO| startNode
    decisionNode -->|YES| isoNode[FAULT ISOLATION IDENTIFY COMPONENT]
    isoNode --> recoNode[FAULT RECOVERY RECONFIGURE REDUNDANCY]
    recoNode --> verifyNode[VERIFY SYSTEM INTEGRITY]
    verifyNode --> startNode
```

This is the canonical **FDIR** loop tested in KTU papers.

## 4.4 CPS Layered Fault-Tolerance Mapping Table

| **CPS Layer** | **Possible Faults** | **Fault Tolerance Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor Layer | Drift, bias, freezing | Sensor redundancy + voting |
| Communication Layer | Packet loss, delay | Retransmission + redundant bus |
| Computation Layer | Bit-flip, software bug | TMR + N-version programming |
| Actuation Layer | Stuck actuator | Dual actuator + monitoring |
| Power Layer | Brownout, blackout | UPS, battery backup |
| Control Law Layer | Parameter drift | Adaptive control, observers |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

**Q: Differentiate between Fault, Error, and Failure in the context of a Cyber-Physical System. Give one example of each from an autonomous vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Term** | **Definition** | **Autonomous Vehicle Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Fault** | The *root cause* of a malfunction — a defect or disturbance in the system | A cracked LiDAR sensor housing |
| **Error** | The *manifestation* of a fault — incorrect system state | Inconsistent distance readings from the LiDAR |
| **Failure** | The *deviation* from specified behaviour at the system boundary | The vehicle fails to detect an obstacle and does not brake |

(3 marks: 1 mark for each correctly defined term with valid example.)

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: Define Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and Mean Time To Repair (MTTR). How are they related to system availability?**

**Model Answer:**

- **MTBF** is the average time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable system, computed as **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**, where **MTTF** is the mean time to failure.
- **MTTR** is the average time required to repair a failed component and bring the system back to operational state.
- The **steady-state availability (A)** is given by:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$$

A high MTBF (long reliable operation) and a low MTTR (fast repair) together maximize availability.

(3 marks: 1 mark each for MTBF, MTTR, and availability relation.)

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]

**Q (a) (7 Marks):** Explain the concept of **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** with a neat block diagram. How does it achieve fault tolerance in a CPS? Derive the reliability expression for a TMR system assuming identical modules with reliability R.

**Model Answer for Q(a):**

**TMR Concept:**
Triple Modular Redundancy is a hardware fault-tolerance technique in which **three identical modules** perform the same operation in parallel, and a **majority voter** selects the output that appears at least twice. The system is said to *tolerate* any single faulty module.

**Block Diagram (Textual):**

```
 INPUT --> [M1] \
 INPUT --> [M2] ---> [VOTER] --> OUTPUT
 INPUT --> [M3] /
```

**Working Principle:**

1. The same input is fed to all three modules simultaneously.
2. The voter compares the three outputs.
3. If at most one module is faulty, the majority output (the correct one) is selected.
4. The faulty module is *masked* — the system continues operating normally.

**Reliability Derivation:**

Using the 2-out-of-3 rule (system works if ≥ 2 modules work), with module reliability R:

- Probability that **all 3** work: $P_3 = R^3$
- Probability that **exactly 2** work: $P_2 = \binom{3}{2} R^2 (1-R) = 3R^2(1-R)$

**Total TMR reliability:**

$$R_{\text{TMR}} = P_3 + P_2 = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R) = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$

**Conclusion:** TMR improves reliability over a single module for $R > 0.5$, masking any single-point fault.

---

**Q (b) (7 Marks):** A CPS consists of **three components in series**, with individual reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, and $R_3 = 0.90$. Compute (i) the system reliability, (ii) the failure rate of the equivalent exponential system, and (iii) the MTTF of the system.

**Model Answer for Q(b):**

**(i) System reliability (series):**

$$R_{\text{system}} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{system}} = 0.7866}$$

**(ii) Equivalent failure rate:**

Using $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ with $t = 1$ hour as the mission time:

$$\lambda = -\ln(R_{\text{system}}) = -\ln(0.7866) = 0.2403 \text{ per hour}$$

**(iii) MTTF of the system:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.2403} \approx 4.16 \text{ hours}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with each component being 90–95% reliable, the series combination drops to ~78.66% — illustrating the multiplicative penalty of series dependencies in CPS.

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**Q (a) (7 Marks):** Describe the **FDIR (Fault Detection, Isolation, and Recovery)** mechanism used in Cyber-Physical Systems. List three common FDIR techniques and explain any one with a CPS example.

**Model Answer for Q(a):**

**FDIR Definition:**
FDIR is a structured methodology that enables a CPS to **detect** the presence of a fault, **isolate** the faulty component, and **recover** normal system operation — typically through reconfiguration, redundancy switching, or graceful degradation.

**Three Common FDIR Techniques:**

1. **Watchdog Timers** — Hardware timers that reset the system if the software fails to "kick" the timer within a deadline.
2. **Built-In Self-Test (BIST)** — On-board diagnostics that periodically test sensor/actuator health.
3. **Observer-Based Fault Detection** — Software observers (e.g., Kalman filters) estimate expected sensor outputs and flag large residuals as faults.

**Worked CPS Example — Kalman Filter Residual Monitoring in a Drone:**

A quadcopter uses a Kalman filter to fuse IMU and GPS data. The filter continuously predicts the next state and compares it to the actual sensor reading. The **residual** (innovation) is:

$$r_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$$

If $\vert r_k \vert > \tau$ (a threshold), a **sensor fault** is flagged. The flight controller then **isolates** the faulty sensor (e.g., GPS) and **recovers** by switching to IMU-only navigation. This ensures the drone can still land safely despite a GPS spoofing attack or signal loss.

---

**Q (b) (7 Marks):** A safety-critical CPS sensor has an MTTF of **5000 hours** and an MTTR of **10 hours**. Compute the steady-state availability. If a parallel redundant sensor (identical) is added, find the new system availability.

**Model Answer for Q(b):**

**Step 1: Single-sensor availability:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 10} = \frac{5000}{5010} \approx 0.99800$$

So single-sensor availability is **99.80%** (about 1.75 hours of downtime per year).

**Step 2: Unavailability of one sensor:**

$$U_1 = 1 - A_1 = 0.00200$$

**Step 3: Parallel system (both must fail for system failure):**

$$U_{\text{system}} = U_1 \times U_1 = (0.00200)^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$A_{\text{system}} = 1 - U_{\text{system}} = 1 - 0.000004 = 0.999996$$

So the parallel redundant system achieves **~99.9996% availability** — roughly **"five-nines plus"** quality, which is the target for safety-critical CPS subsystems.

**Conclusion:** Adding a parallel redundant sensor dramatically improves availability (from 99.80% to 99.9996%) with minimal cost — justifying redundancy in safety-critical CPS designs.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF:** MTTF is for **non-repairable** systems; MTBF is for **repairable** ones. Writing "MTBF = 1/λ" for a repairable system is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting the independence assumption:** When using series/parallel formulas, students often skip the line "assuming statistically independent failures" — the examiner expects this.
> 3. **Mixing up Series and Parallel:** If the question says "all components must work," the answer is **series**, NOT parallel. Read the wording carefully.
> 4. **Incorrect TMR formula:** The 2-out-of-3 voting system uses $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$, NOT a parallel formula. TMR tolerates only one fault.
> 5. **Skipping units:** Always state MTTF, MTBF, and λ with proper units (hours, per hour, etc.).
> 6. **Forgetting the "Useful Life" assumption:** The formula $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is valid only when failure rate is **constant** (the flat region of the bath-tub curve). State this assumption.
> 7. **Not drawing the block diagram:** In TMR/voting questions, a labelled block diagram carries **at least 1–2 marks**. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Reliability** is a *probabilistic metric*; **Fault Tolerance** is a *design property*.
- ✅ The **fault–error–failure** chain is the causal model tested repeatedly.
- ✅ **R(t) = e^(-λt)** is the foundational reliability function (constant failure rate).
- ✅ **MTTF = 1/λ** (non-repairable); **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** (repairable).
- ✅ **Availability A = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR)** — high MTBF + low MTTR.
- ✅ **Series reliability** = product of individual reliabilities.
- ✅ **Parallel reliability** = 1 − product of individual unreliabilities.
- ✅ **TMR (2-out-of-3)** reliability = **3R² − 2R³**.
- ✅ **FDIR** = Fault Detection, Isolation, Recovery — the structured CPS response.
- ✅ **Redundancy types:** Hardware, Software (N-version), Information, Time.
- ✅ **Watchdog timers, BIST, observer-based detection** are common FDIR tools.
- ✅ **FIT** = λ × 10⁹ — used in industrial component datasheets.
- ✅ **Bath-tub curve** has three regions: infant mortality, useful life, wear-out.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions**: independence, constant failure rate, perfect voter, etc.
- ✅ **Always include units** and **labelled diagrams** in KTU exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automotive Electronics, Autonomous Vehicles, Smart Grids, and Energy Management Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT303 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automotive Electronics, Autonomous Vehicles, Smart Grids, and Energy Management Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Automotive Electronics, Autonomous Vehicles, Smart Grids, and Energy Management Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overviews

### 1.1 Automotive Electronics

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Automotive Electronics* refers to the integrated network of embedded electronic systems, sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, and communication buses that monitor, control, and optimize the operation of mechanical subsystems in a modern vehicle. It is the **Cyber** layer that drives the **Physical** automotive machine, forming a quintessential Cyber-Physical System (CPS).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Human Nervous System:**
Think of a modern car as a human body. The **engine, brakes, and wheels** are the *muscles and limbs* (the physical system). The **Engine Control Unit (ECU), sensors (oxygen, speed, temperature), and the wiring harness** are the *nervous system* (the cyber system). Just as your brain receives signals from your eyes and skin and tells your hand to pull back from a hot surface, the ECU receives sensor data and commands actuators to adjust fuel injection, braking force, or steering angle — in real time, without human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A modern premium car contains **80–100 ECUs**, executes over **100 million lines of code**, and generates up to **25 GB of data per hour** from in-vehicle sensors. This staggering density of computation makes automotive electronics the single largest embedded-systems market in the world.

**Key Sub-Domains of Automotive Electronics:**

| Sub-Domain | Physical Function Controlled | Cyber Element |
|------------|------------------------------|---------------|
| **Powertrain Control** | Engine, transmission, hybrid drive | ECU + fuel-injection algorithms |
| **Body Electronics** | Lighting, wipers, climate | Body Control Module (BCM) |
| **Chassis Control** | ABS, ESC, suspension | Yaw-rate sensors, hydraulic modulators |
| **Infotainment** | Audio, navigation, display | HMI, OS (e.g., Android Automotive) |
| **ADAS** | Adaptive cruise, lane-keep, AEB | Radar, camera, LiDAR fusion |
| **Telematics** | Remote diagnostics, OTA updates | Cellular (4G/5G) modem |

---

### 1.2 Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** An *Autonomous Vehicle* is a Cyber-Physical System capable of sensing its environment, perceiving dynamic obstacles, planning a safe trajectory, and executing motion-control commands without human input. It is formally classified by the **SAE J3016** standard into **6 levels of driving automation** (Level 0 → Level 5).

**Conceptual Analogy — A Self-Driving Student Driver:**
Imagine teaching a teenager to drive. You give them **eyes** (cameras), **ears** (radar/ultrasonic), and a **GPS sense of direction** (LiDAR + map). They *perceive* the road, *think* about the rules, and *act* on the steering wheel and pedals. An autonomous vehicle does exactly this — but the "brain" is a stack of machine-learning models running on a high-performance computing platform, and the "eyes" operate 360° with millimeter precision at **77 GHz** (automotive radar) and **905 nm / 1550 nm** (LiDAR wavelengths).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SAE J3016 Levels of Driving Automation** are the most important taxonomy to memorize for KTU examinations:
> - **Level 0** – No Automation (driver does everything)
> - **Level 1** – Driver Assistance (e.g., adaptive cruise control)
> - **Level 2** – Partial Automation (e.g., Tesla Autopilot — hands-on)
> - **Level 3** – Conditional Automation (eyes-off in geo-fenced zones)
> - **Level 4** – High Automation (no driver in specific ODDs)
> - **Level 5** – Full Automation (no steering wheel, all conditions)

**The Three Pillars of AV Perception (Sensing Trinity):**
1. **Camera** — High-resolution RGB; excels at lane markings, traffic signs, and color-based classification. Range ≈ **150–250 m**.
2. **Radar** — Radio Detection And Ranging at **77 GHz** mmWave; immune to fog/rain. Range ≈ **250 m**.
3. **LiDAR** — Light Detection And Ranging; produces dense 3D point clouds. Range ≈ **200–300 m**, accuracy ±**2 cm**.

---

### 1.3 Smart Grids

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Smart Grid* is an electricity-supply network that uses **Information and Communication Technology (ICT)**, bidirectional sensors, advanced metering infrastructure (AMI), and automated control systems to monitor, predict, and actively manage energy generation, transmission, distribution, and consumption in real time.

**Conceptual Analogy — From Landline to Smartphone:**
A traditional power grid is like a **landline phone** — power flows one way (from giant central plants to homes), there is no feedback, and faults take hours to localize. A *smart grid* is the **smartphone equivalent** — it is bidirectional (consumers can also produce via rooftop solar — "prosumers"), it has real-time apps (smart meters, dynamic pricing), and it self-heals (automatic fault isolation and re-routing). The "operating system upgrade" is the overlay of digital intelligence on the analog wires.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A defining feature of the smart grid is the **Smart Meter**, which records consumption in **15-minute intervals** (vs. monthly mechanical meter reads) and supports **Time-of-Use (ToU) pricing**, demand-response events, and remote connect/disconnect — all governed by standards like **IEEE 2030.5** and **IEC 61850**.

---

### 1.4 Energy Management Systems (EMS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** An *Energy Management System* is a CPS-based supervisory control platform that uses real-time measurements, forecasting algorithms, and optimization techniques to balance energy supply, demand, storage, and pricing across a microgrid, building, vehicle, or industrial plant. In a CPS context, EMS is the *cyber brain* that orchestrates *physical* energy assets.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Thermostat of a City:**
Think of the EMS as the **thermostat of an entire city or a battery pack**. Just as a home thermostat measures room temperature (sensor), compares it to your setpoint (logic), and switches the AC on/off (actuator), an EMS measures load demand and renewable generation (sensors), runs optimization (logic), and dispatches generators, charges/discharges batteries, or sheds non-critical loads (actuation). The math is harder, but the principle is identical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, remember the **two classical EMS objectives**:
> 1. **Economic Dispatch** – minimize total generation cost $\sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i(P_i)$ subject to power-balance and capacity constraints.
> 2. **Unit Commitment** – schedule ON/OFF status of generators over a horizon (e.g., 24 h) to minimize start-up + fuel cost while meeting forecasted demand and reserve requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Automotive Electronics — Operational Architecture

Modern automotive electronics is organized as a **hierarchical, bus-connected network of ECUs**. The de-facto communication backbone is the **Controller Area Network (CAN) bus**, standardized as **ISO 11898**.

### 2.1.1 The CAN Bus Frame (Classical CAN, 11-bit ID)

A CAN data frame is the atomic unit of communication between ECUs. Its bit-level structure is essential KTU knowledge:

| Field | Length | Function |
|-------|--------|----------|
| SOF | **1 bit** | Start of Frame (dominant 0) |
| Arbitration ID | **11 bits** | Message priority (lower ID = higher priority) |
| RTR | **1 bit** | Remote Transmission Request |
| IDE | **1 bit** | Identifier Extension (0 = 11-bit) |
| r0 | **1 bit** | Reserved |
| DLC | **4 bits** | Data Length Code (0–8 bytes) |
| Data | **0–64 bits** | Payload |
| CRC | **15 bits** | Cyclic Redundancy Check |
| ACK | **2 bits** | Acknowledgement slot |
| EOF | **7 bits** | End of Frame (recessive 1s) |

**Bit Rate vs. Bus Length (ISO 11898):**

| Bit Rate | Maximum Bus Length |
|----------|-------------------|
| **1 Mbit/s** | **25 m** |
| **500 kbit/s** | **100 m** |
| **250 kbit/s** | **250 m** |
| **125 kbit/s** | **500 m** |

### 2.1.2 Voltage Drop in Automotive Wiring (KTU-favorite)

The supply rail in a 12 V automotive system is often a long copper harness. The voltage drop must be < **10 %** of nominal (per ISO 8820):

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = I_{\text{load}} \times R_{\text{wire}} = I_{\text{load}} \times \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}
$$

where $\rho$ = **1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** (copper resistivity), $L$ = round-trip length (m), $A$ = cross-section (m²), and $I_{\text{load}}$ in amperes.

### 2.1.3 ECU Real-Time Scheduling

AUTOSAR OS (the dominant automotive RTOS standard) uses a **fixed-priority preemptive scheduler** with priorities 0–31 (higher number = higher priority). The worst-case response time of task $\tau_i$ under Rate Monotonic priority assignment is bounded by:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

where $C_i$ = worst-case execution time, $T_j$ = period, and $hp(i)$ = set of higher-priority tasks. A task set is **schedulable** iff $R_i \leq D_i$ (deadline) for all $i$.

---

## 2.2 Autonomous Vehicles — Perception, Planning & Control Stack

The AV software stack is conventionally divided into **4 functional layers**:

1. **Sensing** — Raw data acquisition (camera, radar, LiDAR, IMU, GNSS, wheel odometry).
2. **Perception** — Object detection, semantic segmentation, SLAM.
3. **Planning** — Global route (A\*/Dijkstra), behavioral decision (FSM), local trajectory (polynomial or optimization-based).
4. **Control** — Longitudinal (PID/Model Predictive Control on throttle & brake) + Lateral (steering angle via Stanley or MPC).

### 2.2.1 Sensor Fusion — The Kalman Filter

The most commonly tested fusion equation in KTU is the discrete-time **Kalman Filter** update step:

$$
\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \left( z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} \right)
$$

$$
K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^T \left( H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R \right)^{-1}
$$

$$
P_{k \vert k} = \left( I - K_k H \right) P_{k \vert k-1}
$$

where $\hat{x}_{k \vert k}$ = posterior state estimate, $z_k$ = measurement, $K_k$ = **Kalman Gain**, $P$ = state covariance, $H$ = observation matrix, $R$ = measurement-noise covariance.

> [!NOTE]
> The Kalman Gain $K_k$ is the **trust ratio** — it weighs the prediction against the measurement. When $R$ is large (noisy sensor), $K_k \to 0$ (trust prediction). When $P$ is large (uncertain prediction), $K_k$ grows (trust measurement).

### 2.2.2 PID Longitudinal Controller (Cruise Control)

The classic **Proportional-Integral-Derivative** controller for tracking a desired speed $v_{\text{ref}}$:

$$
u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where $e(t) = v_{\text{ref}}(t) - v(t)$ is the speed error, and $u(t)$ maps to throttle angle (or brake pressure if negative).

### 2.2.3 Latency Budget for an AV (KTU numerical)

A real-time AV must complete the **sense → plan → act** loop within a hard deadline. The end-to-end latency $L_{\text{total}}$ is:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{perceive}} + L_{\text{plan}} + L_{\text{control}} + L_{\text{actuate}}
$$

Typical target: **L_total ≤ 100 ms** for highway L3 autonomy. At **v = 120 km/h** (≈ 33.33 m/s), the car moves only **3.33 m** in this window — critical for collision avoidance.

---

## 2.3 Smart Grids — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Power-Flow in a Distribution Feeder

The complex power flowing through a line of impedance $Z = R + jX$ carrying current $I$ is:

$$
S = V \cdot I^{*} = P + jQ
$$

Real (active) and reactive power losses in the line:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = I^2 R = \frac{P^2 + Q^2}{V^2} R
$$

$$
Q_{\text{loss}} = I^2 X = \frac{P^2 + Q^2}{V^2} X
$$

### 2.3.2 Economic Dispatch (Lambda-Iteration Method)

For $N$ generators with quadratic cost curves $C_i(P_i) = a_i + b_i P_i + c_i P_i^2$, the system marginal cost $\lambda$ (Lagrange multiplier) is found by equating the incremental cost of every online unit:

$$
\frac{dC_i}{dP_i} = 2 c_i P_i + b_i = \lambda \quad \text{for all } i \text{ online}
$$

Subject to the power-balance equation:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i = P_D
$$

where $P_D$ is total demand (neglecting losses for the lossless case). With losses, the **coordination equation** becomes:

$$
\lambda = \frac{dC_i}{dP_i} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{\partial P_L}{\partial P_i}}
$$

where $\frac{\partial P_L}{\partial P_i}$ is the **incremental transmission loss** (B-matrix / Kron's loss formula).

### 2.3.3 Demand Response & Time-of-Use (ToU) Pricing

The customer's effective bill under ToU is:

$$
\text{Bill} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} p_t \cdot E_t
$$

where $p_t$ is the time-varying price (e.g., ₹/kWh) and $E_t$ is energy consumed in slot $t$. The **price elasticity of demand** is:

$$
\varepsilon_{t,s} = \frac{\partial D_t / D_t}{\partial p_s / p_s}
$$

A self-elasticity $\varepsilon_{t,t} < 0$ (typical value **−0.1 to −0.3**) means demand drops when price rises.

### 2.3.4 Renewable Integration — Capacity Credit

The **Capacity Credit (CC)** of a renewable plant is:

$$
CC = \frac{\text{Equivalent Firm Capacity of RE plant}}{\text{Rated Capacity of RE plant}} \times 100\%
$$

For solar PV in tropical Kerala, typical CC ≈ **20–30 %**; for wind, ≈ **10–25 %** depending on site.

---

## 2.4 Energy Management Systems — Hierarchical Architecture

A modern EMS is typically a **3-layer hierarchy** (per IEEE Standard 2030 & IEC 61970/61968):

| Layer | Time Horizon | Function |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| **Unit/Device Level** | ms – s | Local converters, MPPT, inverter control |
| **Plant/Microgrid Level** | s – min | Secondary control, SoC balancing, islanding |
| **System/Market Level** | min – h | Economic dispatch, unit commitment, market clearing |

### 2.4.1 Battery State of Charge (SoC) — Coulomb Counting

$$
SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \frac{1}{C_{\text{nom}}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{\text{bat}}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

where $C_{\text{nom}}$ is the nominal capacity (A·h) and $I_{\text{bat}}$ is positive for discharge.

### 2.4.2 SoC Balancing in a Battery Pack

In an EV pack with $N$ series cells of individual SoC $SoC_i$, the **standard deviation** quantifies imbalance:

$$
\sigma_{SoC} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( SoC_i - \overline{SoC} \right)^2}
$$

Active balancing circuits target $\sigma_{SoC} \leq 2\%$ to maximize pack lifetime.

### 2.4.3 Microgrid Power Balance

For a microgrid with PV, wind, battery, diesel genset, and load:

$$
P_{PV}(t) + P_{wind}(t) + P_{bat}(t) + P_{gen}(t) = P_{load}(t) + P_{\text{loss}}(t) \pm P_{\text{grid}}(t)
$$

A positive $P_{bat}$ means discharging; negative means charging.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the equations in this table — they account for the majority of numericals asked in KTU ESE for Module 4.

| # | Concept | Equation | Meaning of Each Term |
|---|---------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | CAN bus length (max) | $L_{\max} \approx 25 \cdot \frac{1\,\text{Mbit/s}}{f_{\text{bit}}}$ | $f_{\text{bit}}$ in Mbit/s |
| 2 | Wire voltage drop | $V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot \rho L / A$ | $I$ in A, $L$ in m, $A$ in m² |
| 3 | Rate-monotonic response | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ | $C_i$ WCET, $T_j$ period |
| 4 | Kalman update | $\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k(z_k - H\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$ | $K_k$ = Kalman Gain |
| 5 | Kalman gain | $K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$ | $R$ = sensor noise cov. |
| 6 | PID control | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | $e$ = tracking error |
| 7 | Real-time distance | $d = v \cdot L_{\text{total}}$ | $v$ in m/s, $L$ in s |
| 8 | Line power loss | $P_{\text{loss}} = (P^2 + Q^2) R / V^2$ | $P, Q$ in pu or W/VAR |
| 9 | Economic dispatch | $dC_i/dP_i = 2c_i P_i + b_i = \lambda$ | $C_i$ = fuel cost |
| 10 | Coordination eq. (with loss) | $\lambda = IC_i \cdot (1 - \partial P_L / \partial P_i)^{-1}$ | $IC_i$ = incremental cost |
| 11 | Battery SoC | $SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \frac{1}{C_{\text{nom}}} \int I\,dt$ | $C_{\text{nom}}$ in A·h |
| 12 | SoC imbalance | $\sigma_{SoC} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum (SoC_i - \overline{SoC})^2}$ | $\sigma \leq 2\%$ target |
| 13 | Microgrid balance | $\sum P_{\text{gen}} = \sum P_{\text{load}} + P_{\text{loss}}$ | All terms at time $t$ |
| 14 | Price elasticity | $\varepsilon = (\Delta D / D) / (\Delta p / p)$ | $\varepsilon < 0$ always |
| 15 | Capacity credit | $CC = \frac{P_{\text{firm,equiv}}}{P_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless (%) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests Module 4 with **numericals on economic dispatch, CAN bus timing, battery SoC, and PID control**. The following five worked problems cover all major patterns.

---

## Derivation 3.1 — CAN Bus Arbitration & Bit Time

A CAN controller samples the bus once per **Time Quantum (TQ)**. The nominal bit time has 4 segments:

$$
t_{\text{bit}} = t_{\text{Sync}} + t_{\text{Prop}} + t_{\text{Phase1}} + t_{\text{Phase2}}
$$

For a **500 kbit/s** bus with clock $f_{\text{clk}} = 20\,\text{MHz}$ and Baud-Rate Prescaler (BRP) = 4:

$$
TQ = \frac{BRP}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{4}{20 \times 10^6} = 200\,\text{ns}
$$

Choose segment lengths (in TQ): Sync = 1, Prop = 6, Phase1 = 7, Phase2 = 7, SJW = 1. Total TQ per bit:

$$
N_{\text{TQ}} = 1 + 6 + 7 + 7 = 21\,\text{TQ}
$$

Bit time:

$$
t_{\text{bit}} = 21 \times 200\,\text{ns} = 4200\,\text{ns} = 4.2\,\mu s
$$

Actual bit rate:

$$
f_{\text{bit,actual}} = \frac{1}{4.2\,\mu s} \approx 238\,\text{kbit/s}
$$

**Engineering insight:** Bit time tuning must satisfy $t_{\text{Prop}} \geq 2 \cdot t_{\text{prop,physical}}$ (round-trip on the bus) so that a dominant bit anywhere on the bus propagates back to the transmitter before the sample point. For a **25 m** bus with propagation velocity $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, the one-way delay is **125 ns**, so round-trip ≈ **250 ns**, comfortably within the **6 TQ × 200 ns = 1200 ns** propagation segment.

---

## Derivation 3.2 — Wire Gauge Selection for an Automotive Headlamp

A headlamp draws **I = 55 W / 13.5 V ≈ 4.07 A** from the battery via a **L = 4 m** round-trip harness. Maximum allowable voltage drop = **10 % × 13.5 V = 1.35 V**.

Starting from $V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot \rho L / A$, solve for $A$:

$$
A = \frac{I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{V_{\text{drop}}} = \frac{4.07 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 4}{1.35}
$$

Step 1 — multiply numerator:

$$
4.07 \times 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 4 = 28.06 \times 10^{-8} = 2.806 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^3
$$

Step 2 — divide by $V_{\text{drop}}$:

$$
A = \frac{2.806 \times 10^{-7}}{1.35} = 2.079 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^2
$$

Step 3 — convert to mm²:

$$
A = 2.079 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{6} = 0.208\,\text{mm}^2
$$

Step 4 — select the **next standard AWG size above 0.208 mm²**. The nearest automotive-grade is **0.5 mm² (≈ 20 AWG)**, which keeps voltage drop comfortably below the limit.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *textbook KTU-style numerical*. Always end with a **standard size selection** comment, not just the theoretical area.

---

## Derivation 3.3 — Economic Dispatch with 3 Generators (Lambda Iteration)

Three generators supply a total demand of $P_D = 850$ MW. Cost functions (₹/h):

$$
C_1 = 450 + 7.0 P_1 + 0.0080 P_1^2
$$

$$
C_2 = 600 + 7.5 P_2 + 0.0090 P_2^2
$$

$$
C_3 = 350 + 8.5 P_3 + 0.0070 P_3^2
$$

Generator limits: $150 \le P_1 \le 600$, $100 \le P_2 \le 400$, $50 \le P_3 \le 300$ (all in MW).

**Step 1 — Incremental cost (IC) of each unit:**

$$
IC_1 = \frac{dC_1}{dP_1} = 7.0 + 0.0160 P_1
$$

$$
IC_2 = \frac{dC_2}{dP_2} = 7.5 + 0.0180 P_2
$$

$$
IC_3 = \frac{dC_3}{dP_3} = 8.5 + 0.0140 P_3
$$

**Step 2 — Equate ICs (equal-lambda rule):**

$$
7.0 + 0.0160 P_1 = 7.5 + 0.0180 P_2 = 8.5 + 0.0140 P_3 = \lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Express each $P_i$ in terms of $\lambda$:**

$$
P_1 = \frac{\lambda - 7.0}{0.0160} = 62.5 \lambda - 437.5
$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{\lambda - 7.5}{0.0180} = 55.556 \lambda - 416.667
$$

$$
P_3 = \frac{\lambda - 8.5}{0.0140} = 71.429 \lambda - 607.143
$$

**Step 4 — Apply power balance $\sum P_i = 850$ MW:**

$$
(62.5 + 55.556 + 71.429)\lambda - (437.5 + 416.667 + 607.143) = 850
$$

$$
189.485 \lambda - 1461.31 = 850
$$

$$
189.485 \lambda = 2311.31 \Rightarrow \lambda = 12.20\;\text{₹/MWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute individual generations:**

$$
P_1 = 62.5 \times 12.20 - 437.5 = 762.5 - 437.5 = 325\,\text{MW}
$$

$$
P_2 = 55.556 \times 12.20 - 416.667 = 677.78 - 416.67 = 261.11\,\text{MW}
$$

$$
P_3 = 71.429 \times 12.20 - 607.143 = 871.43 - 607.14 = 264.29\,\text{MW}
$$

**Step 6 — Check constraints:** All lie within limits ✓. **Check balance:** $325 + 261.11 + 264.29 = 850.40 \approx 850$ MW ✓ (rounding).

**Step 7 — Total generation cost:**

$$
C_1 = 450 + 7.0 \times 325 + 0.0080 \times 325^2 = 450 + 2275 + 845 = 3570\;\text{₹/h}
$$

$$
C_2 = 600 + 7.5 \times 261.11 + 0.0090 \times 261.11^2 = 600 + 1958.3 + 613.7 = 3172\;\text{₹/h}
$$

$$
C_3 = 350 + 8.5 \times 264.29 + 0.0070 \times 264.29^2 = 350 + 2246.5 + 488.9 = 3085.4\;\text{₹/h}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 3570 + 3172 + 3085.4 = 9827.4\;\text{₹/h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU answer key typically awards **7 marks** for arriving at $\lambda$ and the $P_i$ values, and the remaining **7 marks** for cost computation, constraint verification, and a final summary.

---

## Derivation 3.4 — Battery SoC over a Drive Cycle

A small EV has a **5 kWh** lithium-ion pack ($C_{\text{nom}} = 5{,}000\,\text{Wh}$). Initial SoC = **90 %**. The current profile over 4 successive 15-minute intervals is $I = [50, 70, 40, 80]$ A (discharge = positive).

**Step 1 — Convert intervals to hours:** $\Delta t = 15\,\text{min} = 0.25\,\text{h}$ each.

**Step 2 — Coulomb-counted charge removed (in A·h) per interval:**

$$
\Delta Q_i = I_i \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\Delta Q_1 = 50 \times 0.25 = 12.5\,\text{A·h}
$$

$$
\Delta Q_2 = 70 \times 0.25 = 17.5\,\text{A·h}
$$

$$
\Delta Q_3 = 40 \times 0.25 = 10.0\,\text{A·h}
$$

$$
\Delta Q_4 = 80 \times 0.25 = 20.0\,\text{A·h}
$$

**Step 3 — Total charge removed:** $\Delta Q_{\text{tot}} = 12.5 + 17.5 + 10 + 20 = 60\,\text{A·h}$.

**Step 4 — SoC change (assuming $C_{\text{nom}} = 5{,}000\,\text{Wh} / 12\,\text{V} \approx 416.7\,\text{A·h}$ for a 12 V reference, but for percentage we use the energy view):**

If we work in **Wh**, total energy removed = $V_{\text{nom}} \times \Delta Q_{\text{tot}} = 12 \times 60 = 720\,\text{Wh} = 0.72\,\text{kWh}$ (for 12 V; for a 400 V pack scale accordingly).

As a fraction of pack:

$$
\Delta SoC = \frac{0.72}{5.0} = 0.144 = 14.4\%
$$

**Step 5 — Final SoC:** $SoC_{\text{final}} = 90\% - 14.4\% = 75.6\%$.

**Verification using the integral form:**

$$
SoC_{\text{final}} = SoC_{\text{initial}} - \frac{1}{C_{\text{nom,Wh}}} \int_0^{1\,\text{h}} V \cdot I(t)\, dt
$$

Plugging $V = 12$ V (approximation), $C_{\text{nom,Wh}} = 5000$:

$$
SoC_{\text{final}} = 0.9 - \frac{12 \times 60}{5000} = 0.9 - 0.144 = 0.756 = 75.6\%
$$

Confirmed.

---

## Derivation 3.5 — PID Cruise Control Stability Margin

A vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg is to maintain $v_{\text{ref}} = 100$ km/h on a flat road. The plant (throttle → speed) is approximately first-order:

$$
G(s) = \frac{V(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}, \quad K = 2.5,\; \tau = 1.2\,\text{s}
$$

A **PI** controller ($K_p$, $K_i$) is used (derivative term neglected for longitudinal control to avoid noise amplification):

$$
C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}
$$

**Open-loop transfer function:**

$$
L(s) = C(s) G(s) = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1)}
$$

**For a critically-damped step response**, choose closed-loop damping ratio $\zeta = 1$ and natural frequency $\omega_n = 1.0$ rad/s. The desired closed-loop characteristic polynomial:

$$
s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = s^2 + 2 s + 1
$$

Closed-loop TF:

$$
T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1) + K(K_p s + K_i)}
$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$
s(\tau s + 1) + K K_p s + K K_i = \tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i
$$

Matching with $\omega_n^2 s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + 1$ (after dividing by $\tau$):

$$
s^2 + \frac{1 + K K_p}{\tau} s + \frac{K K_i}{\tau}
$$

So:

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{K K_i}{\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{\omega_n^2 \tau}{K} = \frac{1 \times 1.2}{2.5} = 0.48
$$

$$
2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{1 + K K_p}{\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2(1)(1) = \frac{1 + 2.5 K_p}{1.2}
$$

$$
2.4 = 1 + 2.5 K_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = \frac{1.4}{2.5} = 0.56
$$

**Tuning Summary:** $K_p = 0.56$, $K_i = 0.48$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU does not require advanced control theory — you only need to recognize that PID is the workhorse of automotive speed/position control and be able to set up the basic closed-loop characteristic equation.

```mermaid
    D1[Smart Meter Home A]:::cust
    D2[Smart Meter Home B]:::cust
    D3[Smart Meter Industry]:::cust
    D4[EV Charging Station]:::cust
    D5[Home Energy Management]:::cust

    C1[SCADA Energy Management System]:::ctrl
    C2[Distribution Management System]:::ctrl
    C3[Demand Response Server]:::ctrl
    C4[AMI Head End]:::ctrl
    C5[Outage Management System]:::ctrl
    C6[Market Operator PX]:::ctrl
    C7[Weather Forecast Service]:::ctrl

    G1 --> T1
    G2 --> T1
    G3 --> T1
    G4 --> T2
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> D1
    T5 --> D2
    T5 --> D3
    T5 --> D4
    G5 --> T5
    G6 --> T4

    C1 --- T1
    C1 --- T2
    C2 --- T3
    C2 --- T4
    C2 --- T5
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
    C3 --> D5
    C5 --- T5
    C6 --- C1
    C7 --> C1
    C7 --> C3
```

**Reading the diagram:** Generation (green) → transmission (blue) → distribution (orange) → customers (pink) is the *physical* power path. The *cyber* overlay is the vertical control column (purple) that talks to every layer — this is the essence of a smart-grid CPS.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Energy Management System (3-Layer)

**Reading the diagram:** Each layer's *setpoints* (arrows down) are honored by the layer below within its faster time horizon. The flow forms a closed cyber-physical feedback loop with time-scale separation (1:10:1000 rule — millisecond, second, hour).

---

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix — SAE Levels of Automation

| Level | Name | Steering | Throttle Brake | Eyes | Responsibility |
|-------|------|----------|----------------|------|----------------|
| 0 | No Automation | Driver | Driver | On | Driver |
| 1 | Driver Assistance | Driver or System | Driver or System | On | Driver |
| 2 | Partial Automation | System | System | On | Driver |
| 3 | Conditional Automation | System | System | Off in zone | Shared |
| 4 | High Automation | System | System | Off | System (in ODD) |
| 5 | Full Automation | System | System | Off | System (all) |

> [!NOTE]
> "ODD" = **Operational Design Domain** (geofence, weather, speed range). L4 is *only* L4 inside its ODD — outside, it may request a handover or safely stop.

---
